Students are expected to consult the program section and peruse through the pre-reading section prior to coming to the lecture, labs and tutorials. Students bring conceptual understandings of pre-reading material to classes, tutorials and labs to enable deeper understanding through discussions and feedback.The lectures, tutorials and labs will build on the pre-readings provided to the students. All the pre-reading will be made available on Canvas.During Week 1, students are expected to view the videos made available by the subject coordinator on Canvas. Subsequently, in order to provide the students with feedback on their understanding of the content covered during the preparation, they will be required to complete an online quiz. Feedback provided is automated and numerical.
have_subject.description: This subject introduces you to cloud computing, covering cloud architecture, IaaS, PaaS, SaaS, virtualisation, and multi-tenancy. You'll engage in group work to produce a research report critically analysing a cloud computing aspect, followed by developing cloud-based software engineering skills on an existing platform. The second assignment involves group collaboration to create a cloud-based software application.

items.details: 42904 Cloud Computing and Software as a Service

requisite.rule: Requisite rule: ((1 OR 1a OR 1b OR 1c OR 1d OR 1e))

have_subject.name: Programming on the Internet
learning_outcomes: Identify concepts pertaining to the Internet and World Wide web such as TCP/IP, protocols, domain names, IP addresses and n-tier architecture. (D.1)
Apply principles of internet security to websites. (D.1)
Apply principles of website design to improve efficiency using industry used web development languages. (D.1)
Apply client-side and server-side scripting to manage a range of simple web server interactions. (D.1)
Relate web development frameworks to effectively manage websites. (D.1)
have_subject.learning_and_teaching_activities: Subject presentation includes lectures (1 hour/week), laboratory sessions (2 hours/week), and research and developmentwork for the assignments. Lectures will present the theoretical aspects of Internet programming and web development, including client-side and server side scripting for webpage development. The laboratory sessions are conducted in the lab and require substantial preparation from the students. They will focus on hands-on experience in internet programming and web development. Practical assignments can be performed anywhere, the labs will provide the software necessary to complete these assignments.
have_subject.description: This subject introduces web and internet-based programming, covering the internet's evolution and its technical underpinnings, as well as data presentation techniques. You'll focus on PHP, MySQL, JavaScript, AJAX, XML, and Object-Oriented PHP, providing a solid understanding of web functionality, website construction, and advanced scripting for the web.

requisite.rule: Requisite rule: (1 AND (2 OR 2a OR 2b OR 2c OR 2d OR 2e))

99200.name: Intercultural Communication
learning_outcomes: Critically engage with intercultural communication theories and frameworks and apply these theories to analyse diverse cultural situations and spaces
Reflect on own perceptions of intercultural spaces and encounters and intercultural skills development for professional and everyday contexts
Design and conduct independent, ethical, small scale research about aspects of interculturality
Recognise the ongoing impact of colonialism on and positive contributions of Indigenous cultures in intercultural spaces
Communicate effectively and appropriately in written and oral academic English
99200.learning_and_teaching_activities: Classes will incorporate a range of teaching and learning strategies including presentations, videos, online learning materials, reflective surveys, discussion of readings, international case studies and group activities. These will be complemented by independent student research, readings and participation in online discussion forums.Feedback on assessment tasks will be provided in class, through quizzes and via GradeBook in Canvas.
99200.description: This subject introduces you the core concepts and debates in the interdisciplinary field of intercultural communication. You'll be prepared for the interpersonal and dynamic nature of working in increasingly globalised workplaces and intercultural environments. You'll use a problem-based approach to critically examine numerous frameworks and theories that allow you to interpret intercultural encounters in praxis-based scenarios. You'll apply specific intercultural communication strategies and approaches for their relevance to industry, communities, workplaces and society more broadly.

43029.name: Introduction to Humanitarian Engineering
learning_outcomes: Apply the values and principles of humanitarian engineering in the evaluation of a case study, concept, or approach. (B.1)
Communicate research about humanitarian engineering to inform a multi-disciplinary audience. (E.1)
Reflect on the value of humanitarian engineering and, in particular, on perspectives on socio-technical thinking and co-design. (F.1)
43029.learning_and_teaching_activities: Students have the valuable opportunity to engage in weekly three-hour interactive workshops.Complementing workshops are interactive online content for students to work through out-of-class and in preparation for in-class collaborative activities.Analog and digital collaboration tools in Canvas support all learning.Engagement in this interactive learning approach is critical to contributing to successful progress and can be applied to developing professional skills and knowledge to further studies and/or the professional workplace.Guest speakers with industry experience bring their specialist expertise and share these with classes to inform and enrich classroom discussion.Such design of workshops provide weekly formative feedback that is pertinent to summative assessment tasks.
43029.description: Humanitarian engineering is the application of engineering skills and approaches to enable basic needs and contribute to social justice, with contexts ranging from community and technology development to emergency disaster response, both domestically and internationally. This subject equips you with skills transferable to all engineering fields, including stakeholder engagement, cross-cultural work, human-centred design, and environmental stewardship.You'll build on prior experiences from the Engineers without Borders Challenge, learning about international development, effective humanitarian engineering practices, and key agencies in the field. The subject encourages personal reflection on the significance of humanitarian engineering for your development and allows you to delve into a topic of personal interest (such as a particular case study, approach, context, or how engineering relates to a particular Sustainable Development Goal) in more detail.Interactive three-hour weekly workshops, complemented by online content, are central to the learning process, fostering collaboration and professional skill development. Canvas tools support both analog and digital collaboration, enhancing your learning experience. Industry guest speakers contribute real-world insights, and the workshop format provides ongoing formative feedback, crucial for your assessments and professional growth.

requisite.rule: Requisite rule: (((1 OR 1a OR 1b OR 1c OR 1d OR 1e)) OR ((2 OR 2a OR 2b OR 2c OR 2d OR 2e) AND (3 OR 3a)))

48210.name: Interrogating Technology: Sustainability, Environment and Social Change
learning_outcomes: Use appropriate research tools to interpret philosophical, sociological and political perspectives, and the perspectives of different stakeholders, on emerging technologies. (B.1)
Apply relevant theoretical frameworks to interrogate and represent different aspects of the relationships between technology, society, and the environment. (B.1)
Propose, and advocate for, policy initiatives regarding an emergent technology or technological issue to improve outcomes for a designated stakeholder. (B.1)
48210.learning_and_teaching_activities: In this subject you will develop skills to articulate comprehensive, convincing and informed judgements about technology based on credible sources.Students need to engage in pre-class tasks on Canvas including watching videos, guided reading, and responding to set questions. During class students will use their work for peer review/discussions/sprint presentations and tutor feedback. Those classes allow for groups of students to collaborate in preparation for assignments. Feedback for assignments are provided to students in a number of ways including Canvas one-to-one feedback, group feedback, and peer reviews. Guest speakers will share their experiences with - and perspectives on - subject themes.Groups investigate stakeholders that could be affected by a particular technology and construct a convincing case for consultation. Individually, students develop a policy position paper on behalf of one of those stakeholders. This hinges on good communication and research skills which will take the form of reporting, presenting, and debating. Active participation, peer interaction and feedback are critical for achieving the subject learning outcomes.A program of readings and other resources scaffold the learning required for critical perspectives to interrogate technologies.
48210.description: This subject equips you with the skills to examine the relationship between engineering and society through philosophical, sociological, and political lenses. You'll be introduced to theoretical frameworks and research tools to investigate stakeholder perspectives on complex issues and their influence on policy. Through case studies of new technologies and engineering projects, you'll apply this learning directly to your engineering context, gaining a deeper understanding of the multifaceted interactions at play.

items.details: 48250 Economics and Finance for Engineering Projects

95002.name: TD: Reframing, remixing, reimagining society
learning_outcomes: Integrate different perspectives to analyse complex challenges.
Identify significant issues and emerging opportunities to create positive change in real-world contexts.
Apply transdisciplinary collaboration and change-making methods in teams to generate responses to complex challenges.
Develop a cohesive rationale for proposals and communicate ideas persuasively to diverse audiences.
Synthesise your learning to generate insights about transdisciplinarity and your own role in fostering positive social change.
95002.learning_and_teaching_activities: Students will attend plenary sessions, interact with online modules and unpack learnings in tutorial workshops to gain understanding of relevant concepts and frameworks, and engage in dialogue with academics, key stakeholders, and peers. As students experiment with creative practices by drawing on cases and approaches from across different disciplines, they will have ongoing opportunities for formative feedback by peers and academic staff.
95002.description: In this subject, students interrogate their personal and disciplinary mental models, worldviews, biases and assumptions to question the current framing of complex societal problems by examining a real-world challenge posed by UNICEF. Students explore a range of disciplinary and professional perspectives and collaborate in multidisciplinary teams to creatively remix these ideas. Together, they develop innovative approaches aimed at ensuring inter-generational justice in the face of climate change. A range of creative methods are used to cultivate a culture of curiosity, experimentation, and inventiveness, while also identifying and probing unknowns. Through generating novel responses, students test their own emerging disciplinary or professional expertise; they learn to collaborate across differences, generate insights well beyond their discipline, and use these insights to create new avenues for action in the real world.

requisite.rule: Requisite rule: ((1 OR 1a OR 1b OR 1c OR 1d OR 1e OR 1f OR 1g))

95003.name: TD: Sustainability in an interconnected world
learning_outcomes: Draw from different perspectives to investigate the assumptions inherent in framing complex problem situations
Examine and evaluate methods and practices for engaging with complex challenges from across a range of disciplines and professional domains
Articulate questions and proposals for thinking differently about global challenges, and identify new avenues for action
Communicate new understandings of complex problem situations persuasively to diverse audiences
95003.learning_and_teaching_activities: This subject is offered in a blended mode. Students engage with online modules and materials at their own pace to gain an understanding of relevant concepts and frameworks. This learning is then applied to real-world challenges in collaborative, immersive and experiential tutorials. As students experiment with approaches for investigating complex problem situations, they have ongoing opportunities for formative feedback from peers and academic staff.
95003.description: This subject introduces students to ways of thinking differently about the ‘wicked’ problems we face when tackling big issues around sustainability. Students draw on a range of approaches from across our disciplines and professional domains to explore the complex social and environmental context in which every problem is situated and networked with other problems. Striving towards regenerative sustainable futures, these novel ways of thinking enable students to move away from conventional problem-solving approaches to discover new avenues for action. By working in multidisciplinary teams on complex industry or community challenges, students develop their relational and analytic skills. Students devise their own questions based on insights they’ve uncovered, and communicate their proposals for addressing current or emerging global challenges to a range of diverse audiences.

items.details: 95011 TD: Sustainability in an interconnected world +

95004.name: TD: Technologies reimagined in a complex world
learning_outcomes: Investigate a range of emerging ubiquitous technologies and analyse their impact on careers, organisations and communities
Apply a selection of disciplinary and transdisciplinary research methods to investigate and map the complex impacts of an emerging technology in human societies

Generate reimagined technological responses to real-world challenges by integrating creative and analytic approaches from a range of disciplines with a focus on creating value through sustainable and entrepreneurial innovation
Develop a cohesive rationale for proposals and communicate these proposals/ideas to diverse audiences
Reflect on issues of ethics, values, sustainability and inclusivity in relation to technology and society
95004.learning_and_teaching_activities: This subject is offered in a blended mode with interactive plenaries, online modules and lectures, accompanied by interactive and collaborative tutorials, with regular opportunities for informal feedback from peers and academic staff.Students will explore technology futures reimagined in a complex world from multiple disciplinary and intergenerational perspectives, by conducting their own research and engaging with a range of academic and industry/professional perspectives. Students will translate their learnings into practice by addressing a complex real-world challenge posed by an industry partner. As students collaborate in multidisciplinary teams and experiment with different disciplinary approaches to reimagine technologies, they have the opportunity to critically reflect on technology futures in their own disciplines and consider the implications to their future study and profession.
95004.description: This subject explores the profound impact of ubiquitous technologies on our behaviours, aspirations, and daily lives. Students develop skills in complex systems thinking, enabling them to navigate real-world challenges around technologies of everyday use and reimagine these technologies with proposals promoting inclusivity and sustainability. Students also engage with industry or community partners for practical experience, working in multidisciplinary teams on hands-on projects and collaborative workshops. This subject prepares students for innovation, sustainability, or community development careers, addressing complex challenges and driving systemic change. This subject also helps students explore the transformative power of technology and steer systems toward a more inclusive future.

items.details: 95012 TD: Technologies reimagined in a complex world +

95005.name: TD: Shaping technologies that shape us
learning_outcomes: Combine disciplinary perspectives, evidence, and diverse viewpoints to holistically analyse the factors influencing the development of novel technologies.
Demonstrate capabilities in divergent and convergent thinking techniques, and transdisciplinary methods, across the stages of an innovation project.
Identify actionable, real-world insights about technological trends to guide the future development of technology.
Develop reflexive connection with their own evolving perspectives and contributions as a productive member of a multidisciplinary cohort and team.
Communicate project outcomes creatively, confidently, and convincingly to internal and external project stakeholders, using a range of modes and media.
95005.learning_and_teaching_activities: Experiential problem-based learning; interdisciplinary collaboration; blended learning (technology-mediated instruction); authentic assessment.By collaborating as a cohort and in small group settings, students will examine complex technological trends through the lens of their emerging disciplinary positions and diverse stakeholder perspectives. Interactive Canvas modules will enable students to engage with a range of provocative and engaging multi-media learning resources, including podcasts, videos, selected peer-reviewed articles, and industry reports. These resources will support authentic, practice-based learning to develop intellectual agility, creative skills, and collaboration capability that students can use for the rest of their professional lives. Throughout the subject, students will have ongoing opportunities for formative feedback by peers and academic staff, incrementally building skills they can apply in real-life settings (especially with a view to increasing employability).
95005.description: This subject equips you with critical thinking, creative, and collaborative skills to address complex real-world challenges, enhancing your professional capabilities. In response to an industry partner brief from Microsoft, you will explore the wide-ranging impacts, opportunities, and profound questions posed by the exponential growth of Artificial Intelligence (AI) technologies, applying your disciplinary lens while learning to work with other perspectives. By engaging with a range of thought provoking multi-media learning resources, highly interactive classroom activities, and a range of practical tasks, you will develop key insights into the challenge space and deliver a distinctive agenda for the sustainable development of these transformative technologies.

items.details: 95013 TD: Shaping technologies that shape us +

95006.name: TD: Envisioning futures worth wanting
learning_outcomes: Identify and evaluate complex challenges related to urban sustainability and social change through futures, systems, and social practice theory and methods.
Identify and assess how specific values inform our preferences about futures worth wanting.
Present insights about possible and preferable futures through written, visual, and performance modes of communication.
Reflect on and express insights about your values, perceptions of and preferences about futures and future change.
Demonstrate effective collaboration and individual contributions to team work.
95006.learning_and_teaching_activities: This subject combines authentic assessment through an industry partner challenge, with classroom and online learning. Using a participatory futures project to frame the subject, students will complete reading and learning tasks to understand key concepts, then apply those concepts through hands-on activities and receive formative feedback in facilitated tutorial workshops.
95006.description: This subject introduces you to futures studies, a field vital for tackling complex issues in business, government, and society. You will work individually and in teams to respond to a challenge set with our industry partner, the City of Sydney council, to explore and propose a future worth wanting set in "Sustainable Sydney 2050." You will learn and apply practical techniques and theoretical frameworks to: examine how change happens in complex systems, identify and interpret emerging signals of change, and engage peers and partners in exploring possible futures. You will also critically reflect on how your values and personal perspective, and those of individuals, communities, and organisations, shape the way we imagine and create futures worth wanting.

requisite.rule: Requisite rule: ((1 OR 1a OR 1b OR 1c OR 1d OR 1e OR 1f OR 1g))

95007.name: TD: Pathways to societal transitions
learning_outcomes: Integrate different perspectives to unpack and analyse complex challenges that the digital infrastructure industry is creating for local communities and environments.
Identify significant issues and emerging opportunities to create positive change in real-world contexts.
Apply transdisciplinary and creative research methods and collaboration in teams to generate responses to complex challenges.
Develop a cohesive rationale for proposals and communicate ideas persuasively to diverse audiences.
Synthesise your learning and research to generate insights about transdisciplinarity and your own role in imagining digital futures, and steps to bring about positive social change.
95007.description: Artificial Intelligence seems to be everywhere – embedded in phones, homes, workplaces – but what are the physical infrastructures that make it possible, and how are they reshaping communities and our environment? This subject takes students beyond the screen to investigate the hidden, material side of AI. From data centres and fibre-optic cables to the vast demands for energy, water, and land, students will explore how rapidly expanding digital infrastructure is transforming communities and environments globally, and in their own backyard.Students will hear from a diversity of industry experts working across sectors who are making digital infrastructure expansion possible, and learn how the challenges and opportunities this expansion presents affect sustainability efforts globally, and in Australia. In multidisciplinary teams, students will investigate how digital infrastructure expansion is unfolding in their local community and codesign future scenarios that balance environmental priorities with societal demand for AI. Through this process, they will use a range of ethnographic and creative research methods, including photography, video, and story-making, to learn from their local community and communicate possible futures. This process will be assisted through regular engagements with our industry partner, Lane Cove Council, who will provide first-hand insights into how digital infrastructure expansion is shaping their community.Students participating in this subject will develop skills and confidence in understanding and responding to complex, real-world challenges in multidisciplinary teams, using effective systems approaches to change-making. It is relevant to students from all disciplines including (but not limited to) computer and data science, social sciences, fine arts, communications, science, law and medicine.

requisite.rule: Requisite rule: ((1 OR 1a OR 1b OR 1c OR 1d OR 1e))

95008.name: TD: Transdisciplinary action lab
learning_outcomes: Integrate different perspectives to analyse complex real-world challenges
Demonstrate evaluative judgement grounded in ethical considerations and a reflexive approach in leveraging AI-driven tools.
Apply transdisciplinary collaboration and action-based decision-making methods in teams to generate responses to complex challenges.
Develop reflexive connection with their own evolving perspectives and contributions as a productive member of a multidisciplinary cohort and team
Communicate project outcomes creatively, confidently, and convincingly to internal and external project stakeholders, using a range of modes and media
95008.learning_and_teaching_activities: In this subject, learning will take place in a collaborative, immersive, and experiential environment. A key learning approach is 'learning by doing,' where students work in teams to tackle a complex real-world challenge presented by an industry partner. Ethical collaboration with stakeholders will be encouraged as students develop and test their ideas. Through a combination of formal workshops, informal learning experiences, and hands-on experimentation, students will engage in an iterative trial-and-error process. Online modules and plenary sessions will introduce key concepts and frameworks to deepen their understanding. Teaching staff and peers will provide continuous formative feedback as students refine their initiatives.
95008.description: In this subject, you'll explore real-world challenges posed by the industry partner, MIRO. You’ll take on the role of a decision-maker grappling with uncertainty when facing these pressing challenges and devise practical ways to address them. Working in multidisciplinary teams, you will ideate solutions, field-test them in real-world settings, assess their impact, and propose actions that promote desired outcomes. By crafting responses to complex issues and taking action towards better futures, you'll develop skills in critical thinking, transdisciplinary collaboration, and entrepreneurial mindset. You'll apply these insights to propose enhancements and reimagine future disciplines and future professions.

requisite.rule: Requisite rule: ((1 OR 1a OR 1b OR 1c OR 1d OR 1e))

95009.name: TD: Change-making for social impact
learning_outcomes: Analyse and map systems, organisations and individuals utilising language, principles and tools of complexity to identify leverage points for change and impact
Articulate the current and future structures, processes, and practices that influence change and innovation across organisations, networks and systems
Develop strategies to mobilise and connect people and the organisations, networks and systems they work and live in to create and implement innovation
Design communication strategies to persuade organisational and community leaders to implement innovation in professional or social contexts to achieve impact
95009.learning_and_teaching_activities: The teaching and learning strategies inTD Change Making for Social Impactare grounded in transformative, experiential, and community-based learning. Each week invites students to move through cycles of seeing, being, and doing, beginning with embodied, face-to-face activities that engage emotion, ethics, and complexity, followed by reflective and interactive online sessions that deepen insight and prepare for real-world application. The subject integrates practices from mindfulness, systems thinking/complexity science, decolonial theory, and implementation science, supporting students to explore their own purpose, identity, and agency as they co-design micro-interventions with their communities. Students learn through collaborative inquiry, visual mapping, creative storytelling, ritual, and action, making learning personal, practical, and powerful.
95009.description: How can we transform ourselves and the systems we live in? This subject invites students to explore the deep connection between personal transformation and systemic change. Through embodied, reflective, and collaborative learning, students engage with practices from mindfulness, complexity science, implementation science, decolonial thinking, and First Nations and other diverse knowledge systems. Across two integrated phases—self-transformation and systems evolution—students learn to sit with the discomfort of change within complex systems, understand the roots of suffering and injustice, and take ethical action in response to real-world challenges co-designed with our industry partner Stockland. Through creative, non-written assessments, students map their inner shifts, design micro-interventions within complex systems, and showcase ripple effects of their work through ritual, installation, and action pledges. Learning flows between immersive in-person sessions, interactive online sessions, and reflective practices, supporting students to build purposeful, relational, and sustainable approaches to social impact within the communities they care about.

requisite.rule: Requisite rule: ((1 OR 1a OR 1b OR 1c OR 1d OR 1e OR 1f OR 1g))

have_subject.learning_and_teaching_activities: This subject is offered in a blended mode with interactive plenaries, online modules and lectures, accompanied by interactive and collaborative tutorials, with regular opportunities for informal feedback from peers and academic staff.Students will explore technology futures reimagined in a complex world from multiple disciplinary and intergenerational perspectives, by conducting their own research and engaging with a range of academic and industry/professional perspectives. Students will translate their learnings into practice by addressing a complex real-world challenge posed by an industry partner. As students collaborate in multidisciplinary teams and experiment with different disciplinary approaches to reimagine technologies, they have the opportunity to critically reflect on technology futures in their own disciplines and consider the implications to their future study and profession.
have_subject.description: This subject explores the profound impact of ubiquitous technologies on our behaviours, aspirations, and daily lives. Students develop skills in complex systems thinking, enabling them to navigate real-world challenges around technologies of everyday use and reimagine these technologies with proposals promoting inclusivity and sustainability. Students also engage with industry or community partners for practical experience, working in multidisciplinary teams on hands-on projects and collaborative workshops. This subject prepares students for innovation, sustainability, or community development careers, addressing complex challenges and driving systemic change. This subject also helps students explore the transformative power of technology and steer systems toward a more inclusive future.

have_subject.name: TD: Shaping technologies that shape us
learning_outcomes: Combine disciplinary perspectives, evidence, and diverse viewpoints to holistically analyse the factors influencing the development of novel technologies.
Demonstrate capabilities in divergent and convergent thinking techniques, and transdisciplinary methods, across the stages of an innovation project.
Identify actionable, real-world insights about technological trends to guide the future development of technology.
Develop reflexive connection with their own evolving perspectives and contributions as a productive member of a multidisciplinary cohort and team.
Communicate project outcomes creatively, confidently, and convincingly to internal and external project stakeholders, using a range of modes and media.
have_subject.learning_and_teaching_activities: Experiential problem-based learning; interdisciplinary collaboration; blended learning (technology-mediated instruction); authentic assessment.By collaborating as a cohort and in small group settings, students will examine complex technological trends through the lens of their emerging disciplinary positions and diverse stakeholder perspectives. Interactive Canvas modules will enable students to engage with a range of provocative and engaging multi-media learning resources, including podcasts, videos, selected peer-reviewed articles, and industry reports. These resources will support authentic, practice-based learning to develop intellectual agility, creative skills, and collaboration capability that students can use for the rest of their professional lives. Throughout the subject, students will have ongoing opportunities for formative feedback by peers and academic staff, incrementally building skills they can apply in real-life settings (especially with a view to increasing employability).
have_subject.description: This subject equips you with critical thinking, creative, and collaborative skills to address complex real-world challenges, enhancing your professional capabilities. In response to an industry partner brief from Microsoft, you will explore the wide-ranging impacts, opportunities, and profound questions posed by the exponential growth of Artificial Intelligence (AI) technologies, applying your disciplinary lens while learning to work with other perspectives. By engaging with a range of thought provoking multi-media learning resources, highly interactive classroom activities, and a range of practical tasks, you will develop key insights into the challenge space and deliver a distinctive agenda for the sustainable development of these transformative technologies.

have_subject.name: TD: Envisioning futures worth wanting
learning_outcomes: Identify and evaluate complex challenges related to urban sustainability and social change through futures, systems, and social practice theory and methods.
Identify and assess how specific values inform our preferences about futures worth wanting.
Present insights about possible and preferable futures through written, visual, and performance modes of communication.
Reflect on and express insights about your values, perceptions of and preferences about futures and future change.
Demonstrate effective collaboration and individual contributions to team work.
have_subject.learning_and_teaching_activities: This subject combines authentic assessment through an industry partner challenge, with classroom and online learning. Using a participatory futures project to frame the subject, students will complete reading and learning tasks to understand key concepts, then apply those concepts through hands-on activities and receive formative feedback in facilitated tutorial workshops.
have_subject.description: This subject introduces you to futures studies, a field vital for tackling complex issues in business, government, and society. You will work individually and in teams to respond to a challenge set with our industry partner, the City of Sydney council, to explore and propose a future worth wanting set in "Sustainable Sydney 2050." You will learn and apply practical techniques and theoretical frameworks to: examine how change happens in complex systems, identify and interpret emerging signals of change, and engage peers and partners in exploring possible futures. You will also critically reflect on how your values and personal perspective, and those of individuals, communities, and organisations, shape the way we imagine and create futures worth wanting.

have_subject.name: TD: Pathways to societal transitions
learning_outcomes: Integrate different perspectives to unpack and analyse complex challenges that the digital infrastructure industry is creating for local communities and environments.
Identify significant issues and emerging opportunities to create positive change in real-world contexts.
Apply transdisciplinary and creative research methods and collaboration in teams to generate responses to complex challenges.
Develop a cohesive rationale for proposals and communicate ideas persuasively to diverse audiences.
Synthesise your learning and research to generate insights about transdisciplinarity and your own role in imagining digital futures, and steps to bring about positive social change.
have_subject.description: Artificial Intelligence seems to be everywhere – embedded in phones, homes, workplaces – but what are the physical infrastructures that make it possible, and how are they reshaping communities and our environment? This subject takes students beyond the screen to investigate the hidden, material side of AI. From data centres and fibre-optic cables to the vast demands for energy, water, and land, students will explore how rapidly expanding digital infrastructure is transforming communities and environments globally, and in their own backyard.Students will hear from a diversity of industry experts working across sectors who are making digital infrastructure expansion possible, and learn how the challenges and opportunities this expansion presents affect sustainability efforts globally, and in Australia. In multidisciplinary teams, students will investigate how digital infrastructure expansion is unfolding in their local community and codesign future scenarios that balance environmental priorities with societal demand for AI. Through this process, they will use a range of ethnographic and creative research methods, including photography, video, and story-making, to learn from their local community and communicate possible futures. This process will be assisted through regular engagements with our industry partner, Lane Cove Council, who will provide first-hand insights into how digital infrastructure expansion is shaping their community.Students participating in this subject will develop skills and confidence in understanding and responding to complex, real-world challenges in multidisciplinary teams, using effective systems approaches to change-making. It is relevant to students from all disciplines including (but not limited to) computer and data science, social sciences, fine arts, communications, science, law and medicine.

have_subject.name: TD: Transdisciplinary action lab
learning_outcomes: Integrate different perspectives to analyse complex real-world challenges
Demonstrate evaluative judgement grounded in ethical considerations and a reflexive approach in leveraging AI-driven tools.
Apply transdisciplinary collaboration and action-based decision-making methods in teams to generate responses to complex challenges.
Develop reflexive connection with their own evolving perspectives and contributions as a productive member of a multidisciplinary cohort and team
Communicate project outcomes creatively, confidently, and convincingly to internal and external project stakeholders, using a range of modes and media
have_subject.learning_and_teaching_activities: In this subject, learning will take place in a collaborative, immersive, and experiential environment. A key learning approach is 'learning by doing,' where students work in teams to tackle a complex real-world challenge presented by an industry partner. Ethical collaboration with stakeholders will be encouraged as students develop and test their ideas. Through a combination of formal workshops, informal learning experiences, and hands-on experimentation, students will engage in an iterative trial-and-error process. Online modules and plenary sessions will introduce key concepts and frameworks to deepen their understanding. Teaching staff and peers will provide continuous formative feedback as students refine their initiatives.
have_subject.description: In this subject, you'll explore real-world challenges posed by the industry partner, MIRO. You’ll take on the role of a decision-maker grappling with uncertainty when facing these pressing challenges and devise practical ways to address them. Working in multidisciplinary teams, you will ideate solutions, field-test them in real-world settings, assess their impact, and propose actions that promote desired outcomes. By crafting responses to complex issues and taking action towards better futures, you'll develop skills in critical thinking, transdisciplinary collaboration, and entrepreneurial mindset. You'll apply these insights to propose enhancements and reimagine future disciplines and future professions.

have_subject.name: TD: Change-making for social impact
learning_outcomes: Analyse and map systems, organisations and individuals utilising language, principles and tools of complexity to identify leverage points for change and impact
Articulate the current and future structures, processes, and practices that influence change and innovation across organisations, networks and systems
Develop strategies to mobilise and connect people and the organisations, networks and systems they work and live in to create and implement innovation
Design communication strategies to persuade organisational and community leaders to implement innovation in professional or social contexts to achieve impact
have_subject.learning_and_teaching_activities: The teaching and learning strategies inTD Change Making for Social Impactare grounded in transformative, experiential, and community-based learning. Each week invites students to move through cycles of seeing, being, and doing, beginning with embodied, face-to-face activities that engage emotion, ethics, and complexity, followed by reflective and interactive online sessions that deepen insight and prepare for real-world application. The subject integrates practices from mindfulness, systems thinking/complexity science, decolonial theory, and implementation science, supporting students to explore their own purpose, identity, and agency as they co-design micro-interventions with their communities. Students learn through collaborative inquiry, visual mapping, creative storytelling, ritual, and action, making learning personal, practical, and powerful.
have_subject.description: How can we transform ourselves and the systems we live in? This subject invites students to explore the deep connection between personal transformation and systemic change. Through embodied, reflective, and collaborative learning, students engage with practices from mindfulness, complexity science, implementation science, decolonial thinking, and First Nations and other diverse knowledge systems. Across two integrated phases—self-transformation and systems evolution—students learn to sit with the discomfort of change within complex systems, understand the roots of suffering and injustice, and take ethical action in response to real-world challenges co-designed with our industry partner Stockland. Through creative, non-written assessments, students map their inner shifts, design micro-interventions within complex systems, and showcase ripple effects of their work through ritual, installation, and action pledges. Learning flows between immersive in-person sessions, interactive online sessions, and reflective practices, supporting students to build purposeful, relational, and sustainable approaches to social impact within the communities they care about.

977917.name: In-country Contemporary Society Study
learning_outcomes: Communicate effectively and appropriately in written and oral academic and professional English
Critically analyse the behaviours, norms, and values of host and home cultures, paying particular attention to work and work-place related practices and interactions
Evaluate own culture-based assumptions and how they are informed
Reflect on how learning has occurred through the overseas program
Articulate the value of international experiences for career and professional purposes
977917.learning_and_teaching_activities: This subject provides an immersive, practical learning experience in an international location and supports this learning through pre-departure and post-sojourn activities. Before coming to the subject preparation seminar, students engage with online readings and materials. The subject preparation seminar allows students to work in groups to explore aspects of intercultural learning and to help prepare them for their practical experience in a different society and culture. An online quiz to test students’ understanding of the core concepts is available on UTSOnline before departure. During the overseas stay, students participate in a structured program with the host organisation designed to enhance students’ transferable skills and practical experiences. In the subject return seminar, students reflect collaboratively and individually on their experiences and professional application of their learnt skills. Assessment-specific online modules on UTSOnline allow students to gain a deeper understanding of intercultural communication, reflective learning and international issues.
977917.description: This subject consists of an international study program with a UTS and coursework (two online seminars) at UTS. This subject provides you with the opportunity to develop your intercultural awareness, and sociocultural knowledge through an international study program with an international UTS partner organisation. It is offered in July and Summer sessions with some varying programs. You will also attend two online seminars at UTS before the start of your international program, as well as after returning from your international program to support your learning experience. The international program includes classroom-based or virtual seminars. The subject focuses on improving your intercultural communication skills and developing a cultural awareness of the multi-dimensional nuanced nature of culture. The collaborative and reflective learning activities during the coursework will allow you to develop your intercultural capabilities via critical thinking through the understanding of your own culture and others. The assessments will require your critical and reflection skills through theoretical and methodological approaches. With the knowledge, values, and skills that you have gained through this course, you will be able to apply them in your professional life. To enrol, firstly, you must be accepted into an approved international program such us UTS Global Short Programs and other pre-approved Faculty led international activities. Secondly, you need to provide the confirmation of your acceptance when you submit your eRequest.

items.note: Faculty Approval

In the subject return seminar, students reflect collaboratively and individually on their experiences and professional application of their learnt skills. 97901.description: This subject consists of an international internship program with a UTS and coursework (two online seminars) at UTS. This subject provides you with the opportunity to develop your intercultural awareness, and professional competencies through the completion of an intensive internship, virtual placement, practicum, or entrepreneurship placement with an international organisation. It is offered in July and Summer sessions with some varying programs. You will also attend two online seminars at UTS before the start of your international program, as well as after returning from your international internship program to support your learning experience. This intensive internship includes in-person or virtual placement. The international internship program in a company, organisation, or institution focuses on collaboration in interdisciplinary teams on tasks and projects, tackling the real world, as well as gaining insights into diverse work practices and functions. The subject also focuses on improving your intercultural communication skills and developing a cultural awareness of the multi-dimensional nuanced nature of culture teams, as well as professional competences. The collaborative and reflective learning activities during the coursework will allow you to develop your intercultural capabilities via critical thinking through the understanding of your own culture and others. The assessments will require your critical and reflection skills through theoretical and methodological approaches. With the knowledge, values, and skills that you have gained through this course, you will be able to apply them in your professional life. To enrol, firstly, you must be accepted into an approved international internship program such us UTS Global Short Programs and other pre-approved Faculty led international activities. 41130.name: Professional Practice Studio
learning_outcomes: Apply engineering processes to define a problem, generate possible solutions, select and justify suitable options, and prototype and verify solutions, in a complex interdisciplinary context. (C.1)
Collaborate effectively in interdisciplinary teams and apply team processes, including self and peer evaluation. (E.1)
Articulate and present project work to clients and stakeholders to elicit feedback. (E.1)
Reflect on own developing capabilities to articulate strengths and areas for professional growth. (F.1)
41130.learning_and_teaching_activities: In this subject, students will be introduced to a client problem and through a weekly workshop and work outside class, students are led through a systematic design process to develop solutions to meet client needs. During the early weeks of the studio, workshops will be directed by staff. As students develop greater autonomy in the design process, later weeks will move towards a team-directed mode.
41130.description: The Professional Practice Studio is an interdisciplinary space where students from engineering and IT collaborate on complex, real-world problems alongside industry and community partners. This subject challenges your perceptions of your studies, career, and professional identity. You'll apply engineering and IT skills to unconventional problems, demonstrating the versatility of these disciplines. The studio enhances research, design thinking, and systems thinking skills, while offering networking opportunities with professionals and academics. You'll gain a portfolio-worthy project, learn to articulate your practice to employers, and understand the application of academic learning in professional settings. The studio combines workshops and independent work, transitioning from staff-led to student-directed as you progress. For more details and to prepare for enrolment, visit the UTS Professional Practice Studio website.

SMJ10196.name: Humanitarian Engineering
SMJ10196.description: In this sub-major, students are introduced to some key concepts in humanitarian engineering, following on from their experiences with the Engineers without Borders Challenge. Building on that foundation, they have opportunities to develop their intercultural and transdisciplinary collaboration skills through a range of learning experiences, working with partners and other students from a range of backgrounds and contexts, to equip them to use engineering to make positive changes in a complex world. Students have a choice of transdisciplinary electives. “95009 TD: Changemaking for social impact” and “95003 TD: Sustainability in an interconnected world” are recommended.

contents.structure_details: Complete the following subject:

have_subject.name: Intercultural Communication
learning_outcomes: Critically engage with intercultural communication theories and frameworks and apply these theories to analyse diverse cultural situations and spaces
Reflect on own perceptions of intercultural spaces and encounters and intercultural skills development for professional and everyday contexts
Design and conduct independent, ethical, small scale research about aspects of interculturality
Recognise the ongoing impact of colonialism on and positive contributions of Indigenous cultures in intercultural spaces
Communicate effectively and appropriately in written and oral academic English
have_subject.learning_and_teaching_activities: Classes will incorporate a range of teaching and learning strategies including presentations, videos, online learning materials, reflective surveys, discussion of readings, international case studies and group activities. These will be complemented by independent student research, readings and participation in online discussion forums.Feedback on assessment tasks will be provided in class, through quizzes and via GradeBook in Canvas.
have_subject.description: This subject introduces you the core concepts and debates in the interdisciplinary field of intercultural communication. You'll be prepared for the interpersonal and dynamic nature of working in increasingly globalised workplaces and intercultural environments. You'll use a problem-based approach to critically examine numerous frameworks and theories that allow you to interpret intercultural encounters in praxis-based scenarios. You'll apply specific intercultural communication strategies and approaches for their relevance to industry, communities, workplaces and society more broadly.

contents.structure_details: Complete 6 credit points from the following subjects:

have_subject.name: Introduction to Humanitarian Engineering
learning_outcomes: Apply the values and principles of humanitarian engineering in the evaluation of a case study, concept, or approach. (B.1)
Communicate research about humanitarian engineering to inform a multi-disciplinary audience. (E.1)
Reflect on the value of humanitarian engineering and, in particular, on perspectives on socio-technical thinking and co-design. (F.1)
have_subject.learning_and_teaching_activities: Students have the valuable opportunity to engage in weekly three-hour interactive workshops.Complementing workshops are interactive online content for students to work through out-of-class and in preparation for in-class collaborative activities.Analog and digital collaboration tools in Canvas support all learning.Engagement in this interactive learning approach is critical to contributing to successful progress and can be applied to developing professional skills and knowledge to further studies and/or the professional workplace.Guest speakers with industry experience bring their specialist expertise and share these with classes to inform and enrich classroom discussion.Such design of workshops provide weekly formative feedback that is pertinent to summative assessment tasks.
have_subject.description: Humanitarian engineering is the application of engineering skills and approaches to enable basic needs and contribute to social justice, with contexts ranging from community and technology development to emergency disaster response, both domestically and internationally. This subject equips you with skills transferable to all engineering fields, including stakeholder engagement, cross-cultural work, human-centred design, and environmental stewardship.You'll build on prior experiences from the Engineers without Borders Challenge, learning about international development, effective humanitarian engineering practices, and key agencies in the field. The subject encourages personal reflection on the significance of humanitarian engineering for your development and allows you to delve into a topic of personal interest (such as a particular case study, approach, context, or how engineering relates to a particular Sustainable Development Goal) in more detail.Interactive three-hour weekly workshops, complemented by online content, are central to the learning process, fostering collaboration and professional skill development. Canvas tools support both analog and digital collaboration, enhancing your learning experience. Industry guest speakers contribute real-world insights, and the workshop format provides ongoing formative feedback, crucial for your assessments and professional growth.

have_subject.name: Interrogating Technology: Sustainability, Environment and Social Change
learning_outcomes: Use appropriate research tools to interpret philosophical, sociological and political perspectives, and the perspectives of different stakeholders, on emerging technologies. (B.1)
Apply relevant theoretical frameworks to interrogate and represent different aspects of the relationships between technology, society, and the environment. (B.1)
Propose, and advocate for, policy initiatives regarding an emergent technology or technological issue to improve outcomes for a designated stakeholder. (B.1)
have_subject.learning_and_teaching_activities: In this subject you will develop skills to articulate comprehensive, convincing and informed judgements about technology based on credible sources.Students need to engage in pre-class tasks on Canvas including watching videos, guided reading, and responding to set questions. During class students will use their work for peer review/discussions/sprint presentations and tutor feedback. Those classes allow for groups of students to collaborate in preparation for assignments. Feedback for assignments are provided to students in a number of ways including Canvas one-to-one feedback, group feedback, and peer reviews. Guest speakers will share their experiences with - and perspectives on - subject themes.Groups investigate stakeholders that could be affected by a particular technology and construct a convincing case for consultation. Individually, students develop a policy position paper on behalf of one of those stakeholders. This hinges on good communication and research skills which will take the form of reporting, presenting, and debating. Active participation, peer interaction and feedback are critical for achieving the subject learning outcomes.A program of readings and other resources scaffold the learning required for critical perspectives to interrogate technologies.
have_subject.description: This subject equips you with the skills to examine the relationship between engineering and society through philosophical, sociological, and political lenses. You'll be introduced to theoretical frameworks and research tools to investigate stakeholder perspectives on complex issues and their influence on policy. Through case studies of new technologies and engineering projects, you'll apply this learning directly to your engineering context, gaining a deeper understanding of the multifaceted interactions at play.

items.details: 48250 Economics and Finance for Engineering Projects

have_subject.name: TD: Reframing, remixing, reimagining society
learning_outcomes: Integrate different perspectives to analyse complex challenges.
Identify significant issues and emerging opportunities to create positive change in real-world contexts.
Apply transdisciplinary collaboration and change-making methods in teams to generate responses to complex challenges.
Develop a cohesive rationale for proposals and communicate ideas persuasively to diverse audiences.
Synthesise your learning to generate insights about transdisciplinarity and your own role in fostering positive social change.
have_subject.learning_and_teaching_activities: Students will attend plenary sessions, interact with online modules and unpack learnings in tutorial workshops to gain understanding of relevant concepts and frameworks, and engage in dialogue with academics, key stakeholders, and peers. As students experiment with creative practices by drawing on cases and approaches from across different disciplines, they will have ongoing opportunities for formative feedback by peers and academic staff.
have_subject.description: In this subject, students interrogate their personal and disciplinary mental models, worldviews, biases and assumptions to question the current framing of complex societal problems by examining a real-world challenge posed by UNICEF. Students explore a range of disciplinary and professional perspectives and collaborate in multidisciplinary teams to creatively remix these ideas. Together, they develop innovative approaches aimed at ensuring inter-generational justice in the face of climate change. A range of creative methods are used to cultivate a culture of curiosity, experimentation, and inventiveness, while also identifying and probing unknowns. Through generating novel responses, students test their own emerging disciplinary or professional expertise; they learn to collaborate across differences, generate insights well beyond their discipline, and use these insights to create new avenues for action in the real world.

have_subject.name: TD: Sustainability in an interconnected world
learning_outcomes: Draw from different perspectives to investigate the assumptions inherent in framing complex problem situations
Examine and evaluate methods and practices for engaging with complex challenges from across a range of disciplines and professional domains
Articulate questions and proposals for thinking differently about global challenges, and identify new avenues for action
Communicate new understandings of complex problem situations persuasively to diverse audiences
have_subject.learning_and_teaching_activities: This subject is offered in a blended mode. Students engage with online modules and materials at their own pace to gain an understanding of relevant concepts and frameworks. This learning is then applied to real-world challenges in collaborative, immersive and experiential tutorials. As students experiment with approaches for investigating complex problem situations, they have ongoing opportunities for formative feedback from peers and academic staff.
have_subject.description: This subject introduces students to ways of thinking differently about the ‘wicked’ problems we face when tackling big issues around sustainability. Students draw on a range of approaches from across our disciplines and professional domains to explore the complex social and environmental context in which every problem is situated and networked with other problems. Striving towards regenerative sustainable futures, these novel ways of thinking enable students to move away from conventional problem-solving approaches to discover new avenues for action. By working in multidisciplinary teams on complex industry or community challenges, students develop their relational and analytic skills. Students devise their own questions based on insights they’ve uncovered, and communicate their proposals for addressing current or emerging global challenges to a range of diverse audiences.

have_subject.name: TD: Technologies reimagined in a complex world
learning_outcomes: Investigate a range of emerging ubiquitous technologies and analyse their impact on careers, organisations and communities
Apply a selection of disciplinary and transdisciplinary research methods to investigate and map the complex impacts of an emerging technology in human societies

Generate reimagined technological responses to real-world challenges by integrating creative and analytic approaches from a range of disciplines with a focus on creating value through sustainable and entrepreneurial innovation
Develop a cohesive rationale for proposals and communicate these proposals/ideas to diverse audiences
Reflect on issues of ethics, values, sustainability and inclusivity in relation to technology and society
have_subject.learning_and_teaching_activities: This subject is offered in a blended mode with interactive plenaries, online modules and lectures, accompanied by interactive and collaborative tutorials, with regular opportunities for informal feedback from peers and academic staff.Students will explore technology futures reimagined in a complex world from multiple disciplinary and intergenerational perspectives, by conducting their own research and engaging with a range of academic and industry/professional perspectives. Students will translate their learnings into practice by addressing a complex real-world challenge posed by an industry partner. As students collaborate in multidisciplinary teams and experiment with different disciplinary approaches to reimagine technologies, they have the opportunity to critically reflect on technology futures in their own disciplines and consider the implications to their future study and profession.
have_subject.description: This subject explores the profound impact of ubiquitous technologies on our behaviours, aspirations, and daily lives. Students develop skills in complex systems thinking, enabling them to navigate real-world challenges around technologies of everyday use and reimagine these technologies with proposals promoting inclusivity and sustainability. Students also engage with industry or community partners for practical experience, working in multidisciplinary teams on hands-on projects and collaborative workshops. This subject prepares students for innovation, sustainability, or community development careers, addressing complex challenges and driving systemic change. This subject also helps students explore the transformative power of technology and steer systems toward a more inclusive future.

have_subject.name: TD: Shaping technologies that shape us
learning_outcomes: Combine disciplinary perspectives, evidence, and diverse viewpoints to holistically analyse the factors influencing the development of novel technologies.
Demonstrate capabilities in divergent and convergent thinking techniques, and transdisciplinary methods, across the stages of an innovation project.
Identify actionable, real-world insights about technological trends to guide the future development of technology.
Develop reflexive connection with their own evolving perspectives and contributions as a productive member of a multidisciplinary cohort and team.
Communicate project outcomes creatively, confidently, and convincingly to internal and external project stakeholders, using a range of modes and media.
have_subject.learning_and_teaching_activities: Experiential problem-based learning; interdisciplinary collaboration; blended learning (technology-mediated instruction); authentic assessment.By collaborating as a cohort and in small group settings, students will examine complex technological trends through the lens of their emerging disciplinary positions and diverse stakeholder perspectives. Interactive Canvas modules will enable students to engage with a range of provocative and engaging multi-media learning resources, including podcasts, videos, selected peer-reviewed articles, and industry reports. These resources will support authentic, practice-based learning to develop intellectual agility, creative skills, and collaboration capability that students can use for the rest of their professional lives. Throughout the subject, students will have ongoing opportunities for formative feedback by peers and academic staff, incrementally building skills they can apply in real-life settings (especially with a view to increasing employability).
have_subject.description: This subject equips you with critical thinking, creative, and collaborative skills to address complex real-world challenges, enhancing your professional capabilities. In response to an industry partner brief from Microsoft, you will explore the wide-ranging impacts, opportunities, and profound questions posed by the exponential growth of Artificial Intelligence (AI) technologies, applying your disciplinary lens while learning to work with other perspectives. By engaging with a range of thought provoking multi-media learning resources, highly interactive classroom activities, and a range of practical tasks, you will develop key insights into the challenge space and deliver a distinctive agenda for the sustainable development of these transformative technologies.

have_subject.name: TD: Envisioning futures worth wanting
learning_outcomes: Identify and evaluate complex challenges related to urban sustainability and social change through futures, systems, and social practice theory and methods.
Identify and assess how specific values inform our preferences about futures worth wanting.
Present insights about possible and preferable futures through written, visual, and performance modes of communication.
Reflect on and express insights about your values, perceptions of and preferences about futures and future change.
Demonstrate effective collaboration and individual contributions to team work.
have_subject.learning_and_teaching_activities: This subject combines authentic assessment through an industry partner challenge, with classroom and online learning. Using a participatory futures project to frame the subject, students will complete reading and learning tasks to understand key concepts, then apply those concepts through hands-on activities and receive formative feedback in facilitated tutorial workshops.
have_subject.description: This subject introduces you to futures studies, a field vital for tackling complex issues in business, government, and society. You will work individually and in teams to respond to a challenge set with our industry partner, the City of Sydney council, to explore and propose a future worth wanting set in "Sustainable Sydney 2050." You will learn and apply practical techniques and theoretical frameworks to: examine how change happens in complex systems, identify and interpret emerging signals of change, and engage peers and partners in exploring possible futures. You will also critically reflect on how your values and personal perspective, and those of individuals, communities, and organisations, shape the way we imagine and create futures worth wanting.

have_subject.name: TD: Pathways to societal transitions
learning_outcomes: Integrate different perspectives to unpack and analyse complex challenges that the digital infrastructure industry is creating for local communities and environments.
Identify significant issues and emerging opportunities to create positive change in real-world contexts.
Apply transdisciplinary and creative research methods and collaboration in teams to generate responses to complex challenges.
Develop a cohesive rationale for proposals and communicate ideas persuasively to diverse audiences.
Synthesise your learning and research to generate insights about transdisciplinarity and your own role in imagining digital futures, and steps to bring about positive social change.
have_subject.description: Artificial Intelligence seems to be everywhere – embedded in phones, homes, workplaces – but what are the physical infrastructures that make it possible, and how are they reshaping communities and our environment? This subject takes students beyond the screen to investigate the hidden, material side of AI. From data centres and fibre-optic cables to the vast demands for energy, water, and land, students will explore how rapidly expanding digital infrastructure is transforming communities and environments globally, and in their own backyard.Students will hear from a diversity of industry experts working across sectors who are making digital infrastructure expansion possible, and learn how the challenges and opportunities this expansion presents affect sustainability efforts globally, and in Australia. In multidisciplinary teams, students will investigate how digital infrastructure expansion is unfolding in their local community and codesign future scenarios that balance environmental priorities with societal demand for AI. Through this process, they will use a range of ethnographic and creative research methods, including photography, video, and story-making, to learn from their local community and communicate possible futures. This process will be assisted through regular engagements with our industry partner, Lane Cove Council, who will provide first-hand insights into how digital infrastructure expansion is shaping their community.Students participating in this subject will develop skills and confidence in understanding and responding to complex, real-world challenges in multidisciplinary teams, using effective systems approaches to change-making. It is relevant to students from all disciplines including (but not limited to) computer and data science, social sciences, fine arts, communications, science, law and medicine.

have_subject.name: TD: Transdisciplinary action lab
learning_outcomes: Integrate different perspectives to analyse complex real-world challenges
Demonstrate evaluative judgement grounded in ethical considerations and a reflexive approach in leveraging AI-driven tools.
Apply transdisciplinary collaboration and action-based decision-making methods in teams to generate responses to complex challenges.
Develop reflexive connection with their own evolving perspectives and contributions as a productive member of a multidisciplinary cohort and team
Communicate project outcomes creatively, confidently, and convincingly to internal and external project stakeholders, using a range of modes and media
have_subject.learning_and_teaching_activities: In this subject, learning will take place in a collaborative, immersive, and experiential environment. A key learning approach is 'learning by doing,' where students work in teams to tackle a complex real-world challenge presented by an industry partner. Ethical collaboration with stakeholders will be encouraged as students develop and test their ideas. Through a combination of formal workshops, informal learning experiences, and hands-on experimentation, students will engage in an iterative trial-and-error process. Online modules and plenary sessions will introduce key concepts and frameworks to deepen their understanding. Teaching staff and peers will provide continuous formative feedback as students refine their initiatives.
have_subject.description: In this subject, you'll explore real-world challenges posed by the industry partner, MIRO. You’ll take on the role of a decision-maker grappling with uncertainty when facing these pressing challenges and devise practical ways to address them. Working in multidisciplinary teams, you will ideate solutions, field-test them in real-world settings, assess their impact, and propose actions that promote desired outcomes. By crafting responses to complex issues and taking action towards better futures, you'll develop skills in critical thinking, transdisciplinary collaboration, and entrepreneurial mindset. You'll apply these insights to propose enhancements and reimagine future disciplines and future professions.

have_subject.name: TD: Change-making for social impact
learning_outcomes: Analyse and map systems, organisations and individuals utilising language, principles and tools of complexity to identify leverage points for change and impact
Articulate the current and future structures, processes, and practices that influence change and innovation across organisations, networks and systems
Develop strategies to mobilise and connect people and the organisations, networks and systems they work and live in to create and implement innovation
Design communication strategies to persuade organisational and community leaders to implement innovation in professional or social contexts to achieve impact
have_subject.learning_and_teaching_activities: The teaching and learning strategies inTD Change Making for Social Impactare grounded in transformative, experiential, and community-based learning. Each week invites students to move through cycles of seeing, being, and doing, beginning with embodied, face-to-face activities that engage emotion, ethics, and complexity, followed by reflective and interactive online sessions that deepen insight and prepare for real-world application. The subject integrates practices from mindfulness, systems thinking/complexity science, decolonial theory, and implementation science, supporting students to explore their own purpose, identity, and agency as they co-design micro-interventions with their communities. Students learn through collaborative inquiry, visual mapping, creative storytelling, ritual, and action, making learning personal, practical, and powerful.
have_subject.description: How can we transform ourselves and the systems we live in? This subject invites students to explore the deep connection between personal transformation and systemic change. Through embodied, reflective, and collaborative learning, students engage with practices from mindfulness, complexity science, implementation science, decolonial thinking, and First Nations and other diverse knowledge systems. Across two integrated phases—self-transformation and systems evolution—students learn to sit with the discomfort of change within complex systems, understand the roots of suffering and injustice, and take ethical action in response to real-world challenges co-designed with our industry partner Stockland. Through creative, non-written assessments, students map their inner shifts, design micro-interventions within complex systems, and showcase ripple effects of their work through ritual, installation, and action pledges. Learning flows between immersive in-person sessions, interactive online sessions, and reflective practices, supporting students to build purposeful, relational, and sustainable approaches to social impact within the communities they care about.

requisite.rule: Requisite rule: ((1 OR 1a OR 1b OR 1c OR 1d OR 1e OR 1f OR 1g))
