contents.structure_details: Select 6 credit points from the following subjects:

have_subject.name: In-country Contemporary Society Study
learning_outcomes: Communicate effectively and appropriately in written and oral academic and professional English
Critically analyse the behaviours, norms, and values of host and home cultures, paying particular attention to work and work-place related practices and interactions
Evaluate own culture-based assumptions and how they are informed
Reflect on how learning has occurred through the overseas program
Articulate the value of international experiences for career and professional purposes
have_subject.learning_and_teaching_activities: This subject provides an immersive, practical learning experience in an international location and supports this learning through pre-departure and post-sojourn activities. Before coming to the subject preparation seminar, students engage with online readings and materials. The subject preparation seminar allows students to work in groups to explore aspects of intercultural learning and to help prepare them for their practical experience in a different society and culture. An online quiz to test students’ understanding of the core concepts is available on UTSOnline before departure. During the overseas stay, students participate in a structured program with the host organisation designed to enhance students’ transferable skills and practical experiences. In the subject return seminar, students reflect collaboratively and individually on their experiences and professional application of their learnt skills. Assessment-specific online modules on UTSOnline allow students to gain a deeper understanding of intercultural communication, reflective learning and international issues.
have_subject.description: This subject consists of an international study program with a UTS and coursework (two online seminars) at UTS. This subject provides you with the opportunity to develop your intercultural awareness, and sociocultural knowledge through an international study program with an international UTS partner organisation. It is offered in July and Summer sessions with some varying programs. You will also attend two online seminars at UTS before the start of your international program, as well as after returning from your international program to support your learning experience. The international program includes classroom-based or virtual seminars. The subject focuses on improving your intercultural communication skills and developing a cultural awareness of the multi-dimensional nuanced nature of culture. The collaborative and reflective learning activities during the coursework will allow you to develop your intercultural capabilities via critical thinking through the understanding of your own culture and others. The assessments will require your critical and reflection skills through theoretical and methodological approaches. With the knowledge, values, and skills that you have gained through this course, you will be able to apply them in your professional life. To enrol, firstly, you must be accepted into an approved international program such us UTS Global Short Programs and other pre-approved Faculty led international activities. Secondly, you need to provide the confirmation of your acceptance when you submit your eRequest.

items.note: Faculty Approval

have_subject.name: International Internship
learning_outcomes: Communicate effectively and appropriately in written and oral academic and professional English
Critically analyse the behaviours, norms, and values of host and home cultures, paying particular attention to work and work-place related practices and interactions
Evaluate own culture-based assumptions and how they are informed
Reflect on how learning has occurred through the overseas program
Articulate the value of international experiences for career and professional purposes
have_subject.learning_and_teaching_activities: This subject provides an immersive, practical learning experience in an international location and supports this learning through pre-departure and post-sojourn activities. Before coming to the subject preparation seminar, students engage with online readings and materials. The subject preparation seminar allows students to work in groups to explore aspects of intercultural learning and to help prepare them for their practical experience in a different society and culture. An online quiz to test students’ understanding of the core concepts is available on UTSOnline before departure. During the overseas stay, students participate in a structured program with the host organisation designed to enhance students’ transferable skills and practical experiences. In the subject return seminar, students reflect collaboratively and individually on their experiences and professional application of their learnt skills. Assessment-specific online modules on UTSOnline allow students to gain a deeper understanding of intercultural communication, reflective learning and international issues.
have_subject.description: This subject consists of an international internship program with a UTS and coursework (two online seminars) at UTS. This subject provides you with the opportunity to develop your intercultural awareness, and professional competencies through the completion of an intensive internship, virtual placement, practicum, or entrepreneurship placement with an international organisation. It is offered in July and Summer sessions with some varying programs. You will also attend two online seminars at UTS before the start of your international program, as well as after returning from your international internship program to support your learning experience. This intensive internship includes in-person or virtual placement. The international internship program in a company, organisation, or institution focuses on collaboration in interdisciplinary teams on tasks and projects, tackling the real world, as well as gaining insights into diverse work practices and functions. The subject also focuses on improving your intercultural communication skills and developing a cultural awareness of the multi-dimensional nuanced nature of culture teams, as well as professional competences. The collaborative and reflective learning activities during the coursework will allow you to develop your intercultural capabilities via critical thinking through the understanding of your own culture and others. The assessments will require your critical and reflection skills through theoretical and methodological approaches. With the knowledge, values, and skills that you have gained through this course, you will be able to apply them in your professional life. To enrol, firstly, you must be accepted into an approved international internship program such us UTS Global Short Programs and other pre-approved Faculty led international activities. Secondly, you need to provide the confirmation of your acceptance when you submit your eRequest.

items.note: Faculty Approval

have_subject.name: Professional Practice Studio
learning_outcomes: Apply engineering processes to define a problem, generate possible solutions, select and justify suitable options, and prototype and verify solutions, in a complex interdisciplinary context. (C.1)
Collaborate effectively in interdisciplinary teams and apply team processes, including self and peer evaluation. (E.1)
Articulate and present project work to clients and stakeholders to elicit feedback. (E.1)
Reflect on own developing capabilities to articulate strengths and areas for professional growth. (F.1)
have_subject.learning_and_teaching_activities: In this subject, students will be introduced to a client problem and through a weekly workshop and work outside class, students are led through a systematic design process to develop solutions to meet client needs. During the early weeks of the studio, workshops will be directed by staff. As students develop greater autonomy in the design process, later weeks will move towards a team-directed mode.
have_subject.description: The Professional Practice Studio is an interdisciplinary space where students from engineering and IT collaborate on complex, real-world problems alongside industry and community partners. This subject challenges your perceptions of your studies, career, and professional identity. You'll apply engineering and IT skills to unconventional problems, demonstrating the versatility of these disciplines. The studio enhances research, design thinking, and systems thinking skills, while offering networking opportunities with professionals and academics. You'll gain a portfolio-worthy project, learn to articulate your practice to employers, and understand the application of academic learning in professional settings. The studio combines workshops and independent work, transitioning from staff-led to student-directed as you progress. For more details and to prepare for enrolment, visit the UTS Professional Practice Studio website.

requisite.rule: Requisite rule: ((1 OR 1a OR 1b OR 1c OR 1d OR 1e) AND (2 OR 2a))

41181.name: Information Security and Management
learning_outcomes: Investigate security management issues in organisations by analysing requirements, plans and IT security policies. (B.1)
Propose appropriate information security protection mechanisms by assessing security risks, threats and vulnerabilities. (B.1)
Identify security training and education needs and associated legal and ethical awareness for organisational personnel. (B.1)
Apply organisational planning and project management principles to IT security planning individually and collaboratively. (E.1)
Demonstrate awareness on how to assess and identify cyber security issues when working with, and for, Indigenous Australians. (A.1)
41181.learning_and_teaching_activities: The subject has one contact hour in Week 1 and three contact hours per week in the remaining weeks.This subject involves a combination of guided self-study learning activities, lectures and workshops to support an inquiry-based learning strategy.A weekly online study module presents new material in the form of videos, written articles and open education resources. Students will be required to complete the online study module before taking the lectures and workshops.Regular lectures include interactive quizzes, discussions and reflections on the key topics and feedbacks.Within weekly two-hour workshops, students present the current security issues following the standard procedure, and conduct practical lab independently.Students must attend workshop sessions and lectures to complete the required assessment tasks. Students will receive continued feedback for their exercises and assessments on weekly basis.
41181.description: This subject focuses on training you in IT security policy development and human security management while addressing critical legal and ethical considerations. You will lay the groundwork for applying cyber safety and security measures at a corporate level. By conducting security assessments within business constraints, you will learn to employ professional methodologies effectively. The subject also covers the systematic evaluation of business and security operations, equipping you with skills in contingency planning, risk assessment, risk management, and compliance across diverse business environments. You'll be prepared to develop comprehensive security policies and manage human factors in cybersecurity, ensuring robust protection and ethical governance within organisations.

requisite.rule: Requisite rule: ((1 OR 1a OR 1b OR 1c OR 1d OR 1e OR 1f) AND (2 OR 2a OR 2b OR 2c))

48730.name: Cybersecurity
learning_outcomes: Recognise and compare major theories and principles that are used in the implementation of a secure network. (D.1)
Apply the underlying algorithms used to construct Security Protocols. (D.1)
Identify and recognise major security threats and their mitigation strategy. (D.1)
Explain major methodologies for secure networks and what threat they address. (D.1)
48730.learning_and_teaching_activities: This subject will be delivered as a series of workshops, interactive lectures and lab sessions. The subject relies heavily on UTS Canvas, therefore students are expected to check UTS Canvas regularly to access materials and weekly activities.Workshops will be interactive and hold discussions on important topics. Lecture notes are supported by PowerPoint slides, learning materials and strategies described in the Learning Guide.All workshops and lectures will be recorded and will be available to students after the activities in CANVAS. Students are required to read the lecture materials before the workshops in order to understand the concepts clearly. During the workshop sessions a range of questions will be discussed, linking them to the lecture contents, and helping to focus on topics that students have found most challenging. Also, during the workshop sessions, students will be invited to discuss or present their ideas.All materials given in the subject readings are examinable, even those not directly presented or discussed in lectures. Students will gain most from the class time by reading each week's preparation material in advance and clarifying any questions during the interactive workshop sessions, student consultation times and lab discussion times.During each week's lab session, students will perform a set of provided tasks. The tasks are closely related to the week's lecture topic. There will be discussion sessions during the lab where the lab instructor will pose questions related to the lab activities and students will work either individually or in groups to answer the questions by performing hands-on activities, engaging in group discussions, as well as looking through Internet resources.Students are expected to attend and participate in all lab activities, contribute to online discussions, complete assignments by their due dates and actively participate in groups for the group-based activities both in and out of class time. An average student aiming to pass the subject is expected to invest 9-10 learning hours each session week for this subject.The subject will be delivered as a 1.5 hour interactive lecture, followed by a 1.5 hour laboratory session.
48730.description: This subject equips you with vital cybersecurity skills, addressing the dual aspects of people management and technology to mitigate risks like legal repercussions, financial losses, and eroded trust. You'll develop a robust understanding of cyber-attacks, defense mechanisms, and security services—confidentiality, integrity, availability. You'll also gain hands-on experience with industry-standard technologies such as IPSec, SSL, PGP, and S/MIME. Through practical application, you'll be prepared to enhance the cybersecurity posture of contemporary organisations.

requisite.rule: Requisite rule: (((1 OR 1a OR 1b) AND (2 OR 2a OR 2b OR 2c OR 2d OR 2e)) OR (3))

items.details: Admission into MAJ03512 Biomedical Engineering Major

41180.name: Data Analytics in Cybersecurity
learning_outcomes: Apply data analytics to investigate cybersecurity datasets. (D.1)
Detect and analyse cyber‐attacks using data analytics. (D.1)

Synthesise data analytics with other techniques to appropriately set
rules for intrusion detection. (D.1)
Clearly communicate process of data analysis and security recommendations to a broad audience. (E.1)
41180.learning_and_teaching_activities: Students will on average spend 150 hours over the semester undertaking learning and assessment activities for this subject. For on campus students this includes class time as described, designated activities in the practical sessions, assessment tasks, readings and study time. For off campus students the time should be divided between online learning activities, discussion boards, designated activities in the practical sessions, assessment tasks, readings and study time.
41180.description: Data Analytics for Cybersecurity equips you with the skills to leverage big data and threat intelligence in combating both insider and external cyber threats. You will learn to apply statistical methods to discern patterns and use intelligent technologies to identify security issues from data. The subject introduces machine learning applications in cybersecurity and standard data analytics processes. Through hands-on exercises, you will develop practical skills in analysing corporate computer and network vulnerabilities, thereby enhancing your ability to identify and mitigate security risks. This subject bridges the gap between data analytics and cybersecurity, providing you with the tools to effectively protect modern digital infrastructures.

41185.name: Emerging Topics in Cybersecurity and Privacy
learning_outcomes: Examine various aspects of security systems according to new research outcomes and evolving understandings of security threats. (D.1)
Evaluate the design, implementation, configuration and application of existing security systems based on the latest IT security research outcomes. (C.1)
Synthesize literature and practically apply new research outcomes and approaches to real problems. (C.1)
Collaborate to communicate research synthesis alongside practical results. (E.1)
41185.learning_and_teaching_activities: This subject uses active learning strategies, which involves a combination of lectures, tutorials and workshops to support a research-inspired learning strategy. It also includes elements of guided self-study learning.Students will be required to review online materials, pre-readings and research literatures before taking the on-campus workshops. Within workshops, students collaboratively engage in intensive discussion and activities on selected topics or projects based on guest lectures. Students also present the current research issues and conduct practical lab in IT security independently. In a competitive group project, students will be divided into two teams. One team will perform defensive security engineering and the other will perform offensive security engineering. During the project, students will develop reflective skills to identify how they would improve both group and individual activities through UTSOnline discussion boards as required to communicate ideas and questions with peers when studying the subject.Students must attend workshop sessions and tutorials to complete the required assessment tasks. Feedback for assessment tasks will be given to students once the marking is finalised within 2-3 weeks of submission. Students will also receive continued feedback for their workshops and tutorial exercises on weekly basis. This will be administered by the tutor in the tutorial sessions where the deficiencies in the answers to problems are pointed out to students.
41185.description: This subject delves into contemporary IT security theories and practical skills in the cybersecurity profession, enhanced by guest lectures and workshops. You will develop a professional understanding of cybersecurity by examining emerging threats to web applications and systems, along with cutting-edge privacy and ethical issues. You'll explore current trends and challenges through professional practice and literature review. Working in small teams, you'll apply modern techniques for requirements elicitation, analysis, modelling, specification, and validation to real-world projects.

41183.name: Privacy Preserving
learning_outcomes: Identify and explain the most common privacy models that are used in various communities and organisations. (B.1)
Evaluate the effectiveness of existing privacy measures. (D.1)
Apply privacy preserving techniques appropriate to a variety of security configurations and scenarios. (D.1)
Work as a team to investigate a real‐world privacy breach in a corporate environment. (E.1)
41183.learning_and_teaching_activities: Students will on average spend 80 hours over the session undertaking learning and assessment activities for this subject. For on campus students this includes class time as described, designated activities in the practical sessions, assessment tasks, readings and study time. For distance students the time should be divided between online learning activities, discussion boards, designated activities in the practical sessions, assessment tasks, readings and study time.
41183.description: In this subject you'll master techniques for safeguarding data privacy, enabling organisations to handle personal or confidential data responsibly. The subject explores privacy models to build defenses against privacy breaches, delving into data anonymisation, statistical privacy approaches, and differential privacy, ensuring you can protect and manage sensitive information effectively.

41184.name: Secure Programming and Penetration Testing
learning_outcomes: Classify software security vulnerabilities in the source code of systems, applications, and services. (D.1)
Automate the analysis of a software project using source code analysis on top of compilers to defend against real‐world attacks from malicious entities. (D.1)
Communicate effectively individually and as a team to describe plans for countering changing security threats. (E.1)
41184.learning_and_teaching_activities: This subject uses active learning strategies, which involves a series of on-campus workshops and tutorials to support a hands-on and lab-focused collaborative learning activities. Students will build up their professional experience in secure programming and penetration testing throughout each workshop accomplishment.Outside class, students will be required to review online materials, pre-readings and open education resources before taking the on-campus workshops. Students will also conduct an authentic ethical hacking project in small teams through multi-staged assessments, which mainly composed of information gathering and enumeration plan report, and performance and analysis report in real testing. Students will have experiences in reflecting on and identifying how they may improve both group and individual activities through Canvas discussions boards as required to communicate ideas and questions with peers when studying the subject.Feedback for assessment tasks will be given to students two weeks after submission. Students will also receive in-class feedback for their workshops and tutorial exercises on weekly basis.
41184.description: This subject will equip you with the knowledge and skills necessary to develop secure software and assess the security of systems through source code analysis and penetration testing techniques. There will be coverage of the principles and practices of secure coding, the identification and mitigation of vulnerabilities, software verification, and the methodologies used in penetration testing. You will learn how to approach and develop automatic auditing systems, identifying vulnerabilities and developing security analysis and testing methods to protect systems against cyber-attacks. Additionally, you'll have the chance to engage in national and international security challenges, with assessments on secure programming, software analysis, and your communication skills in speaking and writing.

41182.name: System Security
learning_outcomes: Identify and explain the difference between particular types of security systems. (D.1)
Identify and analyse security vulnerabilities and issues in computer systems. (D.1)
Propose and justify technical solutions and potential remedy actions to increase security. (D.1)
Communicate with peers effectively concerning ethical hacking practice. (E.1)
41182.learning_and_teaching_activities: This subject uses active learning strategies, which involves a combination of lectures, workshops and practical sessions to support a research-inspired learning strategy. It also includes elements of guided self-study learning.Students will be required to review online materials, pre-readings and research literatures before taking the on-campus workshops. Within workshops, students present the current research issues following the standard procedure, and conduct practical lab in system security independently. In group activities, students engage in intensive discussion and activities on selected topics or projects. In assessment 2, students are grouped into defensive and offensive teams. The offensive team has to choose on a subset of the top ten vulnerabilities listed on OWASP project website, identify these vulnerabilities in systems nominated by the defensive team and document their findings. Defensive team Students will be tested on their ability to harden the system. Both teams verify experimental outcomes and to propose solutions to remedy the identified vulnerabilities.Students must attend workshop sessions and tutorials to complete the required assessment tasks. Feedback for assessment tasks will be given to students once the marking is finalised. Students will also receive continued feedback for their workshops and tutorial exercises on weekly basis. This will be administered by the tutor in the tutorial sessions where the deficiencies in the answers to problems are pointed out to students.
41182.description: This subject provides a comprehensive understanding of contemporary system security concepts, strategies, and practical techniques essential for securing operating systems, databases, and servers. You will learn to implement security measures throughout the design and configuration phases, ensuring robust system security and privacy. The subject places a strong emphasis on system hardening mechanisms and prominent security techniques, which you will apply in hands-on practical sessions. By testing the resilience of hardened systems against various attack vectors, you will gain critical skills in defending against real-world cybersecurity threats. This subject fosters both theoretical knowledge and practical expertise, preparing you to effectively safeguard corporate computing environments.

requisite.rule: Requisite rule: ((1 OR 1a OR 1b OR 1c OR 1d OR 1e OR 1f OR 1g OR 1h OR 1i))

contents.structure_details: Complete the following subject:

have_subject.name: Information Security and Management
learning_outcomes: Investigate security management issues in organisations by analysing requirements, plans and IT security policies. (B.1)
Propose appropriate information security protection mechanisms by assessing security risks, threats and vulnerabilities. (B.1)
Identify security training and education needs and associated legal and ethical awareness for organisational personnel. (B.1)
Apply organisational planning and project management principles to IT security planning individually and collaboratively. (E.1)
Demonstrate awareness on how to assess and identify cyber security issues when working with, and for, Indigenous Australians. (A.1)
have_subject.learning_and_teaching_activities: The subject has one contact hour in Week 1 and three contact hours per week in the remaining weeks.This subject involves a combination of guided self-study learning activities, lectures and workshops to support an inquiry-based learning strategy.A weekly online study module presents new material in the form of videos, written articles and open education resources. Students will be required to complete the online study module before taking the lectures and workshops.Regular lectures include interactive quizzes, discussions and reflections on the key topics and feedbacks.Within weekly two-hour workshops, students present the current security issues following the standard procedure, and conduct practical lab independently.Students must attend workshop sessions and lectures to complete the required assessment tasks. Students will receive continued feedback for their exercises and assessments on weekly basis.
have_subject.description: This subject focuses on training you in IT security policy development and human security management while addressing critical legal and ethical considerations. You will lay the groundwork for applying cyber safety and security measures at a corporate level. By conducting security assessments within business constraints, you will learn to employ professional methodologies effectively. The subject also covers the systematic evaluation of business and security operations, equipping you with skills in contingency planning, risk assessment, risk management, and compliance across diverse business environments. You'll be prepared to develop comprehensive security policies and manage human factors in cybersecurity, ensuring robust protection and ethical governance within organisations.

requisite.rule: Requisite rule: ((1 OR 1a OR 1b OR 1c OR 1d OR 1e OR 1f) AND (2 OR 2a OR 2b OR 2c))

contents.structure_details: Select 18 credit points from the following subjects:

have_subject.name: Cybersecurity
learning_outcomes: Recognise and compare major theories and principles that are used in the implementation of a secure network. (D.1)
Apply the underlying algorithms used to construct Security Protocols. (D.1)
Identify and recognise major security threats and their mitigation strategy. (D.1)
Explain major methodologies for secure networks and what threat they address. (D.1)
have_subject.learning_and_teaching_activities: This subject will be delivered as a series of workshops, interactive lectures and lab sessions. The subject relies heavily on UTS Canvas, therefore students are expected to check UTS Canvas regularly to access materials and weekly activities.Workshops will be interactive and hold discussions on important topics. Lecture notes are supported by PowerPoint slides, learning materials and strategies described in the Learning Guide.All workshops and lectures will be recorded and will be available to students after the activities in CANVAS. Students are required to read the lecture materials before the workshops in order to understand the concepts clearly. During the workshop sessions a range of questions will be discussed, linking them to the lecture contents, and helping to focus on topics that students have found most challenging. Also, during the workshop sessions, students will be invited to discuss or present their ideas.All materials given in the subject readings are examinable, even those not directly presented or discussed in lectures. Students will gain most from the class time by reading each week's preparation material in advance and clarifying any questions during the interactive workshop sessions, student consultation times and lab discussion times.During each week's lab session, students will perform a set of provided tasks. The tasks are closely related to the week's lecture topic. There will be discussion sessions during the lab where the lab instructor will pose questions related to the lab activities and students will work either individually or in groups to answer the questions by performing hands-on activities, engaging in group discussions, as well as looking through Internet resources.Students are expected to attend and participate in all lab activities, contribute to online discussions, complete assignments by their due dates and actively participate in groups for the group-based activities both in and out of class time. An average student aiming to pass the subject is expected to invest 9-10 learning hours each session week for this subject.The subject will be delivered as a 1.5 hour interactive lecture, followed by a 1.5 hour laboratory session.
have_subject.description: This subject equips you with vital cybersecurity skills, addressing the dual aspects of people management and technology to mitigate risks like legal repercussions, financial losses, and eroded trust. You'll develop a robust understanding of cyber-attacks, defense mechanisms, and security services—confidentiality, integrity, availability. You'll also gain hands-on experience with industry-standard technologies such as IPSec, SSL, PGP, and S/MIME. Through practical application, you'll be prepared to enhance the cybersecurity posture of contemporary organisations.

requisite.rule: Requisite rule: (((1 OR 1a OR 1b) AND (2 OR 2a OR 2b OR 2c OR 2d OR 2e)) OR (3))

items.details: Admission into MAJ03512 Biomedical Engineering Major

have_subject.name: Data Analytics in Cybersecurity
learning_outcomes: Apply data analytics to investigate cybersecurity datasets. (D.1)
Detect and analyse cyber‐attacks using data analytics. (D.1)

Synthesise data analytics with other techniques to appropriately set
rules for intrusion detection. (D.1)
Clearly communicate process of data analysis and security recommendations to a broad audience. (E.1)
have_subject.learning_and_teaching_activities: Students will on average spend 150 hours over the semester undertaking learning and assessment activities for this subject. For on campus students this includes class time as described, designated activities in the practical sessions, assessment tasks, readings and study time. For off campus students the time should be divided between online learning activities, discussion boards, designated activities in the practical sessions, assessment tasks, readings and study time.
have_subject.description: Data Analytics for Cybersecurity equips you with the skills to leverage big data and threat intelligence in combating both insider and external cyber threats. You will learn to apply statistical methods to discern patterns and use intelligent technologies to identify security issues from data. The subject introduces machine learning applications in cybersecurity and standard data analytics processes. Through hands-on exercises, you will develop practical skills in analysing corporate computer and network vulnerabilities, thereby enhancing your ability to identify and mitigate security risks. This subject bridges the gap between data analytics and cybersecurity, providing you with the tools to effectively protect modern digital infrastructures.

have_subject.name: Emerging Topics in Cybersecurity and Privacy
learning_outcomes: Examine various aspects of security systems according to new research outcomes and evolving understandings of security threats. (D.1)
Evaluate the design, implementation, configuration and application of existing security systems based on the latest IT security research outcomes. (C.1)
Synthesize literature and practically apply new research outcomes and approaches to real problems. (C.1)
Collaborate to communicate research synthesis alongside practical results. (E.1)
have_subject.learning_and_teaching_activities: This subject uses active learning strategies, which involves a combination of lectures, tutorials and workshops to support a research-inspired learning strategy. It also includes elements of guided self-study learning.Students will be required to review online materials, pre-readings and research literatures before taking the on-campus workshops. Within workshops, students collaboratively engage in intensive discussion and activities on selected topics or projects based on guest lectures. Students also present the current research issues and conduct practical lab in IT security independently. In a competitive group project, students will be divided into two teams. One team will perform defensive security engineering and the other will perform offensive security engineering. During the project, students will develop reflective skills to identify how they would improve both group and individual activities through UTSOnline discussion boards as required to communicate ideas and questions with peers when studying the subject.Students must attend workshop sessions and tutorials to complete the required assessment tasks. Feedback for assessment tasks will be given to students once the marking is finalised within 2-3 weeks of submission. Students will also receive continued feedback for their workshops and tutorial exercises on weekly basis. This will be administered by the tutor in the tutorial sessions where the deficiencies in the answers to problems are pointed out to students.
have_subject.description: This subject delves into contemporary IT security theories and practical skills in the cybersecurity profession, enhanced by guest lectures and workshops. You will develop a professional understanding of cybersecurity by examining emerging threats to web applications and systems, along with cutting-edge privacy and ethical issues. You'll explore current trends and challenges through professional practice and literature review. Working in small teams, you'll apply modern techniques for requirements elicitation, analysis, modelling, specification, and validation to real-world projects.

have_subject.name: Privacy Preserving
learning_outcomes: Identify and explain the most common privacy models that are used in various communities and organisations. (B.1)
Evaluate the effectiveness of existing privacy measures. (D.1)
Apply privacy preserving techniques appropriate to a variety of security configurations and scenarios. (D.1)
Work as a team to investigate a real‐world privacy breach in a corporate environment. (E.1)
have_subject.learning_and_teaching_activities: Students will on average spend 80 hours over the session undertaking learning and assessment activities for this subject. For on campus students this includes class time as described, designated activities in the practical sessions, assessment tasks, readings and study time. For distance students the time should be divided between online learning activities, discussion boards, designated activities in the practical sessions, assessment tasks, readings and study time.
have_subject.description: In this subject you'll master techniques for safeguarding data privacy, enabling organisations to handle personal or confidential data responsibly. The subject explores privacy models to build defenses against privacy breaches, delving into data anonymisation, statistical privacy approaches, and differential privacy, ensuring you can protect and manage sensitive information effectively.

have_subject.name: Secure Programming and Penetration Testing
learning_outcomes: Classify software security vulnerabilities in the source code of systems, applications, and services. (D.1)
Automate the analysis of a software project using source code analysis on top of compilers to defend against real‐world attacks from malicious entities. (D.1)
Communicate effectively individually and as a team to describe plans for countering changing security threats. (E.1)
have_subject.learning_and_teaching_activities: This subject uses active learning strategies, which involves a series of on-campus workshops and tutorials to support a hands-on and lab-focused collaborative learning activities. Students will build up their professional experience in secure programming and penetration testing throughout each workshop accomplishment.Outside class, students will be required to review online materials, pre-readings and open education resources before taking the on-campus workshops. Students will also conduct an authentic ethical hacking project in small teams through multi-staged assessments, which mainly composed of information gathering and enumeration plan report, and performance and analysis report in real testing. Students will have experiences in reflecting on and identifying how they may improve both group and individual activities through Canvas discussions boards as required to communicate ideas and questions with peers when studying the subject.Feedback for assessment tasks will be given to students two weeks after submission. Students will also receive in-class feedback for their workshops and tutorial exercises on weekly basis.
have_subject.description: This subject will equip you with the knowledge and skills necessary to develop secure software and assess the security of systems through source code analysis and penetration testing techniques. There will be coverage of the principles and practices of secure coding, the identification and mitigation of vulnerabilities, software verification, and the methodologies used in penetration testing. You will learn how to approach and develop automatic auditing systems, identifying vulnerabilities and developing security analysis and testing methods to protect systems against cyber-attacks. Additionally, you'll have the chance to engage in national and international security challenges, with assessments on secure programming, software analysis, and your communication skills in speaking and writing.

learning_outcomes: Identify and explain the difference between particular types of security systems. (D.1)
Identify and analyse security vulnerabilities and issues in computer systems. (D.1)
Propose and justify technical solutions and potential remedy actions to increase security. (D.1)
Communicate with peers effectively concerning ethical hacking practice. (E.1)
have_subject.learning_and_teaching_activities: This subject uses active learning strategies, which involves a combination of lectures, workshops and practical sessions to support a research-inspired learning strategy. It also includes elements of guided self-study learning.Students will be required to review online materials, pre-readings and research literatures before taking the on-campus workshops. Within workshops, students present the current research issues following the standard procedure, and conduct practical lab in system security independently. In group activities, students engage in intensive discussion and activities on selected topics or projects. In assessment 2, students are grouped into defensive and offensive teams. The offensive team has to choose on a subset of the top ten vulnerabilities listed on OWASP project website, identify these vulnerabilities in systems nominated by the defensive team and document their findings. Defensive team Students will be tested on their ability to harden the system. Both teams verify experimental outcomes and to propose solutions to remedy the identified vulnerabilities.Students must attend workshop sessions and tutorials to complete the required assessment tasks. Feedback for assessment tasks will be given to students once the marking is finalised. Students will also receive continued feedback for their workshops and tutorial exercises on weekly basis. This will be administered by the tutor in the tutorial sessions where the deficiencies in the answers to problems are pointed out to students.
have_subject.description: This subject provides a comprehensive understanding of contemporary system security concepts, strategies, and practical techniques essential for securing operating systems, databases, and servers. You will learn to implement security measures throughout the design and configuration phases, ensuring robust system security and privacy. The subject places a strong emphasis on system hardening mechanisms and prominent security techniques, which you will apply in hands-on practical sessions. By testing the resilience of hardened systems against various attack vectors, you will gain critical skills in defending against real-world cybersecurity threats. This subject fosters both theoretical knowledge and practical expertise, preparing you to effectively safeguard corporate computing environments.

requisite.rule: Requisite rule: ((1 OR 1a OR 1b OR 1c OR 1d OR 1e OR 1f OR 1g OR 1h OR 1i))

48080.name: Introduction to Innovation
learning_outcomes: Identify opportunities for innovation through analysis of current market or industry. (B.1)
Design a new product or service proposal to meet contemporary opportunities in market or industry. (C.1)
Use analysis techniques and business plan to assess viability of new product or service. (D.1)
Communicate with stakeholders on a new product or service using relevant documentation. (E.1)
48080.learning_and_teaching_activities: Student learning is organised as a weekly 3-hour active learning experience which is divided into 2 parts: Part 1, at the beginning of each class, students will be introduced with different sets of concepts and theory with examples and case studies and shown the link with the assessments. In Part 2, students will have opportunities to apply concepts and theory to the chosen real-world area. Conversations, reflections, debates and discussions will be part of a collaborative problem formulation and solving exercise. Students will receive feedback weekly from their educators and their peers. The feedback will be critical for the progress and completion of their assessments.
48080.description: This subject equips you with an understanding of innovation, commercialisation, technology life cycles, and change in engineering and technology. It prepares you for the commercial world by developing your understanding of different roles within an enterprise through a business planning framework. Covering strategy, marketing, operations, personnel, and finance, it offers a practical orientation with contemporary case studies to illustrate concept application. As you approach the end of your undergraduate studies, this knowledge will enable you to communicate effectively with various organisational functions.

requisite.rule: Requisite rule: ((1 OR 1a OR 1b OR 1c OR 1d OR 1e))

items.details: Must have completed at least 120 credit points in  Bachelor's Degree owned by FEIT
Must have completed at least 120 credit points in  Bachelor's Honours Embedded owned by FEIT
Must have completed at least 120 credit points in  Bachelor's Combined Degree owned by FEIT
Must have completed at least 120 credit points in  Bachelor's Combined Honours owned by FEIT
Must have completed at least 120 credit points in  Bachelor's Combined Honours co-owned by FEIT
Must have completed at least 120 credit points in  Bachelor's Combined Degree co-owned by FEIT

81529.name: Futuring for Innovation with Impact
learning_outcomes: Analyse real-world innovation contexts and cases in order to discern connections and patterns that are supportive of emergence of ideas, innovation and change.
Identify issues of interest or concern, and apply a range of disciplinary and professional practice approaches to examine the potential for action.
Analyse trends observed in real-world contexts and creatively combine futuring methods to speculate about future developments.
Apply appropriate methods to conduct enquiry into real-world challenges to identify their constitutive elements and connections situated in specific contexts.
Evaluate the alternatives and articulate a compelling argument for a proposed direction for innovation in response to a real-world challenge.
81529.learning_and_teaching_activities: This subject will consist of six full days scheduled over 3 weeks on Mondays and Thursdays between 10am-4pm. Students will then have tasks to complete in the days between as well as three assessments to write and complete.Learning will take place in a collaborative, immersive, experiential, studio-based environment. Students will work with academics and professionals across a wide range of disciplines.
81529.description: In this subject, you will think beyond the world as it stands today and consider the different futures that may impact your personal innovation practice towards sustainability. You will learn critical research skills and futuring methods to identify trends that shape future worlds. You will explore your own innovative area of interest and assess how possible futures may impact or inform your innovation. This futuring process will leverage qualitative data to identify barriers and enablers to change, to support the design your innovations to have an impact and make a difference towards sustainable development. This subject is hands-on and will broaden your horizon.

items.details: 94657 Innovation Futures: Thinking Beyond What Is

81546.name: Innovation in Complex Systems
learning_outcomes: Explore and represent complex systems and their interrelationships.
Manipulate ideas and findings from diverse perspectives to explore a range of challenges and identify opportunities.
Select, test and evaluate different disciplinary methods for gaining insights into a complex system.
Justify the selection and interpretation of ideas and strategies in collaborative environments.
81546.learning_and_teaching_activities: Learning in this subject will take place in a collaborative, immersive, experiential learning environment. This subject uses problem-based learning strategies that involves students in researching and developing their own / group solutions to complex problems / scenarios. This subject is studio-based. In each studio session students receive feedback and reflection from academics and industry professionals, whilst continuing to work on the problems / feedback and reflection from academics and industry professionals, and continuing to work on the problems / projects they are preparing and completing outside studio session times. Staff, peers and invited experts will give formative feedback continually through class activities as students develop their future-oriented projects.
81546.description: The challenges that we face today have become increasingly complex and connected. In this subject, you will explore how creative intelligence and innovation methods can help us better understand complex systems and make a real difference. The subject will train you in a range of techniques, such as prototyping, visualisations and innovation models. Working on real-world challenges with a hands-on approach, you will gain a range of practical skills to look at complex challenges with a fresh perspective. Thereby you will learn how to be confident with uncertainty and how to turn complex problems into emerging opportunities.

81547.name: Leading Creative Innovation
learning_outcomes: Analyse, create and apply various techniques and methods for understanding, visualising, interpreting and investigating complex problems.
Participate collaboratively in teams and engage with the needs of various and diverse stakeholders.
Test and articulate the value of different methods for exploring, understanding and addressing complex challenges in creative and useful ways.
Explore, ideate and communicate using a range of methods and techniques from different disciplines and professional practices.
Work within a professional context to design and develop ideas, strategies and practices for betterment.
81547.learning_and_teaching_activities: This subject will consist of six full days on campus scheduled over 3 weeks, supplemented by online resources and activities.Learning will take place in a collaborative, experiential, studio-based environment. Students will work with academics and professionals across a range of disciplines and undertake a project set in a real life context to explore the use of technology and methods in creative practice.
81547.description: In this subject, you will learn to lead innovative initiatives by collaborating with diverse individuals and embracing unconventional thinking to nurture ideas. You'll discover how to generate breakthrough opportunities and foresee future possibilities. Through practical application, you'll acquire skills in creative practices, research methods, and possibilities thinking to enhance your  innovation journey. The subject equips you with leadership skills essential for co-designing innovative projects.

99218.name: Intercultural Communication
learning_outcomes: Critically engage with intercultural communication theories and frameworks and apply these theories to analyse diverse cultural situations and spaces
Reflect on own perceptions of intercultural spaces and encounters and intercultural skills development for professional and everyday contexts
Design and conduct independent, ethical, small scale research about aspects of interculturality
Recognise the ongoing impact of colonialism on and positive contributions of Indigenous cultures in intercultural spaces
Communicate effectively and appropriately in written and oral academic English
99218.learning_and_teaching_activities: Classes will incorporate a range of teaching and learning strategies including presentations, videos, online learning materials, reflective surveys, discussion of readings, international case studies and group activities. These will be complemented by independent student research, readings and participation in online discussion forums.Feedback on assessment tasks will be provided in class, through quizzes and via GradeBook in Canvas.
99218.description: This subject provides an introduction to the core concepts and debates in the interdisciplinary field of intercultural communication. It is designed to help prepare students for the interpersonal and dynamic nature of working in increasingly globalised workplaces and intercultural environments. Using a problem-based approach, students learn to critically examine numerous frameworks and theories to interpret intercultural encounters in praxis-based scenarios. The applicability of specific strategies and approaches in intercultural communication are also examined for their relevance to industry, communities and in society more broadly.

requisite.rule: Requisite rule: ((1 OR 1a OR 1b OR 1c OR 1d OR 1e OR 1f OR 1g OR 1h OR 1i OR 1j OR 1k))

976111.name: Contemporary China
learning_outcomes: Reflect on and analyse contemporary China in its historical, political, cultural, social, and economic dimensions.
Employ approaches and frameworks to engage in historical, political, cultural, social and economic inquiry.
Apply knowledges of China ethically in their reflective and inquiry-based works.
Demonstrate critical problem-solving and research-led analytical skills in the context of China.
Communicate effectively in English the findings of independent inquiries and reflections in written form.
Maintain good academic practice, observing ethics and referencing standards.
976111.learning_and_teaching_activities: Contemporary China combines lectures, tutorials, collaborative in-class learning, self-study and individual research.Lectures provide students with an overview of key information, theoretical concepts, and historical context. Assigned readings and in-class activities encourage students to reflect on and engage with the class material, providing students with the training and skills required to complete the assessment tasks.Before each tutorial, students are expected to have completed all of the preparation activities assigned. Online lectures and guiding questions help students to focus on the most important aspects of these activities. Students must come to class prepared to discuss the material and raise questions.Tutorials consist of whole class and small group discussions that extend the preparatory learning. Tutorials focus on development of student understanding and provide formative feedback for assessment tasks. Tutorial activities focus on developing the analytical and literacy skills required for this subject and subsequent study.Students receive peer and academic feedback on an initial journal entry early in the session and will have the opportunity to workshop their topic and initial ideas for the literature review.
976111.description: This subject provides an overview of contemporary China and its history, culture, politics, society and economy. You will learn to identify and analyse current issues from multiple perspectives using theory and methods for intercultural research. The subject combines lectures with tutorials and independent work to develop your analytical and problem-solving abilities in China studies, with a focus on cultural diversity, social and economic change, and political transformation. You will learn to develop comparative and ethical approaches for critical thinking and verbal and written communication skills, reflecting the subject's multidisciplinary approach. Topics include cities, gender, home and work, identity, governance, media, the economy and China's international relationships.

97101.name: Chinese Language and Culture 1
learning_outcomes: Comprehend and respond to basic texts in oral and written Chinese.
Produce basic texts in oral and written Chinese.
Demonstrate intercultural awareness across two or more cultural contexts.
97101.learning_and_teaching_activities: This subject uses a communicative approach in class to maximise students' potential for effective communication in the target language. A series of activities such as interactive activities working in pairs, small and large groups and/or presentations, are promoted in class to enhance students' engagement through content-based learning. Preparation for class activities requires students to complete exercises and reading/listening/viewing activities prior to class to make effective use of resources and subject material (including resources online), community and peer resources available to them to support their learning.Students are encouraged to customise their learning experience as per the Global Chinese approach to promote their engagement with Chinese peoples and societies. This entails keeping in mind their goals for the use of Chinese (for instance studying abroad or travel in a particular country or region) and tailoring their practice and learning to maximise exposure to local variants of the language, relevant cultural norms and authentic materials from the area.As part of an immersive approach to language education, students are encouraged to use the target language within the classroom environment and to build confidence to use the target language outside the classroom. The approximate time commitment expected from students is 12 hours per week, including class time.
97101.description: This subject is designed to provide students who have no prior knowledge of the Chinese language with basic reading, listening, speaking and writing skills. You'll learn Pinyin for pronunciation and acquire your vocabulary by around 200 words. Through active participation in collaborative activities, you'll develop intercultural competence, learning to navigate between cultures.Your learning will involve a communicative approach, engaging in interactive tasks and activities to enhance language proficiency. You will be expected to prepare for classes through various exercises and utilise online and peer resources. The language learning approach will focus on linguistic and cultural practices across the Chinese world.You'll be expected to commit to approximately 12 hours of weekly study, including class time, and immerse yourself in Chinese both inside and outside the classroom to build confidence and fluency.

97109.name: Communicating About Chinese Media
learning_outcomes: Develop awareness of register, rhetoric, genre, concept and specialist vocabulary through exposure to a wide variety of media and academic readings
Communicate professionally and academically in writing and speech in Chinese through competent use of specific linguistic skills and devices
Reflect on and analyse critically the impact of social issues as seen from media sources on the Chinese society and its people.
97109.learning_and_teaching_activities: The subject utilises flipped and hybrid teaching and learning strategies and both online and face-to-face delivery modes. Students use online and face-to-face class time for interactive practice and advancement of linguistic skills through personalised learning and group discussions, practices and/or presentations in flexible but guided and monitored physical or virtual classroom activities. Students also deepen their understanding of professional practices, theories and concepts and clarify subject content.Preparation for these activities requires students to complete exercises and reading/listening/viewing activities prior to coming to class and for them to make effective use of subject materials (including those posted online), community and peer resources available to them to support their learning.Students will customise their learning experience by engaging with Chinese media and their language, culture and practices from intercultural perspectives, and tailoring their learning experience to maximise understanding and appreciation of Chinese history, culture and society.Students are encouraged to explore subject materials and concepts through independent research, creative outputs and/or close textual analysis.
97109.description: This subject equips you with advanced Chinese linguistic skills, preparing you for careers in Chinese-language media and critical reflection on media practices. You'll master crafting nuanced texts, employing specialised vocabulary and rhetoric, and analysing contemporary China through diverse media sources. Learning combines online and in-person methods, including interactive practice and group work, with a focus on personalised and collaborative learning. Prior to classes, you'll engage with preparatory exercises and materials to enhance in-class discussions and presentations. You'll also tailor your studies to your interests, exploring Chinese media, culture, and society through independent research and creative analysis, fostering a deep intercultural understanding.

learning_outcomes: Comprehend and respond to complex texts in oral and written Chinese.
Produce complex texts in oral and written Chinese.
Demonstrate intercultural awareness across two or more cultural contexts.

You'll be expected to prepare for classes through various exercises and analyse a variety of authentic sources in a manner that considers their cultural provenance and role. You’ll explore subject materials and concepts through independent research, creative outputs and/or close textual analysis. The language learning approach will focus on linguistic and cultural practices across the Chinese world.You’ll be expected to commit to approximately 12 hours of weekly study, including class time, and immerse yourself in Chinese both inside and outside the classroom to build confidence and fluency.

97112.name: Language in Chinese Screen Studies
learning_outcomes: Develop awareness of register, rhetoric, genre, concept and specialist vocabulary through exposure to contemporary Chinese movies and a wide variety of academic and professional readings
Communicate professionally and academically in writing and speech in Chinese through competent use of specific linguistic skills and devices
Analyse cinematic representation of Chinese social, cultural and political themes;
Develop basic writing skills to prepare a movie synopsis, storyboard, screen play and film reviews
97112.learning_and_teaching_activities: The subject utilises flipped and hybrid teaching and learning strategies and both online and face-to-face delivery modes. Students use online and face-to-face class time for interactive practice and advancement of linguistic skills through personalised learning and group discussions, practices and/or presentations in flexible but guided and monitored physical or virtual classroom activities. 97112.description: This subject equips you with advanced Modern Standard Chinese skills, focusing on specialised vocabulary and linguistic nuances relevant to film analysis. You'll gain proficiency in evaluating Chinese cinema within its socio-economic milieu, mastering technical language in both Chinese and English to critique films and script your mini screenplays. Learning is interactive, blending online and in-person sessions for practice and discussion, while independent study and resource utilisation enhance your grasp of professional practices and theoretical concepts. You'll tailor your experience by exploring the Chinese film industry's cultural context, fostering a deeper appreciation of China's heritage and societal norms through independent research and creative engagement.

97116.name: Chinese Business and Administrative Language
learning_outcomes: Select and deploy register, rhetoric, genre, concept and specialist vocabulary through exposure to business and administrative writings of a wide range of genres and purposes
Communicate professionally in writing and speech in Chinese through competent use of specific and advanced linguistic skills and devices
Develop awareness of when to deploy various Chinese and business cross-cultural communication practices
97116.learning_and_teaching_activities: The subject utilises flipped and hybrid teaching and learning strategies and both online and face-to-face delivery modes. Students use online and face-to-face class time for interactive practice and advancement of linguistic skills through personalised learning and group discussions, practices and/or presentations in flexible but guided and monitored physical or virtual classroom activities. Students also deepen their understanding of professional practices, theories and concepts and clarify subject content.Preparation for these activities requires students to complete exercises and reading/listening/viewing activities prior to coming to class and for them to make effective use of subject materials (including those posted online), community and peer resources available to them to support their learning.Students will customise their learning experience by engaging with Chinese business and professional language and practices from intercultural perspectives, and tailoring their practice and learning to maximise understanding and appreciation of Chinese business and administrative systems, customs and common practices.Students are encouraged to explore subject materials and concepts through independent research, creative outputs and/or close textual analysis.
97116.description: This subject equips you with specialised proficiency in Modern Standard Chinese, focusing on business and administrative communication. You'll master formal writing styles, expand your business vocabulary, and enhance speaking skills through practical assessments, preparing you for cross-cultural business environments. Learning combines online and in-person methods, including interactive practice and group work, to advance your linguistic abilities. You'll engage with Chinese business language and practices, customising your learning to understand and appreciate the nuances of Chinese professional settings. Independent research and creative analysis further deepen your knowledge, making you more culturally aware and employable internationally.

976411.name: Contemporary France
learning_outcomes: Reflect on and analyse contemporary France in its historical, political, cultural, social, and economic dimensions.
Employ approaches and frameworks to engage in historical, political, cultural, social and economic inquiry.
Apply knowledges of France ethically in their reflective and inquiry-based works.
Demonstrate critical problem-solving and research-led analytical skills in the context of France.
Communicate effectively in English the findings of independent inquiries and reflections in written form.
Maintain good academic practice, observing ethics and referencing standards.
976411.learning_and_teaching_activities: Contemporary France combines lectures, tutorials, collaborative in-class learning, self-study and individual research.Lectures provide students with an overview of important theoretical concepts and historical context. Assigned readings and in-class activities are designed for students to engage with and reflect on these concepts and contexts. Tutorials also provide the training and skills required to complete the assessment tasks: visual analysis, critical reflection, annotated bibliographies, literature reviews and scoping a research topic.Contemporary France requires student engagement in and outside of class. Multimedia resources will be provided to students in tutorials and online, and these resources can be used to complete assessment tasks where appropriate. Students are encouraged to apply critical thinking skills to their weekly reflections and assessment tasks. Contemporary France also allows students a good degree of flexibility to choose their own topic for their literature reviews in consultation with their tutor based on their individual interests and disciplinary background.
976411.description: This subject blends lectures and independent and collaborative learning to provide a foundational understanding of contemporary French society, politics and culture. You'll develop critical thinking and intercultural research skills. In weeks 2 and 12 you'll follow an umbrella programme shared by all European contemporary society subjects, exploring identity, divisions and crises in Europe. Between weeks 3 and 11 you'll explore France's historical evolution and the concept of French identity, with special focus on France's 'revolutionary' spirit, the importance of its Republican values, and its relations with former French colonies. These elements are crucial for understanding France's place in the modern world and the social and political issues shaping contemporary French society.

97401.name: French Language and Culture 1
learning_outcomes: Comprehend and respond to simple texts in oral and written French.
Produce simple texts in oral and written French.
Demonstrate intercultural awareness across two or more cultural contexts.
97401.learning_and_teaching_activities: This subject uses a communicative approach in class to maximise students' potential for effective communication in the target language. A series of activities such as interactive activities working in pairs, small and large groups and/or presentations, are promoted in class to enhance students' engagement through content-based learning. Preparation for class activities requires students to complete exercises and reading/listening/viewing activities prior to class to make effective use of resources and subject material (including resources online), community and peer resources available to them to support their learning.Students are encouraged to customise their learning experience as per the Global French approach to promote their engagement with francophone peoples and societies. This entails keeping in mind their goals for the use of French (for instance studying abroad or travel in a particular country or region) and tailoring their practice and learning to maximize exposure to local variants of the language, relevant cultural norms and authentic materials from the area.As part of an immersive approach to language education, students are encouraged to use the target language within the classroom environment and to build confidence to use the target language outside the classroom. The approximate time commitment expected from students is 12 hours per week, including class time.
97401.description: This subject is designed to provide students who have no prior knowledge of the French language with basic reading, listening, speaking and writing skills. It corresponds to Level A1 (basic user) of the Common European Framework of Reference (CEFR) for Languages. Through active participation in collaborative activities, you'll develop intercultural competence, learning to navigate between cultures.Your learning will involve a communicative approach, engaging in interactive tasks and activities to enhance language proficiency. You'll be expected to prepare for classes through various exercises and utilise online and peer resources. The language learning approach will focus on linguistic and cultural practices across the francophone world.You’ll be expected to commit to approximately 12 hours of weekly study, including class time, and immerse yourself in French both inside and outside the classroom to build confidence and fluency.

97407.name: Francophone Identities in Conflict
learning_outcomes: Comprehend and respond to complex texts in oral and written French.
Produce complex texts in oral and written French.
Demonstrate intercultural awareness across two or more cultural contexts.
97407.learning_and_teaching_activities: Class time is used for interactive practice and advancement of skills through work in pairs, small and large groups and/or presentations, as well as clarification of subject content. Students will spend a substantial portion of each lesson in dialogue with their peers and their teacher.Preparation for these activities requires students to complete exercises and reading/listening/viewing activities prior to coming to class and for them to make effective use of subject materials (including UTS Online), community and peer resources available to them to support their learning. As this is an 8 credit point subject, the approximate time commitment expected from students is 12 hours per week, including class time.Students will customise their learning experience as per the Global French approach to promote their engagement with francophone peoples and societies. This entails keeping in mind one's goals for the use of French (for instance studying abroad or travel in a particular country or region) and tailoring one's practice and learning to maximise exposure to local variants of the language, relevant cultural norms and authentic materials from the area.Students are strongly encouraged to prioritise the use of French when communicating with each other or with their teacher in class.Students are encouraged to explore subject materials and concepts through independent research, creative outputs and/or close textual analysis.
97407.description: This subject is designed to provide students who are proficient in reading, listening, speaking and writing in French with opportunities to engage with francophone culture. It corresponds to Level B2/C1 (independent/proficient user) of the Common European Framework of Reference (CEFR) for Languages. You’ll examine and engage with the sociocultural contexts in which the language is used, specifically with regard to questions of history and conflict in the francophone world.Via written, oral and aural assessments that demonstrate continuing mastery of the French language, your learning will involve a communicative approach, engaging in interactive tasks and using a variety of resources to enhance language proficiency. You'll be expected to prepare for classes through various exercises and analyse a variety of authentic sources in a manner that considers their cultural provenance and role. You’ll explore subject materials and concepts through independent research, creative outputs and/or close textual analysis. The language learning approach will focus on linguistic and cultural practices across the francophone world.You’ll be expected to commit to approximately 12 hours of weekly study, including class time, and immerse yourself in French both inside and outside the classroom to build confidence and fluency.

976421.name: Contemporary Germany
learning_outcomes: Reflect on and analyse contemporary Germany in its historical, political, cultural, social, and economic dimensions.
Employ approaches and frameworks to engage in historical, political, cultural, social and economic inquiry.
Apply knowledges of Germany ethically in their reflective and inquiry-based works.
Demonstrate critical problem-solving and research-led analytical skills in the context of Germany.
Communicate effectively in English the findings of independent inquiries and reflections in written form.
Maintain good academic practice, observing ethics and referencing standards.
976421.learning_and_teaching_activities: Contemporary Germany combines lectures, tutorials, collaborative in-class learning, reflection and individual research.Lectures provide students with an overview of key theoretical concepts and historical context. Assigned readings and in-class activities are designed for students to engage with and reflect on these concepts and contexts. Tutorials also provide the training and skills required to complete the assessment tasks: visual analysis, critical reflection, annotated bibliographies, essays and scoping a research topic.Contemporary Germany requires student engagement in and outside of class. Multimedia resources are provided to students in tutorials and online, and these resources can be used to complete assessment tasks where appropriate. Students are encouraged to apply critical thinking skills to their weekly reflections and assessment tasks. Contemporary Germany also allows students a good degree of flexibility to choose their own topic for their essays in consultation with their tutor based on their individual interests and disciplinary background.Formative feedback on students' reflective writing will be provided in the week 3 tutorial. Extensive feedback will be provided on students’ Essay Proposal, giving students the opportunity to refine their Essay topic based on tutor feedback.
976421.description: This subject blends lectures, independent and collaborative learning to provide a foundational understanding of contemporary German history, politics, society, and culture. You'll develop critical thinking and intercultural research skills, essential for advanced In-country Studies. In weeks 2 and 12 you'll follow an umbrella program shared by all contemporary European society subjects, exploring its identity, divisions, and crises. Between weeks 3 and 11, you'll focus on Germany's post-World War II era, examining its divided past, reunification, and current challenges, including identity, social movements, economic growth, immigration, and international relations, alongside language issues and popular culture. These elements are crucial for understanding Germany's place in the modern world. German popular culture is dealt with throughout.

requisite.rule: Requisite rule: ((1 AND 2) OR ((3 OR 3a OR 3b OR 3c)) OR ((4 OR 4a) AND (5 OR 5a OR 5b OR 5c OR 5d OR 5e OR 5f OR 5g OR 5h OR 5i OR 5j OR 5k)) OR (6) OR ((7 OR 7a OR 7b OR 7c)))
