976431.name: Contemporary Italy
learning_outcomes: Reflect on and analyse contemporary Italy in its historical, political, cultural, social, and economic dimensions.
Employ approaches and frameworks to engage in historical, political, cultural, social and economic inquiry.
Apply knowledges of Italy ethically in their reflective and inquiry-based works.
Demonstrate critical problem-solving and research-led analytical skills in the context of Italy.
Communicate effectively in English the findings of independent inquiries and reflections in written form.
Maintain good academic practice, observing ethics and referencing standards.
976431.learning_and_teaching_activities: Contemporary Italy combines lectures, tutorials, collaborative learning, reflection and individual research. Lectures provide students with an overview of key theoretical concepts and historical context. Assigned readings and in-class activities are designed for students to engage with and reflect on these concepts and contexts. Tutorials also provide the training and skills required to complete the assessment tasks: visual analysis, critical reflection, essays and scoping a research topic. Contemporary Italy requires student engagement in and outside of sessions. Multimedia resources are provided to students in tutorials and online, and these resources can be used to complete assessment tasks where appropriate. Students are encouraged to apply critical thinking skills to their weekly reflections and assessment tasks. Contemporary Italy also allows students a good degree of flexibility to choose their own topic for their essays in consultation with their tutor based on their individual interests and disciplinary background. Formative feedback on students' reflective writing will be provided in the Week 3 tutorial.
976431.description: This subject blends lectures, independent and collaborative learning to provide a foundational understanding of contemporary Italy's history and culture. Through this subject, you'll develop critical thinking and intercultural research skills. In weeks 2 and 12 you'll follow an umbrella program shared by all contemporary European society subjects, exploring its identity, divisions, and crises. Between weeks 3 and 11, you'll focus on Italy, its evolution from Unification and the Fascist era to post-war economic growth and recent sociopolitical changes, with a special emphasis on the roles of migration and multilingualism in shaping Italian culture and identity.

97801.learning_and_teaching_activities: This subject uses a communicative approach to maximise students’ potential for effective communication in the target language. A series of activities, including presentations and interactive activities where students work in pairs and small or large groups, are promoted to enhance students’ engagement through content-based learning. Students are required to complete exercises and reading/listening/viewing activities prior to each session to make effective use of the resources and subject materials (including online resources and community and peer resources) available to them to support their learning.Students are encouraged to customise their learning experience to promote their engagement with Italian-speaking peoples and societies. This entails keeping in mind their goals for the use of Italian (for instance: studying abroad; travelling in a particular country or region) and tailoring their practice and learning to maximise exposure to local variants of the language, relevant cultural norms, and authentic materials from the area.As part of an immersive approach to language education, students are encouraged to use the target language within the sessions and to build their confidence in using the target language. The approximate time commitment expected from students is 12 hours per week, including session time.

The language learning approach will focus on linguistic and cultural practices across the italophone world. You’ll be expected to commit to approximately 12 hours of weekly study, including class time, and immerse yourself in Italian both inside and outside the classroom to build confidence and fluency.

97805.name: Italy and Migration
learning_outcomes: Comprehend, reflect upon and analyse intermediate texts in spoken and written Italian.
Produce intermediate texts in spoken and written Italian.
Demonstrate intercultural awareness across two or more cultural contexts.
97805.learning_and_teaching_activities: Italy and Migration proposes an innovative blended, practice-based and research-inspired learning experience. Primary and secondary sources are analysed through a communicative and reflective approach, aimed at stimulating progressive and continuous learning, as well as reflecting on the use of language in context. The analyses are developed through engagement with a series of authentic intermedial materials (such as films, poetry, short stories, narratives, maps) and scaffolded through the discussion of these materials in real-life scenarios and situations (debates, role-plays, interviews). The subject’s structure favours the interaction of physical and virtual learning environments as well as the construction of a nuanced and personalised learning experience.In addition to the in-class work, students are asked to complete a series of online activities, which strengthen their independent learning skills and facilitate a customised learning experience. The approximate time commitment expected from students is 12 hours per week, including class time.
97805.description: This subject equips you with an understanding of Italian migratory dynamics, enhancing your Italian language skills. It corresponds to Level B1+ of the Common European Framework of Reference for Languages (CEFR). You'll learn to analyse and discuss Italy's migration stories through engaging with texts, movies, audio and visual materials. Materials are explored through a communicative and reflective approach, aimed at stimulating progressive and continuous learning, as well as reflecting on the use of language in context. Your learning journey involves active participation in collaborative exercises. Expect to dedicate around 12 hours weekly, including independent online activities, to develop a tailored and comprehensive learning experience.

The analysis is supported by series of supplementary authentic intermedial materials (podcasts, images, videos) and scaffolded through the use of real-life scenarios and situations (debates, role-plays, interviews). Italian Language and the Arts is structured so to favour the interaction within a virtual learning environment as well as the construction of a nuanced and personalised learning experience. In addition to the sessions, students are asked to complete a series of on-line activities, which strengthen their independent learning skills and facilitate a customised learning experience. The approximate time commitment expected from students is 12 hours per week, including sessions.
97808.description: This subject equips you with a deep understanding of visual and artistic cultures in Italy, enhancing your Italian language skills. It corresponds to Level B1+ of the Common European Framework of Reference for Languages (CEFR). You'll learn to analyse and discuss artworks, connecting them to their socio-cultural contexts. Materials are explored through a communicative and reflective approach, aimed at stimulating progressive and continuous learning, as well as reflecting on the use of language in context. Your learning journey involves active participation in collaborative exercises. Expect to dedicate around 12 hours weekly, including independent online activities, to develop a tailored and comprehensive learning experience.

976211.name: Contemporary Japan
learning_outcomes: Reflect on and analyse contemporary Japan in its historical, political, cultural, social, and economic dimensions.
Employ approaches and frameworks to engage in historical, political, cultural, social and economic inquiry.
Apply knowledges of Japan ethically in their reflective and inquiry-based works.
Demonstrate critical problem-solving and research-led analytical skills in the context of Japan.
Communicate effectively in English the findings of independent inquiries and reflections in written form.
Maintain good academic practice, observing ethics and referencing standards.
976211.learning_and_teaching_activities: Contemporary Japan combines lectures, tutorials, collaborative in-class learning, self-study and individual research.Lectures provide students with an overview of key theoretical concepts and historical context. Assigned readings and in-class activities encourage students to engage with and reflect on these concepts and contexts, providing students with the training and skills required to complete the assessment tasks.Before each lecture and tutorial, students are expected to have completed all of the preparation activities assigned. Guiding questions help students to focus on the most important aspects of these activities. Tutorials and lectures include preliminary work and formative feedback on assessment tasks.Tutorials consist of whole class and small group discussions that extend the preparatory learning. Students must come to class prepared to discuss the material and raise questions. Tutorial activities focus on developing the analytical and information literacy skills required both for this subject and subsequent study.Students receive peer and academic feedback on an initial journal entry early in the session and will have the opportunity to workshop their topic and initial ideas for the literature review.
976211.description: This subject provides an overview of contemporary Japan, examining its history, politics, culture, society, and economy. You'll acquire critical skills to pinpoint and analyse current issues, using theoretical and methodological frameworks for intercultural research. The subject combines lectures with independent work to enhance your problem-solving and analytical abilities in Japan studies, with a focus on cultural diversity, social change, and ethical practices. You'll develop critical thinking and communication skills, reflecting the subject's multidisciplinary nature. Early topics cover foundational concepts of Japan Studies, while subsequent weeks delve into Japanese identity, politics, work life, gender and Japan's international relationships.

97201.name: Japanese Language and Culture 1
learning_outcomes: Comprehend and respond to basic-level texts in oral and written language.
Produce basic-level texts in oral and written language.
Demonstrate intercultural awareness across two or more cultural contexts.
97201.learning_and_teaching_activities: This subject uses a communicative approach in class to maximise students' potential for effective communication in the target language. A series of activities such as interactive activities working in pairs, small and large groups and/or presentations, are promoted in class to enhance students' engagement through content-based learning. Preparation for class activities requires students to complete exercises and reading/listening/viewing activities prior to class to make effective use of resources and subject material (including resources online), community and peer resources available to them to support their learning.Students are encouraged to customise their learning experience as per the Global Japanese approach to promote their engagement with Japanese peoples and societies. This entails keeping in mind their goals for the use of Japanese (for instance studying abroad or travel in a particular country or region) and tailoring their practice and learning to maximize exposure to local variants of the language, relevant cultural norms and authentic materials from the area.As part of an immersive approach to language education, students are encouraged to use the target language within the classroom environment and to build confidence to use the target language outside the classroom. The approximate time commitment expected from students is 12 hours per week, including class time.
97201.description: This subject is designed to provide students who have no prior knowledge of the Japanese language with basic reading, listening, speaking and writing skills. It corresponds to N5 of the Japanese Language Proficiency Test (JLPT) or Level A1 (basic user) of the Common European Framework of Reference (CEFR) for Languages. Through active participation in collaborative activities, you'll develop intercultural competence, learning to navigate between cultures. Your learning will involve a communicative approach, engaging in interactive tasks and activities to enhance language proficiency. You'll be expected to prepare for classes through various exercises and utilise online and peer resources. The language learning approach will focus on linguistic and cultural practices across the francophone world. You’ll be expected to commit to approximately 12 hours of weekly study, including class time, and immerse yourself in Japanese both inside and outside the classroom to build confidence and fluency.

97207.name: Japanese Films and Popular Culture
learning_outcomes: Comprehend and respond to intermediate–advanced level texts in oral and written language.
Produce intermediate-advanced level texts in oral and written language.
Demonstrate intercultural awareness across two or more cultural contexts.
97207.learning_and_teaching_activities: This subject proposes a blended, practice-based and research-inspired learning experience. Japanese pop culture and films are analysed through a communicative and reflective approach to stimulate progressive and continuous learning of the target language. A series of authetic materials (books, articles and videos) are used to promote the use of real-life scenarios and situations such as interviews and discussion.Students are encouraged to customise their learning experience as per the Global Japanese approach to promote their engagement with Japanese peoples and societies. This entails keeping in mind their goals for the use of Japanese (for instance studying abroad or travel in a particular country or region) and tailoring their practice and learning to maximize exposure to local variants of the language, relevant cultural norms and authentic materials from the area.In addition to the in-class work, students are expected to complete a series of online activities, which strengthen their independent learning skills and facilitate a customised learning experience. The approximate time commitment expected from students is 12 hours per week, including class time.
97207.description: This subject is designed to provide students who are proficient in reading, listening, speaking and writing in Japanese with opportunities to engage with Japanese-speaking culture. It corresponds to N2/N1 of the Japanese Language Proficiency Test (JLPT) or Level B2/C1 (independent/proficient user) of the Common European Framework of Reference (CEFR) for Languages. You’ll examine the sociocultural contexts in which the Japanese language is used, focusing on the influence of cinema and other forms of popular culture on society. Via written and oral assessments that demonstrate continuing mastery of the Japanese language, your learning will involve a communicative approach, engaging in interactive tasks and resources to enhance language proficiency. You'll be expected to prepare for classes through various exercises and analyse a variety of authentic sources in a manner that considers their cultural provenance and role. You’ll explore subject materials and concepts through independent research, creative outputs and/or close textual analysis. The language learning approach will focus on linguistic and cultural practices across the Japanese-speaking world. You’ll be expected to commit to approximately 12 hours of weekly study, including class time, and immerse yourself in Japanese both inside and outside the classroom to build confidence and fluency.

976451.name: Contemporary Spain
learning_outcomes: Reflect on and analyse contemporary Spain in its historical, political, cultural, social, and economic dimensions.
Employ approaches and frameworks to engage in historical, political, cultural, social and economic inquiry.
Apply knowledges of Spain ethically in their reflective and inquiry-based works.
Demonstrate critical problem-solving and research-led analytical skills in the context of Spain.
Communicate effectively in English the findings of independent inquiries and reflections in written form.
Maintain good academic practice, observing ethics and referencing standards.
976451.learning_and_teaching_activities: Contemporary Spain combines lectures, tutorials, collaborative in-class learning, reflection and individual research.Lectures provide students with an overview of key theoretical concepts and historical context. Assigned readings and in-class activities are designed for students to engage with and reflect on these concepts and contexts. Tutorials also provide the training and skills required to complete the assessment tasks: visual analysis, critical reflection, annotated bibliographies, literature reviews and scoping a research topic.Contemporary Spain requires student engagement in and outside of class. Multimedia resources are provided to students in tutorials and online, and these resources can be used to complete assessment tasks where appropriate. Students are encouraged to apply critical thinking skills to their weekly reflections and assessment tasks. Contemporary Spain also allows students a good degree of flexibility to choose their own topic for their literature reviews in consultation with their tutor based on their individual interests and disciplinary background.Formative feedback on students' reflective writing will be provided in the week 3 tutorial. Extensive feedback will be provided on students’ Literature Review Proposal, giving students the opportunity to refine their Literature Review topic based on tutor feedback.
976451.description: This subject introduces you to contemporary Spain through its historical, political, cultural, social, and economic dimensions from a local, national, continental, and global context. You will be provided with the critical skills to identify, reflect, and analyse major current socio-cultural challenges that shape contemporary Spain through the learning of theoretical and methodological approaches for engaging you in intercultural research. In weeks 2 and 12, you will follow an umbrella program shared by all contemporary European society subjects that introduces general issues relating to contemporary Europe: definitions, geographical space, addressing its identity, unification, internal divisions, colonialism, and the impact of migration, among others. In the other weeks, the subject focuses on divergent and historical ways of conceptualising Spain. You will learn about important moments in Spain’s history, such as the unification, the Second Republic, the Civil War, the Franco dictatorship, the restoration of democracy, and the sociopolitical developments of recent decades. You will delve into the diversity of national and cultural identities (regionalisms), plurilingualism, gender, film, music, art, migration, economics, and the evolving socio-cultural aspects of contemporary Spain. This subject will also provide you with an understanding of some of the current challenges that the country faces and how a Spanish citizen thinks and feels in the twenty-first century. The collaborative and reflective learning activities will allow you to develop your intercultural capabilities via critical thinking on Spain's diverse and dynamic culture(s), as well as an international perspective. In the assessments, you will apply your critical and reflection skills to identify and analyse contemporary Spain, through theoretical and methodological approaches.

Preparation for class activities requires students to complete exercises and reading/listening/viewing activities prior to class to make effective use of resources and subject material (including resources online), community and peer resources available to them to support their learning.Students are encouraged to customise their learning experience as per the Global Spanish approach to promote their engagement with Hispanic-phone peoples and societies. This entails keeping in mind their goals for the use of Spanish (for instance studying abroad or travel in a particular country or region) and tailoring their practice and learning to maximize exposure to local variants of the language, relevant cultural norms and authentic materials from the area.As part of an immersive approach to language education, students are encouraged to use the target language within the classroom environment and to build confidence to use the target language outside the classroom. The approximate time commitment expected from students is 12 hours per week, including class time.
97501.description: This subject is designed to provide students who have no prior knowledge of the Spanish language with basic reading, listening, speaking and writing skills. It corresponds to Level A1 (basic user) of the Common European Framework of Reference (CEFR) for Languages. Through active participation in collaborative activities, you'll develop intercultural competence, learning to navigate between cultures. Your learning will involve a communicative approach, engaging in interactive tasks and activities to enhance language proficiency. You'll be expected to prepare for classes through various exercises and utilise online and peer resources. The language learning approach will focus on linguistic and cultural practices across the francophone world.You’ll be expected to commit to approximately 12 hours of weekly study, including class time, and immerse yourself in Spanish both inside and outside the classroom to build confidence and fluency.

97507.name: Human Rights in Hispanophone Societies
learning_outcomes: Comprehend, reflect upon and analyse complex texts in spoken (oral) and written (language).
Produce complex texts in spoken (oral) and written (language).
Demonstrate critical cultural awareness of Spanish speaking people and societies.
97507.learning_and_teaching_activities: Human Rights in Hispanophone Societies blends practice-based and research-inspired learning experience. Oral and written popular texts are analysed through a communicative and reflective approach, able to stimulate progressive and continuous learning, as well as reflection on the use of language in context. The analysis is supported by a series of supplementary authentic intermedial materials (songs, films and documentaries) and scaffolded through the use of real-life scenarios and situations, such as debates. The subject is structured so as to favour the interaction of in-class and independent learning environments as well as the construction of a nuanced and personalised learning experience.In addition the in-class work, students are asked to submit research-based written texts which strengthen their independent learning skills and facilitate a customised learning experience. The approximate time commitment expected from students is 12 hours per week, including class time.
97507.description: This subject is designed to provide students who are proficient in reading, listening, speaking and writing in Spanish with opportunities to engage with Hispanic culture. It corresponds to Level B2+ (independent/proficient user) of the Common European Framework of Reference (CEFR) for Languages. You’ll examine and engage with the sociocultural contexts in which the language is used, specifically with regard to human rights in Hispanic cultures. Via written and oral assessments that demonstrate continuing mastery of the Spanish language, your learning will involve a genre-based approach, engaging in interactive tasks and resources to enhance language proficiency. You'll be expected to prepare for classes through various exercises and analyse a variety of authentic sources in a manner that considers their cultural provenance and role. You’ll explore subject materials and concepts through independent research, creative outputs and/or close textual analysis. The language learning approach will focus on linguistic and cultural practices across the Hispanic world. You’ll be expected to commit to approximately 12 hours of weekly study, including class time, and immerse yourself in Spanish both inside and outside the classroom to build confidence and fluency.

SMJ09034.name: International Studies
SMJ09034.description: This sub-major provides students with foundational concepts and approaches for deepening understandings of other contemporary societies and cultures. Students select a country/regional specialisation for developing their linguistic, cultural and intercultural capabilities in an international framework.

contents.structure_details: (STM09034) Select one of the following options:

have_sub_structures.structure_details: Complete all of the following subjects:
Complete all of the following subjects:

have_subject.name: Intercultural Communication
learning_outcomes: Critically engage with intercultural communication theories and frameworks and apply these theories to analyse diverse cultural situations and spaces
Reflect on own perceptions of intercultural spaces and encounters and intercultural skills development for professional and everyday contexts
Design and conduct independent, ethical, small scale research about aspects of interculturality
Recognise the ongoing impact of colonialism on and positive contributions of Indigenous cultures in intercultural spaces
Communicate effectively and appropriately in written and oral academic English
have_subject.learning_and_teaching_activities: Classes will incorporate a range of teaching and learning strategies including presentations, videos, online learning materials, reflective surveys, discussion of readings, international case studies and group activities. These will be complemented by independent student research, readings and participation in online discussion forums.Feedback on assessment tasks will be provided in class, through quizzes and via GradeBook in Canvas.
have_subject.description: This subject provides an introduction to the core concepts and debates in the interdisciplinary field of intercultural communication. It is designed to help prepare students for the interpersonal and dynamic nature of working in increasingly globalised workplaces and intercultural environments. Using a problem-based approach, students learn to critically examine numerous frameworks and theories to interpret intercultural encounters in praxis-based scenarios. The applicability of specific strategies and approaches in intercultural communication are also examined for their relevance to industry, communities and in society more broadly.

requisite.rule: Requisite rule: ((1 OR 1a OR 1b OR 1c OR 1d OR 1e OR 1f OR 1g OR 1h OR 1i OR 1j OR 1k))

have_subject.name: Contemporary China
learning_outcomes: Reflect on and analyse contemporary China in its historical, political, cultural, social, and economic dimensions.
Employ approaches and frameworks to engage in historical, political, cultural, social and economic inquiry.
Apply knowledges of China ethically in their reflective and inquiry-based works.
Demonstrate critical problem-solving and research-led analytical skills in the context of China.
Communicate effectively in English the findings of independent inquiries and reflections in written form.
Maintain good academic practice, observing ethics and referencing standards.
have_subject.learning_and_teaching_activities: Contemporary China combines lectures, tutorials, collaborative in-class learning, self-study and individual research.Lectures provide students with an overview of key information, theoretical concepts, and historical context. Assigned readings and in-class activities encourage students to reflect on and engage with the class material, providing students with the training and skills required to complete the assessment tasks.Before each tutorial, students are expected to have completed all of the preparation activities assigned. Online lectures and guiding questions help students to focus on the most important aspects of these activities. Students must come to class prepared to discuss the material and raise questions.Tutorials consist of whole class and small group discussions that extend the preparatory learning. Tutorials focus on development of student understanding and provide formative feedback for assessment tasks. Tutorial activities focus on developing the analytical and literacy skills required for this subject and subsequent study.Students receive peer and academic feedback on an initial journal entry early in the session and will have the opportunity to workshop their topic and initial ideas for the literature review.
have_subject.description: This subject provides an overview of contemporary China and its history, culture, politics, society and economy. You will learn to identify and analyse current issues from multiple perspectives using theory and methods for intercultural research. The subject combines lectures with tutorials and independent work to develop your analytical and problem-solving abilities in China studies, with a focus on cultural diversity, social and economic change, and political transformation. You will learn to develop comparative and ethical approaches for critical thinking and verbal and written communication skills, reflecting the subject's multidisciplinary approach. Topics include cities, gender, home and work, identity, governance, media, the economy and China's international relationships.

requisite.rule: Requisite rule: ((1 AND 2) OR ((3 OR 3a OR 3b OR 3c)) OR ((4 OR 4a) AND (5 OR 5a OR 5b OR 5c OR 5d OR 5e OR 5f OR 5g OR 5h OR 5i OR 5j OR 5k)) OR (6) OR ((7 OR 7a OR 7b OR 7c)))

learning_outcomes: Comprehend and respond to simple texts in oral and written Chinese.
Produce simple texts in oral and written Chinese.
Demonstrate intercultural awareness across two or more cultural contexts.
have_subject.learning_and_teaching_activities: This subject uses a communicative approach in class to maximise students' potential for effective communication in the target language. A series of activities such as interactive activities working in pairs, small and large groups and/or presentations, are promoted in class to enhance students' engagement through content-based learning. Preparation for class activities requires students to complete exercises and reading/listening/viewing activities prior to class to make effective use of resources and subject material (including resources online), community and peer resources available to them to support their learning.Students are encouraged to customise their learning experience as per the Global Chinese approach to promote their engagement with Chinese peoples and societies. This entails keeping in mind their goals for the use of Chinese (for instance studying abroad or travel in a particular country or region) and tailoring their practice and learning to maximize exposure to local variants of the language, relevant cultural norms and authentic materials from the area.As part of an immersive approach to language education, students are encouraged to use the target language within the classroom environment and to build confidence to use the target language outside the classroom. The approximate time commitment expected from students is 12 hours per week, including class time.
have_subject.description: This subject is designed to provide students who have low intermediate knowledge of the Chinese language with basic reading, listening, speaking and writing skills, expanding your vocabulary by around 400 words. It corresponds to Level 2 of the Chinese Proficiency Test (HSK). Through active participation in collaborative activities, you'll develop intercultural competence, learning to navigate between cultures.Your learning will involve a communicative approach, engaging in interactive tasks and activities to enhance language proficiency. You'll be expected to prepare for classes through various exercises and utilise online and peer resources. The language learning approach will focus on linguistic and cultural practices across the Chinese world.You’ll be expected to commit to approximately 12 hours of weekly study, including class time, and immerse yourself in Chinese both inside and outside the classroom to build confidence and fluency.

have_subject.name: Communicating About Chinese Media
learning_outcomes: Develop awareness of register, rhetoric, genre, concept and specialist vocabulary through exposure to a wide variety of media and academic readings
Communicate professionally and academically in writing and speech in Chinese through competent use of specific linguistic skills and devices
Reflect on and analyse critically the impact of social issues as seen from media sources on the Chinese society and its people.
have_subject.learning_and_teaching_activities: The subject utilises flipped and hybrid teaching and learning strategies and both online and face-to-face delivery modes. Students use online and face-to-face class time for interactive practice and advancement of linguistic skills through personalised learning and group discussions, practices and/or presentations in flexible but guided and monitored physical or virtual classroom activities. Students also deepen their understanding of professional practices, theories and concepts and clarify subject content.Preparation for these activities requires students to complete exercises and reading/listening/viewing activities prior to coming to class and for them to make effective use of subject materials (including those posted online), community and peer resources available to them to support their learning.Students will customise their learning experience by engaging with Chinese media and their language, culture and practices from intercultural perspectives, and tailoring their learning experience to maximise understanding and appreciation of Chinese history, culture and society.Students are encouraged to explore subject materials and concepts through independent research, creative outputs and/or close textual analysis.
have_subject.description: This subject equips you with advanced Chinese linguistic skills, preparing you for careers in Chinese-language media and critical reflection on media practices. You'll master crafting nuanced texts, employing specialised vocabulary and rhetoric, and analysing contemporary China through diverse media sources. Learning combines online and in-person methods, including interactive practice and group work, with a focus on personalised and collaborative learning. Prior to classes, you'll engage with preparatory exercises and materials to enhance in-class discussions and presentations. You'll also tailor your studies to your interests, exploring Chinese media, culture, and society through independent research and creative analysis, fostering a deep intercultural understanding.

have_subject.name: Chinese Festivals and Ceremonies
learning_outcomes: Comprehend and respond to complex texts in oral and written Chinese.
Produce complex texts in oral and written Chinese.
Demonstrate intercultural awareness across two or more cultural contexts.

have_subject.description: This subject is designed to provide students who are proficient in reading, listening, speaking and writing in Chinese with opportunities to engage with Chinese culture. It corresponds to Level 3/4 of the Chinese Proficiency Test (HSK). You’ll examine and engage with the sociocultural contexts in which the language is used, specifically with regard to festivals and ceremonies in the Chinese world.Via written and oral assessments that demonstrate continuing mastery of the Chinese language, your learning will involve a communicative approach, engaging in interactive tasks and resources to enhance language proficiency. You'll be expected to prepare for classes through various exercises and analyse a variety of authentic sources in a manner that considers their cultural provenance and role. You’ll explore subject materials and concepts through independent research, creative outputs and/or close textual analysis. The language learning approach will focus on linguistic and cultural practices across the Chinese world.You’ll be expected to commit to approximately 12 hours of weekly study, including class time, and immerse yourself in Chinese both inside and outside the classroom to build confidence and fluency.

have_subject.name: Language in Chinese Screen Studies
learning_outcomes: Develop awareness of register, rhetoric, genre, concept and specialist vocabulary through exposure to contemporary Chinese movies and a wide variety of academic and professional readings
Communicate professionally and academically in writing and speech in Chinese through competent use of specific linguistic skills and devices
Analyse cinematic representation of Chinese social, cultural and political themes;
Develop basic writing skills to prepare a movie synopsis, storyboard, screen play and film reviews
have_subject.learning_and_teaching_activities: The subject utilises flipped and hybrid teaching and learning strategies and both online and face-to-face delivery modes. Students use online and face-to-face class time for interactive practice and advancement of linguistic skills through personalised learning and group discussions, practices and/or presentations in flexible but guided and monitored physical or virtual classroom activities. Students also deepen their understanding of professional practices, theories and concepts and clarify subject content.Preparation for these activities requires students to complete exercises and reading/listening/viewing activities prior to coming to class and for them to make effective use of subject materials (including those posted online), community and peer resources available to them to support their learning.Students will customise their learning experience by engaging with Chinese film industry and its culture and practices from intercultural perspectives, and tailoring their learning experience to maximise understanding and appreciation of Chinese history, culture and society.Students are encouraged to explore subject materials and concepts through independent research, creative outputs and/or close textual analysis.
have_subject.description: This subject equips you with advanced Modern Standard Chinese skills, focusing on specialised vocabulary and linguistic nuances relevant to film analysis. You'll gain proficiency in evaluating Chinese cinema within its socio-economic milieu, mastering technical language in both Chinese and English to critique films and script your mini screenplays. Learning is interactive, blending online and in-person sessions for practice and discussion, while independent study and resource utilisation enhance your grasp of professional practices and theoretical concepts. You'll tailor your experience by exploring the Chinese film industry's cultural context, fostering a deeper appreciation of China's heritage and societal norms through independent research and creative engagement.

Through active participation in collaborative activities, you'll develop intercultural competence, learning to navigate between cultures.Your learning will involve a communicative approach, engaging in interactive tasks and activities to enhance language proficiency. The language learning approach will focus on linguistic and cultural practices across the Chinese world.You’ll be expected to commit to approximately 12 hours of weekly study, including class time, and immerse yourself in Chinese both inside and outside the classroom to build confidence and fluency.

have_subject.name: Chinese Business and Administrative Language
learning_outcomes: Select and deploy register, rhetoric, genre, concept and specialist vocabulary through exposure to business and administrative writings of a wide range of genres and purposes
Communicate professionally in writing and speech in Chinese through competent use of specific and advanced linguistic skills and devices
Develop awareness of when to deploy various Chinese and business cross-cultural communication practices
have_subject.learning_and_teaching_activities: The subject utilises flipped and hybrid teaching and learning strategies and both online and face-to-face delivery modes. Students use online and face-to-face class time for interactive practice and advancement of linguistic skills through personalised learning and group discussions, practices and/or presentations in flexible but guided and monitored physical or virtual classroom activities. Students also deepen their understanding of professional practices, theories and concepts and clarify subject content.Preparation for these activities requires students to complete exercises and reading/listening/viewing activities prior to coming to class and for them to make effective use of subject materials (including those posted online), community and peer resources available to them to support their learning.Students will customise their learning experience by engaging with Chinese business and professional language and practices from intercultural perspectives, and tailoring their practice and learning to maximise understanding and appreciation of Chinese business and administrative systems, customs and common practices.Students are encouraged to explore subject materials and concepts through independent research, creative outputs and/or close textual analysis.
have_subject.description: This subject equips you with specialised proficiency in Modern Standard Chinese, focusing on business and administrative communication. You'll master formal writing styles, expand your business vocabulary, and enhance speaking skills through practical assessments, preparing you for cross-cultural business environments. Learning combines online and in-person methods, including interactive practice and group work, to advance your linguistic abilities. You'll engage with Chinese business language and practices, customising your learning to understand and appreciate the nuances of Chinese professional settings. Independent research and creative analysis further deepen your knowledge, making you more culturally aware and employable internationally.

have_sub_structures.structure_details: Complete all of the following subjects:
Complete all of the following subjects:

have_subject.name: Intercultural Communication
learning_outcomes: Critically engage with intercultural communication theories and frameworks and apply these theories to analyse diverse cultural situations and spaces
Reflect on own perceptions of intercultural spaces and encounters and intercultural skills development for professional and everyday contexts
Design and conduct independent, ethical, small scale research about aspects of interculturality
Recognise the ongoing impact of colonialism on and positive contributions of Indigenous cultures in intercultural spaces
Communicate effectively and appropriately in written and oral academic English
have_subject.learning_and_teaching_activities: Classes will incorporate a range of teaching and learning strategies including presentations, videos, online learning materials, reflective surveys, discussion of readings, international case studies and group activities. These will be complemented by independent student research, readings and participation in online discussion forums.Feedback on assessment tasks will be provided in class, through quizzes and via GradeBook in Canvas.
have_subject.description: This subject provides an introduction to the core concepts and debates in the interdisciplinary field of intercultural communication. It is designed to help prepare students for the interpersonal and dynamic nature of working in increasingly globalised workplaces and intercultural environments. Using a problem-based approach, students learn to critically examine numerous frameworks and theories to interpret intercultural encounters in praxis-based scenarios. The applicability of specific strategies and approaches in intercultural communication are also examined for their relevance to industry, communities and in society more broadly.

requisite.rule: Requisite rule: ((1 OR 1a OR 1b OR 1c OR 1d OR 1e OR 1f OR 1g OR 1h OR 1i OR 1j OR 1k))

have_subject.name: Contemporary France
learning_outcomes: Reflect on and analyse contemporary France in its historical, political, cultural, social, and economic dimensions.
Employ approaches and frameworks to engage in historical, political, cultural, social and economic inquiry.
Apply knowledges of France ethically in their reflective and inquiry-based works.
Demonstrate critical problem-solving and research-led analytical skills in the context of France.
Communicate effectively in English the findings of independent inquiries and reflections in written form.
Maintain good academic practice, observing ethics and referencing standards.
have_subject.learning_and_teaching_activities: Contemporary France combines lectures, tutorials, collaborative in-class learning, self-study and individual research.Lectures provide students with an overview of important theoretical concepts and historical context. Assigned readings and in-class activities are designed for students to engage with and reflect on these concepts and contexts. Tutorials also provide the training and skills required to complete the assessment tasks: visual analysis, critical reflection, annotated bibliographies, literature reviews and scoping a research topic.Contemporary France requires student engagement in and outside of class. Multimedia resources will be provided to students in tutorials and online, and these resources can be used to complete assessment tasks where appropriate. Students are encouraged to apply critical thinking skills to their weekly reflections and assessment tasks. Contemporary France also allows students a good degree of flexibility to choose their own topic for their literature reviews in consultation with their tutor based on their individual interests and disciplinary background.
have_subject.description: This subject blends lectures and independent and collaborative learning to provide a foundational understanding of contemporary French society, politics and culture. You'll develop critical thinking and intercultural research skills. In weeks 2 and 12 you'll follow an umbrella programme shared by all European contemporary society subjects, exploring identity, divisions and crises in Europe. Between weeks 3 and 11 you'll explore France's historical evolution and the concept of French identity, with special focus on France's 'revolutionary' spirit, the importance of its Republican values, and its relations with former French colonies. These elements are crucial for understanding France's place in the modern world and the social and political issues shaping contemporary French society.

requisite.rule: Requisite rule: ((1 AND 2) OR ((3 OR 3a OR 3b OR 3c)) OR ((4 OR 4a) AND (5 OR 5a OR 5b OR 5c OR 5d OR 5e OR 5f OR 5g OR 5h OR 5i OR 5j OR 5k)) OR (6) OR ((7 OR 7a OR 7b OR 7c)))

have_sub_structures.structure_details: Select 8 credit points from the following subjects:

have_subject.name: French Language and Culture 1
learning_outcomes: Comprehend and respond to simple texts in oral and written French.
Produce simple texts in oral and written French.
Demonstrate intercultural awareness across two or more cultural contexts.
have_subject.learning_and_teaching_activities: This subject uses a communicative approach in class to maximise students' potential for effective communication in the target language. A series of activities such as interactive activities working in pairs, small and large groups and/or presentations, are promoted in class to enhance students' engagement through content-based learning. Preparation for class activities requires students to complete exercises and reading/listening/viewing activities prior to class to make effective use of resources and subject material (including resources online), community and peer resources available to them to support their learning.Students are encouraged to customise their learning experience as per the Global French approach to promote their engagement with francophone peoples and societies. This entails keeping in mind their goals for the use of French (for instance studying abroad or travel in a particular country or region) and tailoring their practice and learning to maximize exposure to local variants of the language, relevant cultural norms and authentic materials from the area.As part of an immersive approach to language education, students are encouraged to use the target language within the classroom environment and to build confidence to use the target language outside the classroom. The approximate time commitment expected from students is 12 hours per week, including class time.
have_subject.description: This subject is designed to provide students who have no prior knowledge of the French language with basic reading, listening, speaking and writing skills. It corresponds to Level A1 (basic user) of the Common European Framework of Reference (CEFR) for Languages. Through active participation in collaborative activities, you'll develop intercultural competence, learning to navigate between cultures.Your learning will involve a communicative approach, engaging in interactive tasks and activities to enhance language proficiency. You'll be expected to prepare for classes through various exercises and utilise online and peer resources. The language learning approach will focus on linguistic and cultural practices across the francophone world.You’ll be expected to commit to approximately 12 hours of weekly study, including class time, and immerse yourself in French both inside and outside the classroom to build confidence and fluency.

The approximate time commitment expected from students is 12 hours per week, including class time.
have_subject.description: This subject is designed to provide students who have some prior knowledge of the French language with basic reading, listening, speaking and writing skills. You'll be expected to prepare for classes through various exercises and utilise online and peer resources. The language learning approach will focus on linguistic and cultural practices across the francophone world.You’ll be expected to commit to approximately 12 hours of weekly study, including class time, and immerse yourself in French both inside and outside the classroom to build confidence and fluency.

have_subject.name: Francophone Identities in Conflict
learning_outcomes: Comprehend and respond to complex texts in oral and written French.
Produce complex texts in oral and written French.
Demonstrate intercultural awareness across two or more cultural contexts.
have_subject.learning_and_teaching_activities: Class time is used for interactive practice and advancement of skills through work in pairs, small and large groups and/or presentations, as well as clarification of subject content. Students will spend a substantial portion of each lesson in dialogue with their peers and their teacher.Preparation for these activities requires students to complete exercises and reading/listening/viewing activities prior to coming to class and for them to make effective use of subject materials (including UTS Online), community and peer resources available to them to support their learning. As this is an 8 credit point subject, the approximate time commitment expected from students is 12 hours per week, including class time.Students will customise their learning experience as per the Global French approach to promote their engagement with francophone peoples and societies. This entails keeping in mind one's goals for the use of French (for instance studying abroad or travel in a particular country or region) and tailoring one's practice and learning to maximise exposure to local variants of the language, relevant cultural norms and authentic materials from the area.Students are strongly encouraged to prioritise the use of French when communicating with each other or with their teacher in class.Students are encouraged to explore subject materials and concepts through independent research, creative outputs and/or close textual analysis.
have_subject.description: This subject is designed to provide students who are proficient in reading, listening, speaking and writing in French with opportunities to engage with francophone culture. It corresponds to Level B2/C1 (independent/proficient user) of the Common European Framework of Reference (CEFR) for Languages. You’ll examine and engage with the sociocultural contexts in which the language is used, specifically with regard to questions of history and conflict in the francophone world.Via written, oral and aural assessments that demonstrate continuing mastery of the French language, your learning will involve a communicative approach, engaging in interactive tasks and using a variety of resources to enhance language proficiency. You'll be expected to prepare for classes through various exercises and analyse a variety of authentic sources in a manner that considers their cultural provenance and role. You’ll explore subject materials and concepts through independent research, creative outputs and/or close textual analysis. The language learning approach will focus on linguistic and cultural practices across the francophone world.You’ll be expected to commit to approximately 12 hours of weekly study, including class time, and immerse yourself in French both inside and outside the classroom to build confidence and fluency.

have_sub_structures.structure_details: Complete all of the following subjects:
Complete all of the following subjects:

have_subject.name: Intercultural Communication
learning_outcomes: Critically engage with intercultural communication theories and frameworks and apply these theories to analyse diverse cultural situations and spaces
Reflect on own perceptions of intercultural spaces and encounters and intercultural skills development for professional and everyday contexts
Design and conduct independent, ethical, small scale research about aspects of interculturality
Recognise the ongoing impact of colonialism on and positive contributions of Indigenous cultures in intercultural spaces
Communicate effectively and appropriately in written and oral academic English
have_subject.learning_and_teaching_activities: Classes will incorporate a range of teaching and learning strategies including presentations, videos, online learning materials, reflective surveys, discussion of readings, international case studies and group activities. These will be complemented by independent student research, readings and participation in online discussion forums.Feedback on assessment tasks will be provided in class, through quizzes and via GradeBook in Canvas.
have_subject.description: This subject provides an introduction to the core concepts and debates in the interdisciplinary field of intercultural communication. It is designed to help prepare students for the interpersonal and dynamic nature of working in increasingly globalised workplaces and intercultural environments. Using a problem-based approach, students learn to critically examine numerous frameworks and theories to interpret intercultural encounters in praxis-based scenarios. The applicability of specific strategies and approaches in intercultural communication are also examined for their relevance to industry, communities and in society more broadly.

requisite.rule: Requisite rule: ((1 OR 1a OR 1b OR 1c OR 1d OR 1e OR 1f OR 1g OR 1h OR 1i OR 1j OR 1k))

have_subject.name: Contemporary Germany
learning_outcomes: Reflect on and analyse contemporary Germany in its historical, political, cultural, social, and economic dimensions.
Employ approaches and frameworks to engage in historical, political, cultural, social and economic inquiry.
Apply knowledges of Germany ethically in their reflective and inquiry-based works.
Demonstrate critical problem-solving and research-led analytical skills in the context of Germany.
Communicate effectively in English the findings of independent inquiries and reflections in written form.
Maintain good academic practice, observing ethics and referencing standards.
have_subject.learning_and_teaching_activities: Contemporary Germany combines lectures, tutorials, collaborative in-class learning, reflection and individual research.Lectures provide students with an overview of key theoretical concepts and historical context. Assigned readings and in-class activities are designed for students to engage with and reflect on these concepts and contexts. Tutorials also provide the training and skills required to complete the assessment tasks: visual analysis, critical reflection, annotated bibliographies, essays and scoping a research topic.Contemporary Germany requires student engagement in and outside of class. Multimedia resources are provided to students in tutorials and online, and these resources can be used to complete assessment tasks where appropriate. Students are encouraged to apply critical thinking skills to their weekly reflections and assessment tasks. Contemporary Germany also allows students a good degree of flexibility to choose their own topic for their essays in consultation with their tutor based on their individual interests and disciplinary background.Formative feedback on students' reflective writing will be provided in the week 3 tutorial. Extensive feedback will be provided on students’ Essay Proposal, giving students the opportunity to refine their Essay topic based on tutor feedback.
have_subject.description: This subject blends lectures, independent and collaborative learning to provide a foundational understanding of contemporary German history, politics, society, and culture. You'll develop critical thinking and intercultural research skills, essential for advanced In-country Studies. In weeks 2 and 12 you'll follow an umbrella program shared by all contemporary European society subjects, exploring its identity, divisions, and crises. Between weeks 3 and 11, you'll focus on Germany's post-World War II era, examining its divided past, reunification, and current challenges, including identity, social movements, economic growth, immigration, and international relations, alongside language issues and popular culture. These elements are crucial for understanding Germany's place in the modern world. German popular culture is dealt with throughout.

requisite.rule: Requisite rule: ((1 AND 2) OR ((3 OR 3a OR 3b OR 3c)) OR ((4 OR 4a) AND (5 OR 5a OR 5b OR 5c OR 5d OR 5e OR 5f OR 5g OR 5h OR 5i OR 5j OR 5k)) OR (6) OR ((7 OR 7a OR 7b OR 7c)))

have_sub_structures.structure_details: Complete all of the following subjects:
Complete all of the following subjects:

have_subject.name: Intercultural Communication
learning_outcomes: Critically engage with intercultural communication theories and frameworks and apply these theories to analyse diverse cultural situations and spaces
Reflect on own perceptions of intercultural spaces and encounters and intercultural skills development for professional and everyday contexts
Design and conduct independent, ethical, small scale research about aspects of interculturality
Recognise the ongoing impact of colonialism on and positive contributions of Indigenous cultures in intercultural spaces
Communicate effectively and appropriately in written and oral academic English
have_subject.learning_and_teaching_activities: Classes will incorporate a range of teaching and learning strategies including presentations, videos, online learning materials, reflective surveys, discussion of readings, international case studies and group activities. These will be complemented by independent student research, readings and participation in online discussion forums.Feedback on assessment tasks will be provided in class, through quizzes and via GradeBook in Canvas.
have_subject.description: This subject provides an introduction to the core concepts and debates in the interdisciplinary field of intercultural communication. It is designed to help prepare students for the interpersonal and dynamic nature of working in increasingly globalised workplaces and intercultural environments. Using a problem-based approach, students learn to critically examine numerous frameworks and theories to interpret intercultural encounters in praxis-based scenarios. The applicability of specific strategies and approaches in intercultural communication are also examined for their relevance to industry, communities and in society more broadly.

requisite.rule: Requisite rule: ((1 OR 1a OR 1b OR 1c OR 1d OR 1e OR 1f OR 1g OR 1h OR 1i OR 1j OR 1k))

have_subject.name: Contemporary Italy
learning_outcomes: Reflect on and analyse contemporary Italy in its historical, political, cultural, social, and economic dimensions.
Employ approaches and frameworks to engage in historical, political, cultural, social and economic inquiry.
Apply knowledges of Italy ethically in their reflective and inquiry-based works.
Demonstrate critical problem-solving and research-led analytical skills in the context of Italy.
Communicate effectively in English the findings of independent inquiries and reflections in written form.
Maintain good academic practice, observing ethics and referencing standards.
have_subject.learning_and_teaching_activities: Contemporary Italy combines lectures, tutorials, collaborative learning, reflection and individual research. Lectures provide students with an overview of key theoretical concepts and historical context. Assigned readings and in-class activities are designed for students to engage with and reflect on these concepts and contexts. Tutorials also provide the training and skills required to complete the assessment tasks: visual analysis, critical reflection, essays and scoping a research topic. Contemporary Italy requires student engagement in and outside of sessions. Multimedia resources are provided to students in tutorials and online, and these resources can be used to complete assessment tasks where appropriate. Students are encouraged to apply critical thinking skills to their weekly reflections and assessment tasks. Contemporary Italy also allows students a good degree of flexibility to choose their own topic for their essays in consultation with their tutor based on their individual interests and disciplinary background. Formative feedback on students' reflective writing will be provided in the Week 3 tutorial.
have_subject.description: This subject blends lectures, independent and collaborative learning to provide a foundational understanding of contemporary Italy's history and culture. Through this subject, you'll develop critical thinking and intercultural research skills. In weeks 2 and 12 you'll follow an umbrella program shared by all contemporary European society subjects, exploring its identity, divisions, and crises. Between weeks 3 and 11, you'll focus on Italy, its evolution from Unification and the Fascist era to post-war economic growth and recent sociopolitical changes, with a special emphasis on the roles of migration and multilingualism in shaping Italian culture and identity.

requisite.rule: Requisite rule: ((1 AND 2) OR ((3 OR 3a OR 3b OR 3c)) OR ((4 OR 4a) AND (5 OR 5a OR 5b OR 5c OR 5d OR 5e OR 5f OR 5g OR 5h OR 5i OR 5j OR 5k)) OR (6) OR ((7 OR 7a OR 7b OR 7c)))

have_subject.name: Italy and Migration
learning_outcomes: Comprehend, reflect upon and analyse intermediate texts in spoken and written Italian.
Produce intermediate texts in spoken and written Italian.
Demonstrate intercultural awareness across two or more cultural contexts.
have_subject.learning_and_teaching_activities: Italy and Migration proposes an innovative blended, practice-based and research-inspired learning experience. Primary and secondary sources are analysed through a communicative and reflective approach, aimed at stimulating progressive and continuous learning, as well as reflecting on the use of language in context. The analyses are developed through engagement with a series of authentic intermedial materials (such as films, poetry, short stories, narratives, maps) and scaffolded through the discussion of these materials in real-life scenarios and situations (debates, role-plays, interviews). The subject’s structure favours the interaction of physical and virtual learning environments as well as the construction of a nuanced and personalised learning experience.In addition to the in-class work, students are asked to complete a series of online activities, which strengthen their independent learning skills and facilitate a customised learning experience. The approximate time commitment expected from students is 12 hours per week, including class time.
have_subject.description: This subject equips you with an understanding of Italian migratory dynamics, enhancing your Italian language skills. It corresponds to Level B1+ of the Common European Framework of Reference for Languages (CEFR). You'll learn to analyse and discuss Italy's migration stories through engaging with texts, movies, audio and visual materials. Materials are explored through a communicative and reflective approach, aimed at stimulating progressive and continuous learning, as well as reflecting on the use of language in context. Your learning journey involves active participation in collaborative exercises. Expect to dedicate around 12 hours weekly, including independent online activities, to develop a tailored and comprehensive learning experience.

have_subject.name: Contemporary Japan
learning_outcomes: Reflect on and analyse contemporary Japan in its historical, political, cultural, social, and economic dimensions.
Employ approaches and frameworks to engage in historical, political, cultural, social and economic inquiry.
Apply knowledges of Japan ethically in their reflective and inquiry-based works.
Demonstrate critical problem-solving and research-led analytical skills in the context of Japan.
Communicate effectively in English the findings of independent inquiries and reflections in written form.
Maintain good academic practice, observing ethics and referencing standards.
have_subject.learning_and_teaching_activities: Contemporary Japan combines lectures, tutorials, collaborative in-class learning, self-study and individual research.Lectures provide students with an overview of key theoretical concepts and historical context. Assigned readings and in-class activities encourage students to engage with and reflect on these concepts and contexts, providing students with the training and skills required to complete the assessment tasks.Before each lecture and tutorial, students are expected to have completed all of the preparation activities assigned. Guiding questions help students to focus on the most important aspects of these activities. Tutorials and lectures include preliminary work and formative feedback on assessment tasks.Tutorials consist of whole class and small group discussions that extend the preparatory learning. Students must come to class prepared to discuss the material and raise questions. Tutorial activities focus on developing the analytical and information literacy skills required both for this subject and subsequent study.Students receive peer and academic feedback on an initial journal entry early in the session and will have the opportunity to workshop their topic and initial ideas for the literature review.
have_subject.description: This subject provides an overview of contemporary Japan, examining its history, politics, culture, society, and economy. You'll acquire critical skills to pinpoint and analyse current issues, using theoretical and methodological frameworks for intercultural research. The subject combines lectures with independent work to enhance your problem-solving and analytical abilities in Japan studies, with a focus on cultural diversity, social change, and ethical practices. You'll develop critical thinking and communication skills, reflecting the subject's multidisciplinary nature. Early topics cover foundational concepts of Japan Studies, while subsequent weeks delve into Japanese identity, politics, work life, gender and Japan's international relationships.

requisite.rule: Requisite rule: ((1 AND 2) OR ((3 OR 3a OR 3b OR 3c)) OR ((4 OR 4a) AND (5 OR 5a OR 5b OR 5c OR 5d OR 5e OR 5f OR 5g OR 5h OR 5i OR 5j OR 5k)) OR (6) OR ((7 OR 7a OR 7b OR 7c)))

have_sub_structures.structure_details: Select 8 credit points from the following subjects:

have_subject.name: Japanese Language and Culture 1
learning_outcomes: Comprehend and respond to basic-level texts in oral and written language.
Produce basic-level texts in oral and written language.
Demonstrate intercultural awareness across two or more cultural contexts.
have_subject.learning_and_teaching_activities: This subject uses a communicative approach in class to maximise students' potential for effective communication in the target language. A series of activities such as interactive activities working in pairs, small and large groups and/or presentations, are promoted in class to enhance students' engagement through content-based learning. Preparation for class activities requires students to complete exercises and reading/listening/viewing activities prior to class to make effective use of resources and subject material (including resources online), community and peer resources available to them to support their learning.Students are encouraged to customise their learning experience as per the Global Japanese approach to promote their engagement with Japanese peoples and societies. This entails keeping in mind their goals for the use of Japanese (for instance studying abroad or travel in a particular country or region) and tailoring their practice and learning to maximize exposure to local variants of the language, relevant cultural norms and authentic materials from the area.As part of an immersive approach to language education, students are encouraged to use the target language within the classroom environment and to build confidence to use the target language outside the classroom. The approximate time commitment expected from students is 12 hours per week, including class time.
have_subject.description: This subject is designed to provide students who have no prior knowledge of the Japanese language with basic reading, listening, speaking and writing skills. It corresponds to N5 of the Japanese Language Proficiency Test (JLPT) or Level A1 (basic user) of the Common European Framework of Reference (CEFR) for Languages. Through active participation in collaborative activities, you'll develop intercultural competence, learning to navigate between cultures. Your learning will involve a communicative approach, engaging in interactive tasks and activities to enhance language proficiency. You'll be expected to prepare for classes through various exercises and utilise online and peer resources. The language learning approach will focus on linguistic and cultural practices across the francophone world. You’ll be expected to commit to approximately 12 hours of weekly study, including class time, and immerse yourself in Japanese both inside and outside the classroom to build confidence and fluency.

have_subject.name: Japanese Films and Popular Culture
learning_outcomes: Comprehend and respond to intermediate–advanced level texts in oral and written language.
Produce intermediate-advanced level texts in oral and written language.
Demonstrate intercultural awareness across two or more cultural contexts.
have_subject.learning_and_teaching_activities: This subject proposes a blended, practice-based and research-inspired learning experience. Japanese pop culture and films are analysed through a communicative and reflective approach to stimulate progressive and continuous learning of the target language. A series of authetic materials (books, articles and videos) are used to promote the use of real-life scenarios and situations such as interviews and discussion.Students are encouraged to customise their learning experience as per the Global Japanese approach to promote their engagement with Japanese peoples and societies. This entails keeping in mind their goals for the use of Japanese (for instance studying abroad or travel in a particular country or region) and tailoring their practice and learning to maximize exposure to local variants of the language, relevant cultural norms and authentic materials from the area.In addition to the in-class work, students are expected to complete a series of online activities, which strengthen their independent learning skills and facilitate a customised learning experience. The approximate time commitment expected from students is 12 hours per week, including class time.
have_subject.description: This subject is designed to provide students who are proficient in reading, listening, speaking and writing in Japanese with opportunities to engage with Japanese-speaking culture. It corresponds to N2/N1 of the Japanese Language Proficiency Test (JLPT) or Level B2/C1 (independent/proficient user) of the Common European Framework of Reference (CEFR) for Languages. You’ll examine the sociocultural contexts in which the Japanese language is used, focusing on the influence of cinema and other forms of popular culture on society. Via written and oral assessments that demonstrate continuing mastery of the Japanese language, your learning will involve a communicative approach, engaging in interactive tasks and resources to enhance language proficiency. You'll be expected to prepare for classes through various exercises and analyse a variety of authentic sources in a manner that considers their cultural provenance and role. You’ll explore subject materials and concepts through independent research, creative outputs and/or close textual analysis. The language learning approach will focus on linguistic and cultural practices across the Japanese-speaking world. You’ll be expected to commit to approximately 12 hours of weekly study, including class time, and immerse yourself in Japanese both inside and outside the classroom to build confidence and fluency.

Complete all of the following subjects:

have_subject.name: Intercultural Communication
learning_outcomes: Critically engage with intercultural communication theories and frameworks and apply these theories to analyse diverse cultural situations and spaces
Reflect on own perceptions of intercultural spaces and encounters and intercultural skills development for professional and everyday contexts
Design and conduct independent, ethical, small scale research about aspects of interculturality
Recognise the ongoing impact of colonialism on and positive contributions of Indigenous cultures in intercultural spaces
Communicate effectively and appropriately in written and oral academic English
have_subject.learning_and_teaching_activities: Classes will incorporate a range of teaching and learning strategies including presentations, videos, online learning materials, reflective surveys, discussion of readings, international case studies and group activities. These will be complemented by independent student research, readings and participation in online discussion forums.Feedback on assessment tasks will be provided in class, through quizzes and via GradeBook in Canvas.
have_subject.description: This subject provides an introduction to the core concepts and debates in the interdisciplinary field of intercultural communication. It is designed to help prepare students for the interpersonal and dynamic nature of working in increasingly globalised workplaces and intercultural environments. Using a problem-based approach, students learn to critically examine numerous frameworks and theories to interpret intercultural encounters in praxis-based scenarios. The applicability of specific strategies and approaches in intercultural communication are also examined for their relevance to industry, communities and in society more broadly.
