have_subject.name: Contemporary Spain
learning_outcomes: Reflect on and analyse contemporary Spain in its historical, political, cultural, social, and economic dimensions.
Employ approaches and frameworks to engage in historical, political, cultural, social and economic inquiry.
Apply knowledges of Spain ethically in their reflective and inquiry-based works.
Demonstrate critical problem-solving and research-led analytical skills in the context of Spain.
Communicate effectively in English the findings of independent inquiries and reflections in written form.
Maintain good academic practice, observing ethics and referencing standards.
have_subject.learning_and_teaching_activities: Contemporary Spain combines lectures, tutorials, collaborative in-class learning, reflection and individual research.Lectures provide students with an overview of key theoretical concepts and historical context. Assigned readings and in-class activities are designed for students to engage with and reflect on these concepts and contexts. Tutorials also provide the training and skills required to complete the assessment tasks: visual analysis, critical reflection, annotated bibliographies, literature reviews and scoping a research topic.Contemporary Spain requires student engagement in and outside of class. Multimedia resources are provided to students in tutorials and online, and these resources can be used to complete assessment tasks where appropriate. Students are encouraged to apply critical thinking skills to their weekly reflections and assessment tasks. Contemporary Spain also allows students a good degree of flexibility to choose their own topic for their literature reviews in consultation with their tutor based on their individual interests and disciplinary background.Formative feedback on students' reflective writing will be provided in the week 3 tutorial. Extensive feedback will be provided on students’ Literature Review Proposal, giving students the opportunity to refine their Literature Review topic based on tutor feedback.
have_subject.description: This subject introduces you to contemporary Spain through its historical, political, cultural, social, and economic dimensions from a local, national, continental, and global context. You will be provided with the critical skills to identify, reflect, and analyse major current socio-cultural challenges that shape contemporary Spain through the learning of theoretical and methodological approaches for engaging you in intercultural research. In weeks 2 and 12, you will follow an umbrella program shared by all contemporary European society subjects that introduces general issues relating to contemporary Europe: definitions, geographical space, addressing its identity, unification, internal divisions, colonialism, and the impact of migration, among others. In the other weeks, the subject focuses on divergent and historical ways of conceptualising Spain. You will learn about important moments in Spain’s history, such as the unification, the Second Republic, the Civil War, the Franco dictatorship, the restoration of democracy, and the sociopolitical developments of recent decades. You will delve into the diversity of national and cultural identities (regionalisms), plurilingualism, gender, film, music, art, migration, economics, and the evolving socio-cultural aspects of contemporary Spain. This subject will also provide you with an understanding of some of the current challenges that the country faces and how a Spanish citizen thinks and feels in the twenty-first century. The collaborative and reflective learning activities will allow you to develop your intercultural capabilities via critical thinking on Spain's diverse and dynamic culture(s), as well as an international perspective. In the assessments, you will apply your critical and reflection skills to identify and analyse contemporary Spain, through theoretical and methodological approaches.

requisite.rule: Requisite rule: ((1 AND 2) OR ((3 OR 3a OR 3b OR 3c)) OR ((4 OR 4a) AND (5 OR 5a OR 5b OR 5c OR 5d OR 5e OR 5f OR 5g OR 5h OR 5i OR 5j OR 5k)) OR (6) OR ((7 OR 7a OR 7b OR 7c)))

have_sub_structures.structure_details: Select 8 credit points from the following subjects:

have_subject.name: Spanish Language and Culture 1
learning_outcomes: Comprehend and respond to simple texts in oral and written Spanish
Produce simple texts in oral and written Spanish
Demonstrate intercultural awareness across two or more cultural contexts.
have_subject.learning_and_teaching_activities: This subject uses a communicative approach in class to maximise students' potential for effective communication in the target language. A series of activities such as interactive activities working in pairs, small and large groups and/or presentations, are promoted in class to enhance students' engagement through content-based learning. Preparation for class activities requires students to complete exercises and reading/listening/viewing activities prior to class to make effective use of resources and subject material (including resources online), community and peer resources available to them to support their learning.Students are encouraged to customise their learning experience as per the Global Spanish approach to promote their engagement with Hispanic-phone peoples and societies. This entails keeping in mind their goals for the use of Spanish (for instance studying abroad or travel in a particular country or region) and tailoring their practice and learning to maximize exposure to local variants of the language, relevant cultural norms and authentic materials from the area.As part of an immersive approach to language education, students are encouraged to use the target language within the classroom environment and to build confidence to use the target language outside the classroom. The approximate time commitment expected from students is 12 hours per week, including class time.
have_subject.description: This subject is designed to provide students who have no prior knowledge of the Spanish language with basic reading, listening, speaking and writing skills. It corresponds to Level A1 (basic user) of the Common European Framework of Reference (CEFR) for Languages. Through active participation in collaborative activities, you'll develop intercultural competence, learning to navigate between cultures. Your learning will involve a communicative approach, engaging in interactive tasks and activities to enhance language proficiency. You'll be expected to prepare for classes through various exercises and utilise online and peer resources. The language learning approach will focus on linguistic and cultural practices across the francophone world.You’ll be expected to commit to approximately 12 hours of weekly study, including class time, and immerse yourself in Spanish both inside and outside the classroom to build confidence and fluency.

have_subject.name: Human Rights in Hispanophone Societies
learning_outcomes: Comprehend, reflect upon and analyse complex texts in spoken (oral) and written (language).
Produce complex texts in spoken (oral) and written (language).
Demonstrate critical cultural awareness of Spanish speaking people and societies.
have_subject.learning_and_teaching_activities: Human Rights in Hispanophone Societies blends practice-based and research-inspired learning experience. Oral and written popular texts are analysed through a communicative and reflective approach, able to stimulate progressive and continuous learning, as well as reflection on the use of language in context. The analysis is supported by a series of supplementary authentic intermedial materials (songs, films and documentaries) and scaffolded through the use of real-life scenarios and situations, such as debates. The subject is structured so as to favour the interaction of in-class and independent learning environments as well as the construction of a nuanced and personalised learning experience.In addition the in-class work, students are asked to submit research-based written texts which strengthen their independent learning skills and facilitate a customised learning experience. The approximate time commitment expected from students is 12 hours per week, including class time.
have_subject.description: This subject is designed to provide students who are proficient in reading, listening, speaking and writing in Spanish with opportunities to engage with Hispanic culture. It corresponds to Level B2+ (independent/proficient user) of the Common European Framework of Reference (CEFR) for Languages. You’ll examine and engage with the sociocultural contexts in which the language is used, specifically with regard to human rights in Hispanic cultures. Via written and oral assessments that demonstrate continuing mastery of the Spanish language, your learning will involve a genre-based approach, engaging in interactive tasks and resources to enhance language proficiency. You'll be expected to prepare for classes through various exercises and analyse a variety of authentic sources in a manner that considers their cultural provenance and role. You’ll explore subject materials and concepts through independent research, creative outputs and/or close textual analysis. The language learning approach will focus on linguistic and cultural practices across the Hispanic world. You’ll be expected to commit to approximately 12 hours of weekly study, including class time, and immerse yourself in Spanish both inside and outside the classroom to build confidence and fluency.

40005.name: Advanced iOS Development
learning_outcomes: Design well-structured solution blueprints that satisfy given software requirements within the iOS development environment. (C.1)
Develop skills and competencies in implementing a solution using Swift programming language with the iOS development environment. (D.1)
Work with teams when designing and developing iOS applications and communicate the developed solution to a wider audience. (E.1)
40005.learning_and_teaching_activities: This subject will be delivered through a series of lectures and labs. Students will attend a lecture (1.5 hours) and a lab class (1.5 hours) each week. The lecture includes various kinds of activities, such as topic/concept explanation by the lecturer, Q/A, student discussion, student presentation and software demonstration.The lectures are supported by hands-on lab sessions to complement the concepts taught during the lectures. Each lab class includes explanation of concepts with examples by the tutor and exercises for students to practise to gain better understanding of the iOS development environment for knowledge presentation, and knowledge-based reasoning and inference for better decision making.
40005.description: This subject offers an advanced insight into iOS application development with Swift and SwiftUI, where you'll master complex concepts, frameworks, and tools to create robust, innovative iOS apps. It emphasises hands-on experience with industry-standard practices, pushing you to devise solutions that address real-world software needs.

items.details: 41889 Application Development in the iOS Environment

40006.name: iOS industry Studio
learning_outcomes: Identify the social impacts of iOS applications. (B.1)
Design a solution to demonstrate software skills with SwiftUI. (C.1)
Demonstrate technical capability in software development techniques and tools with SwiftUI. (D.1)
Demonstrate communication and collaboration skills in a team to develop iOS applications. (E.1)
Critically self-review their performance to improve themselves and their teams. (F.1)
40006.learning_and_teaching_activities: The subject will be conducted via a weekly 3 hours studio session, wherein students will work in teams to develop a software based solution, under the direction of mentors, using iOS related tools with SwiftUI.The Software Development Studio (SDS) provides a learning environment similar to a workplace.Students will work in development roles appropriate to their stage of learning and experience. Students from all the SDS subjects will work in multi-year teams, progressing in responsibility as they demonstrate professional competencies, or graduate attributes.Students are expected to use the SDS outside of their class time to work in their teams. Students are also expected to write reflections about their SDS experience each week, and to participate in peer evaluations of teamwork.The semester is organised into 3 sprints and at each sprint students are required to present the progress of their team project. They will be discussing their Design Journal, Reflection Journal, Team project management in view of receiving feedback from studio mentors and to have opportunities to provide feedback and reflect on this iterative experience.
40006.description: This studio is designed to enhance your practical iOS application development skills to become professionally competent. You will participate in reflective design practices, collaborate in teams, receive mentorship and learn advanced techniques. This hands-on learning experience will provide you with a comprehensive understanding of the iOS development environment and its frameworks to deliver real-world applications.

items.details: 41889 Application Development in the iOS Environment

41889.name: Application Development in the iOS Environment
learning_outcomes: Design a well-structured programming solution that satisfies given software requirements. (C.1)
Implement the solution using Swift programming language under the iOS development environment. (D.1)
Independently explore and utilise various iOS frameworks and APIs. (D.1)
Demonstrate the fully tested software using iOS simulator for iPads and iPhones. (D.1)
41889.learning_and_teaching_activities: This subject is composed of formal lectures, video learning materials, practical programming exercises/projects, lab quizzes and a collaborative report.The lectures will present new topics on iOS each week. Live code demonstrations will be provided in the lectures to enhance students' understanding of the topic. Students are encouraged to follow the demonstrations on their own computers whenever possible. Questioning by students is actively encouraged.The video materials provide the detailed demonstration on how to use iOS development environment and focus on GUI programming. They will give student the opportunities to self study these topics prior to the lectures and the laboratory exercises.The laboratories consist of small programming exercises that help student to get practical understanding of the software development concepts delivered in the lectures. The expected results from the exercises are provided so that students can check their progresses. There will be three small quizzes in the laboratories at various stages to check students' knowledge. This ensures students acquire necessary knowledge at a consistent pace. The last three laboratories will be dedicated to group collaboration work for Project 3. Laboratory assistants will be there to facilitate the sessions, answering questions, providing comment and encourage group collaboration.The individual projects will present the students with the opportunity to practise the knowledge and the skills learned from the lectures and the laboratory exercises. The students will create small, useful, working programs independently. The projects will be assessed by both fellow students (peer assessment) and the laboratory assistants.The group project ensures the students have the capabilities to collaboratively explore the vast amount of online iOS documentation and knowledge bases and apply the knowledge into practical usage. The project also encourages the students to learn from their peer group via peer assessment.
41889.description: This subject introduces you to iOS application development, focusing on the Swift programming language and the iOS development environment. You'll gain practical experience with iOS app frameworks through hands-on software development exercises.

31777.name: Advanced Interaction Design
learning_outcomes: Use appropriate conceptual tools to accurately describe, critique, analyse and evaluate user experiences that arise through-and-during interactions with information technology. (D.1)
Identify the types of user experiences that are most appropriate for different types of human-computer interactions. (C.1)
Apply appropriate methods, concepts, and techniques to successfully design interactive technologies that can support engaging user experiences during use. (C.1)
Describe the range of social/technical and experiential concepts that are important to consider when designing technologies that can be experienced positively by users. (B.1)
Demonstrate awareness on how to design in a positive and respectful manner when working with, and for, Indigenous Australians. (A.1)
31777.learning_and_teaching_activities: This subject is linked with the postgraduate subject 32509 Advanced Interaction Design. While both cohorts will engage with the same lecture materials, undergraduate students will attend separate tutorials from the postgraduate students.Students will be asked to engage with pre-recorded video lectures on various topics and attend tutorial sessions. The pre-recorded lectures videos will allow students to learn theoretical aspects of User Experience as conceptualised, researched and supported through the design processes of Interaction Design. In addition, online lecture discussions are held to help students clarify their understanding of the video lecture materials. Tutorials will provide students with continuous guidance and formative feedback as they practice the design skills presented in the videos. The learning activities are designed to iteratively build students’ skills, confidence and understanding in the subject matter, providing them with the best possible opportunity to succeed. Then, students will work individually and with a group on various problems that contribute to their overall assessment. Again, they will receive regular guidance and feedback from their tutor.With guidance from tutors, students are required to create and maintain a personal portfolio of their work in the subject throughout the session. They will be required to choose work samples they have created that through a guided reflection, demonstrate their learning and developing knowledge of the various tools and skills required in designing for user experience.Students are expected to use various online resources, including some provided through Canvas to access materials such as prescribed readings, videos, guides, and research materials to prepare for class assignments and individual work. Accessing and reading prescribed texts, as well as completing required set tasks before class will enable students to engage more deeply and successfully in the collaborative activities of the workshop and their weekly tutorial tasks. The subject values learning-through-doing. Students will be expected to participate regularly in online discussion forum, and to collaborate with others in a session-long group assignment to solve a real-life problem through design activities.
31777.description: This subject focuses on user experience (UX) in interaction design, crucial for technology adoption and competitive edge. You'll learn conceptual tools to discuss, analyse, and critique UX with digital technologies. The subject covers a range of interactions like mobile, social, pervasive computing, and IoT, helping you understand how to design for specific user experiences in context. You'll also learn design methods like user research, sketching, prototyping, and evaluation to create technology solutions that are not only effective but engaging. By building on fundamental design concepts, you'll be equipped to design technologies that optimise user experience.

31260.name: Fundamentals of Interaction Design
learning_outcomes: Apply a range of approaches, techniques, tools, and methods available when designing useful and usable technology. (D.1)
Design digital mockup of prototype to achieve success for usability. (C.1)
Assist people through design of interconnected solutions going beyond isolated technologies. (B.1)
31260.learning_and_teaching_activities: This subject includes online lecture presentations (1 hour), which will take place via MS Teams, and face-to-face tutorial sessions (1.5 hours). The lecture presentations will allow students to learn the theoretical aspects of Interaction Design, while the tutorials will allow students to work individually and with a group on problems that contribute to their overall assessment. The learning activities are designed so that students are provided with continuous guidance and formative feedback in attempting and completing the assessment items. The activities are designed to iteratively build the students’ skills, confidence and understanding in the subject matter, providing them with the best possible opportunity to succeed. Tutorial activities in particular are designed to foster group work that involves collaborative problem solving, discussions, and learning to critique and receive feedback from others.Students are expected to use various online resources such as the UTS Library and Canvas to access materials, such as prescribed readings, videos, guides, and research materials to prepare for class assignments and individual work. Besides providing background knowledge to the lectures, some of these materials will be used in class and group discussions of key topics and critiques during tutorials. Accessing and reading prescribed texts, as well as completing the required set tasks before class will enable students to engage more deeply and successfully in the collaborative activities of the workshop and their weekly tutorial tasks. This subject values learning-through-doing. Students will be expected to collaborate with others during and outside of class on group assignments to solve a real-life problem through working together on a range of design activities.Interaction Design is all around us in everyday life, but many of us are not aware of it. Therefore, this subject highlights real-world examples of interactive systems that currently exist, have been used in the past, or will in the near future. These examples are presented in the lectures as well as forming part of the assignments and tutorial activities. For example, students will be required to look up and describe existing real-world examples of digital systems for their journal assignments, while describing them using the theory and models provided to them in the lectures and practised in the tutorials. These real-world examples not only help in demonstrating the application of Interaction Design knowledge in everyday life but also indicate to students what a professional interaction designer's job could entail.
31260.description: This subject develops your ability to design, evaluate, and implement interactive systems with a human-centered approach. You'll understand human-computer interaction (HCI) and interaction design principles, learning key concepts, tools, and techniques. You'll also consider how designers' metaphors for human activity affect system usability and how human-centered design and evaluation can enhance interactive systems.

41020.name: Human-centred Design Methods
learning_outcomes: Demonstrate knowledge of human-centred design methods. (D.1)
Demonstrate an ability to plan, design and carry out small user research projects. (C.1)
Develop an analysis of the findings from user research suitable for use in professional projects. (B.1)
Explain results and outcomes to others in their cohort and to broader audiences through paper writing, poster and video presentations. (E.1)
Apply positive and respectful ethical practices, such as the AIATSIS Code and NHMRC’s ethical code for researchers. (A.1, B.1)
41020.learning_and_teaching_activities: Learningin this subject is comprised of three elements: self-directed study, class meetings (1.5 hours, 7 times), and practical tutorials (2 hours, 11 times). First and foremost, this subject requires a significant amount of self-directed study and work outside of class hours.Self-studywill include watching videos about the subject material and reading state-of-the-art papers onhuman-centred design methods used in research, which will be made available through Canvas and the UTS Library. These should be studied as preparation before each class meeting.Theclass meetingswill be with both cohorts 32405 (PG) & 41020 (UG), and will include a mix of activities, such as presentations, Q&As, discussions, a quiz and an interview. The information offered during self-directed study and class meetings are necessary input for the students to be able to participate in the tutorials.Tutorialswill be with smaller groups,and are designed to support and guide the students through the necessary steps to implement human-centred design methods for the main deliverables. Students are encouraged to discuss, critique, and learn from each other. Tutors are available to stimulate such discussions and provide guidance and formative feedback.Interactivity, as in conversation between teaching staff and students, is important in this subject. Students are highly encouraged to ask and answer questions, be active and proactive, for example by sharing things they have learned (both in class meetings as well as tutorials). The more interactive each class meeting or tutorial is, the more the students will learn (especially the ones being interactive), but also the better the teachers can tailor the subject material to the students’ needs. Therefore, it is in the students’ interest to participate in class.Please note that:Students are expected to carry out reading for the subject before and during its delivery. It is highly recommended to read beyond the provided materials.This subject is intended to give students practical experience in user research projects using methods widely used in industry and academia. Most of the actual research and the preparation of data for analysis will be done outside of class time. In class students will be guided through the set-up of these projects, they will be prepared for a practice run, which will be discussed in class, all preparing them to head off and execute the project themselves. Again, in class it will be explained how to analyse the findings, they will try one example together, to then analyse their own findings outside of class.Students will be expected to produce work of significant depth.
41020.description: This subject focuses on making people the core of the interaction design process through human-centred design methods, with particular attention to interviewing and probes. Interviewing gathers direct information and adapts flexibly across the design process, while probes, using tailored props, require more setup but can inspire creative insights. You'll learn both methods, applying them step-by-step to real participants, guided by a brief and with readings and examples to tailor your approach. You must recruit at least three strangers (people you do not know) for interviews and one for the probe study.

40007.name: iOS Innovation Studio
learning_outcomes: Identify and engage with a range of project stakeholders to obtain domain-specific knowledge. (B.1)
Apply Design Thinking approaches to design user-friendly digital solutions to meet stakeholder requirements. (C.1)
Produce a semi-functional prototype using Swift and Xcode to implement an iOS app. (D.1)
Collaboratively articulate project milestones based on feedback and teamwork that address a real-world problem. (E.1)
Reflect throughout design and development process with feedback to justify areas of, and potential for, professional growth as IOS technologist. (F.1)
40007.learning_and_teaching_activities: The studio employs a challenge-based learning approach providing a student-centred learning experience. You will form a team to identify a real-world problem and develop solution ideas in the form of high-fidelity app concepts and semi-functional iOS app prototypes.The studio process involves 4 checkpoints where you will be required to evidence your progress of 7 deliverables throughout the semester Using ongoing formative feedback by the studio mentors and the peers play a key role in achieving learning objectives as well as opportunities to incorporate reflective practice.Some guiding videos on developing effective teamwork skills will be provided.
40007.description: This Foundational studio subject offers you a challenge-based learning environment where you will develop fundamental skills in solving real-world problems, collaborating in diverse teams, and designing and developing apps within the Apple iOS ecosystem. The subject empowers you with a skill set at the intersection of technology, creativity, and entrepreneurship. Through the studio experience, you will work in multidisciplinary cohorts mixing with peers from a range of backgrounds and experiences to work through the design thinking process to develop a semi-functional iOS prototype app.

SMJ10190.name: iOS Industry
SMJ10190.description: This sub major aims to empower students with skills in mobile application design and development using iOS app frameworks. Students learn to build applications under the iOS development environment. The taught concepts are supplemented with practical experience in iOS Studios.Students must complete 48023 Programming Fundamentals or 41039 Programming 1, and 31271 Database Fundamentals or equivalent before commencing this sub-major. It is recommended to complete 31260 Fundamentals of Interaction Design. For adding the sub major to UG Engineering courses, only students in Software Engineering major, and possibly Data Science Engineering, and Flexible Engineering can do the sub major.

contents.structure_details: Complete all of the following subjects:

have_subject.name: Advanced iOS Development
learning_outcomes: Design well-structured solution blueprints that satisfy given software requirements within the iOS development environment. (C.1)
Develop skills and competencies in implementing a solution using Swift programming language with the iOS development environment. (D.1)
Work with teams when designing and developing iOS applications and communicate the developed solution to a wider audience. (E.1)
have_subject.learning_and_teaching_activities: This subject will be delivered through a series of lectures and labs. Students will attend a lecture (1.5 hours) and a lab class (1.5 hours) each week. The lecture includes various kinds of activities, such as topic/concept explanation by the lecturer, Q/A, student discussion, student presentation and software demonstration.The lectures are supported by hands-on lab sessions to complement the concepts taught during the lectures. Each lab class includes explanation of concepts with examples by the tutor and exercises for students to practise to gain better understanding of the iOS development environment for knowledge presentation, and knowledge-based reasoning and inference for better decision making.
have_subject.description: This subject offers an advanced insight into iOS application development with Swift and SwiftUI, where you'll master complex concepts, frameworks, and tools to create robust, innovative iOS apps. It emphasises hands-on experience with industry-standard practices, pushing you to devise solutions that address real-world software needs.

items.details: 41889 Application Development in the iOS Environment

have_subject.name: iOS industry Studio
learning_outcomes: Identify the social impacts of iOS applications. (B.1)
Design a solution to demonstrate software skills with SwiftUI. (C.1)
Demonstrate technical capability in software development techniques and tools with SwiftUI. (D.1)
Demonstrate communication and collaboration skills in a team to develop iOS applications. (E.1)
Critically self-review their performance to improve themselves and their teams. (F.1)
have_subject.learning_and_teaching_activities: The subject will be conducted via a weekly 3 hours studio session, wherein students will work in teams to develop a software based solution, under the direction of mentors, using iOS related tools with SwiftUI.The Software Development Studio (SDS) provides a learning environment similar to a workplace.Students will work in development roles appropriate to their stage of learning and experience. Students from all the SDS subjects will work in multi-year teams, progressing in responsibility as they demonstrate professional competencies, or graduate attributes.Students are expected to use the SDS outside of their class time to work in their teams. Students are also expected to write reflections about their SDS experience each week, and to participate in peer evaluations of teamwork.The semester is organised into 3 sprints and at each sprint students are required to present the progress of their team project. They will be discussing their Design Journal, Reflection Journal, Team project management in view of receiving feedback from studio mentors and to have opportunities to provide feedback and reflect on this iterative experience.
have_subject.description: This studio is designed to enhance your practical iOS application development skills to become professionally competent. You will participate in reflective design practices, collaborate in teams, receive mentorship and learn advanced techniques. This hands-on learning experience will provide you with a comprehensive understanding of the iOS development environment and its frameworks to deliver real-world applications.

items.details: 41889 Application Development in the iOS Environment

have_subject.name: Application Development in the iOS Environment
learning_outcomes: Design a well-structured programming solution that satisfies given software requirements. (C.1)
Implement the solution using Swift programming language under the iOS development environment. (D.1)
Independently explore and utilise various iOS frameworks and APIs. (D.1)
Demonstrate the fully tested software using iOS simulator for iPads and iPhones. (D.1)
have_subject.learning_and_teaching_activities: This subject is composed of formal lectures, video learning materials, practical programming exercises/projects, lab quizzes and a collaborative report.The lectures will present new topics on iOS each week. Live code demonstrations will be provided in the lectures to enhance students' understanding of the topic. Students are encouraged to follow the demonstrations on their own computers whenever possible. Questioning by students is actively encouraged.The video materials provide the detailed demonstration on how to use iOS development environment and focus on GUI programming. They will give student the opportunities to self study these topics prior to the lectures and the laboratory exercises.The laboratories consist of small programming exercises that help student to get practical understanding of the software development concepts delivered in the lectures. The expected results from the exercises are provided so that students can check their progresses. There will be three small quizzes in the laboratories at various stages to check students' knowledge. This ensures students acquire necessary knowledge at a consistent pace. The last three laboratories will be dedicated to group collaboration work for Project 3. Laboratory assistants will be there to facilitate the sessions, answering questions, providing comment and encourage group collaboration.The individual projects will present the students with the opportunity to practise the knowledge and the skills learned from the lectures and the laboratory exercises. The students will create small, useful, working programs independently. The projects will be assessed by both fellow students (peer assessment) and the laboratory assistants.The group project ensures the students have the capabilities to collaboratively explore the vast amount of online iOS documentation and knowledge bases and apply the knowledge into practical usage. The project also encourages the students to learn from their peer group via peer assessment.
have_subject.description: This subject introduces you to iOS application development, focusing on the Swift programming language and the iOS development environment. You'll gain practical experience with iOS app frameworks through hands-on software development exercises.

contents.structure_details: Select 6 credit points from the following subjects:

have_subject.name: Advanced Interaction Design
learning_outcomes: Use appropriate conceptual tools to accurately describe, critique, analyse and evaluate user experiences that arise through-and-during interactions with information technology. (D.1)
Identify the types of user experiences that are most appropriate for different types of human-computer interactions. (C.1)
Apply appropriate methods, concepts, and techniques to successfully design interactive technologies that can support engaging user experiences during use. (C.1)
Describe the range of social/technical and experiential concepts that are important to consider when designing technologies that can be experienced positively by users. (B.1)
Demonstrate awareness on how to design in a positive and respectful manner when working with, and for, Indigenous Australians. (A.1)
have_subject.learning_and_teaching_activities: This subject is linked with the postgraduate subject 32509 Advanced Interaction Design. While both cohorts will engage with the same lecture materials, undergraduate students will attend separate tutorials from the postgraduate students.Students will be asked to engage with pre-recorded video lectures on various topics and attend tutorial sessions. The pre-recorded lectures videos will allow students to learn theoretical aspects of User Experience as conceptualised, researched and supported through the design processes of Interaction Design. In addition, online lecture discussions are held to help students clarify their understanding of the video lecture materials. Tutorials will provide students with continuous guidance and formative feedback as they practice the design skills presented in the videos. The learning activities are designed to iteratively build students’ skills, confidence and understanding in the subject matter, providing them with the best possible opportunity to succeed. Then, students will work individually and with a group on various problems that contribute to their overall assessment. Again, they will receive regular guidance and feedback from their tutor.With guidance from tutors, students are required to create and maintain a personal portfolio of their work in the subject throughout the session. They will be required to choose work samples they have created that through a guided reflection, demonstrate their learning and developing knowledge of the various tools and skills required in designing for user experience.Students are expected to use various online resources, including some provided through Canvas to access materials such as prescribed readings, videos, guides, and research materials to prepare for class assignments and individual work. Accessing and reading prescribed texts, as well as completing required set tasks before class will enable students to engage more deeply and successfully in the collaborative activities of the workshop and their weekly tutorial tasks. The subject values learning-through-doing. Students will be expected to participate regularly in online discussion forum, and to collaborate with others in a session-long group assignment to solve a real-life problem through design activities.
have_subject.description: This subject focuses on user experience (UX) in interaction design, crucial for technology adoption and competitive edge. You'll learn conceptual tools to discuss, analyse, and critique UX with digital technologies. The subject covers a range of interactions like mobile, social, pervasive computing, and IoT, helping you understand how to design for specific user experiences in context. You'll also learn design methods like user research, sketching, prototyping, and evaluation to create technology solutions that are not only effective but engaging. By building on fundamental design concepts, you'll be equipped to design technologies that optimise user experience.

have_subject.name: Cloud Computing and Software as a Service
learning_outcomes: Articulate the need for cloud computing, elements of cloud computing for e-business, e-government, e-banking, e-health and e-learning. (B.1)
Describe the architecture of different types of Cloud Services (IaaS, PaaS and SaaS), different types of Virtualization and their industrial use. (D.1)
Critically understand the current issues in Cloud Computing for informed business decision making. (D.1)
Create a Cloud Computing prototye that will be on a Cloud platform for areas such as e-government, e-banking, e-health and e-learning. (C.1)
have_subject.learning_and_teaching_activities: This subject will consist of two formal contact sessions per week: 1.5 hours of lecture supplemented by 1.5 hours of lab/tutorial work. The teaching and learning approach used in this subject includes theoretical discussion of topics (during the lectures) supplemented with practical exercises and example (during the labs or tutorials). There will be 10 weekly 1.5 hours classes. The tutorials will consist of a range of critical thinking and collaborative tasks, including group discussions and group presentations. Students are required to form groups (of three) and work as a group during the tutorials on the tutorial case study. During the tutorial students will receive verbal feedback from the tutor (and other fellow students). Students may choose to extend the formed tutorial group for the assignment. In the labs each week students are required to program or build a mini-application in the Cloud. Students can choose to work either in a group or individually during the labs. Similar to the tutorials, students will receive verbal feedback from the tutor specifically on their lab work. Both the tutorial and labs will focus on concepts covered during the classes in the week before.The subject coordinator/lecturer will release content on certain topics, for pre-reading by the students prior to coming to the class. Students are expected to consult the program section and peruse through the pre-reading section prior to coming to the lecture, labs and tutorials. Students bring conceptual understandings of pre-reading material to classes, tutorials and labs to enable deeper understanding through discussions and feedback.The lectures, tutorials and labs will build on the pre-readings provided to the students. All the pre-reading will be made available on Canvas.During Week 1, students are expected to view the videos made available by the subject coordinator on Canvas. Subsequently, in order to provide the students with feedback on their understanding of the content covered during the preparation, they will be required to complete an online quiz. Feedback provided is automated and numerical.
have_subject.description: This subject introduces you to cloud computing, covering cloud architecture, IaaS, PaaS, SaaS, virtualisation, and multi-tenancy. You'll engage in group work to produce a research report critically analysing a cloud computing aspect, followed by developing cloud-based software engineering skills on an existing platform. The second assignment involves group collaboration to create a cloud-based software application.

items.details: 42904 Cloud Computing and Software as a Service

requisite.rule: Requisite rule: ((1 OR 1a OR 1b OR 1c OR 1d OR 1e))

have_subject.name: Fundamentals of Interaction Design
learning_outcomes: Apply a range of approaches, techniques, tools, and methods available when designing useful and usable technology. (D.1)
Design digital mockup of prototype to achieve success for usability. (C.1)
Assist people through design of interconnected solutions going beyond isolated technologies. (B.1)
have_subject.learning_and_teaching_activities: This subject includes online lecture presentations (1 hour), which will take place via MS Teams, and face-to-face tutorial sessions (1.5 hours). The lecture presentations will allow students to learn the theoretical aspects of Interaction Design, while the tutorials will allow students to work individually and with a group on problems that contribute to their overall assessment. The learning activities are designed so that students are provided with continuous guidance and formative feedback in attempting and completing the assessment items. The activities are designed to iteratively build the students’ skills, confidence and understanding in the subject matter, providing them with the best possible opportunity to succeed. Tutorial activities in particular are designed to foster group work that involves collaborative problem solving, discussions, and learning to critique and receive feedback from others.Students are expected to use various online resources such as the UTS Library and Canvas to access materials, such as prescribed readings, videos, guides, and research materials to prepare for class assignments and individual work. Besides providing background knowledge to the lectures, some of these materials will be used in class and group discussions of key topics and critiques during tutorials. Accessing and reading prescribed texts, as well as completing the required set tasks before class will enable students to engage more deeply and successfully in the collaborative activities of the workshop and their weekly tutorial tasks. This subject values learning-through-doing. Students will be expected to collaborate with others during and outside of class on group assignments to solve a real-life problem through working together on a range of design activities.Interaction Design is all around us in everyday life, but many of us are not aware of it. Therefore, this subject highlights real-world examples of interactive systems that currently exist, have been used in the past, or will in the near future. These examples are presented in the lectures as well as forming part of the assignments and tutorial activities. For example, students will be required to look up and describe existing real-world examples of digital systems for their journal assignments, while describing them using the theory and models provided to them in the lectures and practised in the tutorials. These real-world examples not only help in demonstrating the application of Interaction Design knowledge in everyday life but also indicate to students what a professional interaction designer's job could entail.
have_subject.description: This subject develops your ability to design, evaluate, and implement interactive systems with a human-centered approach. You'll understand human-computer interaction (HCI) and interaction design principles, learning key concepts, tools, and techniques. You'll also consider how designers' metaphors for human activity affect system usability and how human-centered design and evaluation can enhance interactive systems.

have_subject.name: Human-centred Design Methods
learning_outcomes: Demonstrate knowledge of human-centred design methods. (D.1)
Demonstrate an ability to plan, design and carry out small user research projects. (C.1)
Develop an analysis of the findings from user research suitable for use in professional projects. (B.1)
Explain results and outcomes to others in their cohort and to broader audiences through paper writing, poster and video presentations. (E.1)
Apply positive and respectful ethical practices, such as the AIATSIS Code and NHMRC’s ethical code for researchers. (A.1, B.1)

First and foremost, this subject requires a significant amount of self-directed study and work outside of class hours.Self-studywill include watching videos about the subject material and reading state-of-the-art papers onhuman-centred design methods used in research, which will be made available through Canvas and the UTS Library. These should be studied as preparation before each class meeting.Theclass meetingswill be with both cohorts 32405 (PG) & 41020 (UG), and will include a mix of activities, such as presentations, Q&As, discussions, a quiz and an interview. The information offered during self-directed study and class meetings are necessary input for the students to be able to participate in the tutorials.Tutorialswill be with smaller groups,and are designed to support and guide the students through the necessary steps to implement human-centred design methods for the main deliverables. Students are encouraged to discuss, critique, and learn from each other. Tutors are available to stimulate such discussions and provide guidance and formative feedback.Interactivity, as in conversation between teaching staff and students, is important in this subject. The more interactive each class meeting or tutorial is, the more the students will learn (especially the ones being interactive), but also the better the teachers can tailor the subject material to the students’ needs. Therefore, it is in the students’ interest to participate in class.Please note that:Students are expected to carry out reading for the subject before and during its delivery. It is highly recommended to read beyond the provided materials.This subject is intended to give students practical experience in user research projects using methods widely used in industry and academia. In class students will be guided through the set-up of these projects, they will be prepared for a practice run, which will be discussed in class, all preparing them to head off and execute the project themselves. Again, in class it will be explained how to analyse the findings, they will try one example together, to then analyse their own findings outside of class.Students will be expected to produce work of significant depth.

Interviewing gathers direct information and adapts flexibly across the design process, while probes, using tailored props, require more setup but can inspire creative insights. You'll learn both methods, applying them step-by-step to real participants, guided by a brief and with readings and examples to tailor your approach. You must recruit at least three strangers (people you do not know) for interviews and one for the probe study.

learning_outcomes: Identify and engage with a range of project stakeholders to obtain domain-specific knowledge. (B.1)
Apply Design Thinking approaches to design user-friendly digital solutions to meet stakeholder requirements. (C.1)
Produce a semi-functional prototype using Swift and Xcode to implement an iOS app. (D.1)
Collaboratively articulate project milestones based on feedback and teamwork that address a real-world problem. (E.1)
Reflect throughout design and development process with feedback to justify areas of, and potential for, professional growth as IOS technologist. (F.1)

You will form a team to identify a real-world problem and develop solution ideas in the form of high-fidelity app concepts and semi-functional iOS app prototypes.The studio process involves 4 checkpoints where you will be required to evidence your progress of 7 deliverables throughout the semester Using ongoing formative feedback by the studio mentors and the peers play a key role in achieving learning objectives as well as opportunities to incorporate reflective practice.Some guiding videos on developing effective teamwork skills will be provided.

The subject empowers you with a skill set at the intersection of technology, creativity, and entrepreneurship. Through the studio experience, you will work in multidisciplinary cohorts mixing with peers from a range of backgrounds and experiences to work through the design thinking process to develop a semi-functional iOS prototype app.

SMJ09035.name: Language other than English
SMJ09035.description: The LOTE sub-major enables students to develop communicative competence in a language other than English. Three consecutive 8 credit point subjects are taken from one of the following language programs: Chinese, French, German, Italian, Japanese or Spanish. Students require three semesters to complete this sub-major and are advised to start in Autumn of their second year, as not all levels of language and culture are offered every semester.

contents.structure_details: Select one of the following language and culture options:

have_sub_structures.structure_details: (CBK90494) Select 24 credit points from the following subjects:

have_subject.name: Chinese Business and Administrative Language
learning_outcomes: Select and deploy register, rhetoric, genre, concept and specialist vocabulary through exposure to business and administrative writings of a wide range of genres and purposes
Communicate professionally in writing and speech in Chinese through competent use of specific and advanced linguistic skills and devices
Develop awareness of when to deploy various Chinese and business cross-cultural communication practices
have_subject.learning_and_teaching_activities: The subject utilises flipped and hybrid teaching and learning strategies and both online and face-to-face delivery modes. Students use online and face-to-face class time for interactive practice and advancement of linguistic skills through personalised learning and group discussions, practices and/or presentations in flexible but guided and monitored physical or virtual classroom activities. Students also deepen their understanding of professional practices, theories and concepts and clarify subject content.Preparation for these activities requires students to complete exercises and reading/listening/viewing activities prior to coming to class and for them to make effective use of subject materials (including those posted online), community and peer resources available to them to support their learning.Students will customise their learning experience by engaging with Chinese business and professional language and practices from intercultural perspectives, and tailoring their practice and learning to maximise understanding and appreciation of Chinese business and administrative systems, customs and common practices.Students are encouraged to explore subject materials and concepts through independent research, creative outputs and/or close textual analysis.
have_subject.description: This subject equips you with specialised proficiency in Modern Standard Chinese, focusing on business and administrative communication. You'll master formal writing styles, expand your business vocabulary, and enhance speaking skills through practical assessments, preparing you for cross-cultural business environments. Learning combines online and in-person methods, including interactive practice and group work, to advance your linguistic abilities. You'll engage with Chinese business language and practices, customising your learning to understand and appreciate the nuances of Chinese professional settings. Independent research and creative analysis further deepen your knowledge, making you more culturally aware and employable internationally.

have_subject.name: Chinese Culture and Heritage
learning_outcomes: Comprehend and respond to complex texts in oral and written Chinese.
Produce complex texts in oral and written Chinese.
Demonstrate intercultural awareness across two or more cultural contexts.
have_subject.learning_and_teaching_activities: This subject uses a communicative approach in class to maximise students' potential for effective communication in the target language. A series of activities such as interactive activities working in pairs, small and large groups and/or presentations, are promoted in class to enhance students' engagement through content-based learning. Preparation for class activities requires students to complete exercises and reading/listening/viewing activities prior to class to make effective use of resources and subject material (including resources online), community and peer resources available to them to support their learning.Students are encouraged to customise their learning experience as per the Global Chinese approach to promote their engagement with Chinese peoples and societies. This entails keeping in mind their goals for the use of Chinese (for instance studying abroad or travel in a particular country or region) and tailoring their practice and learning to maximize exposure to local variants of the language, relevant cultural norms and authentic materials from the area.As part of an immersive approach to language education, students are encouraged to use the target language within the classroom environment and to build confidence to use the target language outside the classroom. The approximate time commitment expected from students is 12 hours per week, including class time.
have_subject.description: This subject is designed to provide students who are proficient in reading, listening, speaking and writing in Chinese with opportunities to engage with Chinese culture. It corresponds to Level 4 of the Chinese Proficiency Test (HSK). You’ll examine and engage with the sociocultural contexts in which the language is used, specifically with regard to key cultural aspects such as philosophy, religion, art, and fashion in the Chinese world.Via written and oral assessments that demonstrate continuing mastery of the Chinese language, your learning will involve a communicative approach, engaging in interactive tasks and resources to enhance language proficiency. You'll be expected to prepare for classes through various exercises and analyse a variety of authentic sources in a manner that considers their cultural provenance and role. You’ll explore subject materials and concepts through independent research, creative outputs and/or close textual analysis. The language learning approach will focus on linguistic and cultural practices across the Chineseworld.You’ll be expected to commit to approximately 12 hours of weekly study, including class time, and immerse yourself in Chinese both inside and outside the classroom to build confidence and fluency.

have_subject.name: Chinese Festivals and Ceremonies
learning_outcomes: Comprehend and respond to complex texts in oral and written Chinese.
Produce complex texts in oral and written Chinese.
Demonstrate intercultural awareness across two or more cultural contexts.
have_subject.learning_and_teaching_activities: This subject uses a communicative approach in class to maximise students' potential for effective communication in the target language. A series of activities such as interactive activities working in pairs, small and large groups and/or presentations, are promoted in class to enhance students' engagement through content-based learning. Preparation for class activities requires students to complete exercises and reading/listening/viewing activities prior to class to make effective use of resources and subject material (including resources online), community and peer resources available to them to support their learning.Students are encouraged to customise their learning experience as per the Global Chinese approach to promote their engagement with Chinese peoples and societies. This entails keeping in mind their goals for the use of Chinese (for instance studying abroad or travel in a particular country or region) and tailoring their practice and learning to maximize exposure to local variants of the language, relevant cultural norms and authentic materials from the area.As part of an immersive approach to language education, students are encouraged to use the target language within the classroom environment and to build confidence to use the target language outside the classroom. The approximate time commitment expected from students is 12 hours per week, including class time.
have_subject.description: This subject is designed to provide students who are proficient in reading, listening, speaking and writing in Chinese with opportunities to engage with Chinese culture. It corresponds to Level 3/4 of the Chinese Proficiency Test (HSK). You’ll examine and engage with the sociocultural contexts in which the language is used, specifically with regard to festivals and ceremonies in the Chinese world.Via written and oral assessments that demonstrate continuing mastery of the Chinese language, your learning will involve a communicative approach, engaging in interactive tasks and resources to enhance language proficiency. You'll be expected to prepare for classes through various exercises and analyse a variety of authentic sources in a manner that considers their cultural provenance and role. You’ll explore subject materials and concepts through independent research, creative outputs and/or close textual analysis. The language learning approach will focus on linguistic and cultural practices across the Chinese world.You’ll be expected to commit to approximately 12 hours of weekly study, including class time, and immerse yourself in Chinese both inside and outside the classroom to build confidence and fluency.

have_subject.name: Chinese Language and Culture 1
learning_outcomes: Comprehend and respond to basic texts in oral and written Chinese.
Produce basic texts in oral and written Chinese.
Demonstrate intercultural awareness across two or more cultural contexts.
have_subject.learning_and_teaching_activities: This subject uses a communicative approach in class to maximise students' potential for effective communication in the target language. A series of activities such as interactive activities working in pairs, small and large groups and/or presentations, are promoted in class to enhance students' engagement through content-based learning. Preparation for class activities requires students to complete exercises and reading/listening/viewing activities prior to class to make effective use of resources and subject material (including resources online), community and peer resources available to them to support their learning.Students are encouraged to customise their learning experience as per the Global Chinese approach to promote their engagement with Chinese peoples and societies. This entails keeping in mind their goals for the use of Chinese (for instance studying abroad or travel in a particular country or region) and tailoring their practice and learning to maximise exposure to local variants of the language, relevant cultural norms and authentic materials from the area.As part of an immersive approach to language education, students are encouraged to use the target language within the classroom environment and to build confidence to use the target language outside the classroom. The approximate time commitment expected from students is 12 hours per week, including class time.
have_subject.description: This subject is designed to provide students who have no prior knowledge of the Chinese language with basic reading, listening, speaking and writing skills. You'll learn Pinyin for pronunciation and acquire your vocabulary by around 200 words. Through active participation in collaborative activities, you'll develop intercultural competence, learning to navigate between cultures.Your learning will involve a communicative approach, engaging in interactive tasks and activities to enhance language proficiency. You will be expected to prepare for classes through various exercises and utilise online and peer resources. have_subject.name: Communicating About Chinese Media
learning_outcomes: Develop awareness of register, rhetoric, genre, concept and specialist vocabulary through exposure to a wide variety of media and academic readings
Communicate professionally and academically in writing and speech in Chinese through competent use of specific linguistic skills and devices
Reflect on and analyse critically the impact of social issues as seen from media sources on the Chinese society and its people.
have_subject.learning_and_teaching_activities: The subject utilises flipped and hybrid teaching and learning strategies and both online and face-to-face delivery modes. Students use online and face-to-face class time for interactive practice and advancement of linguistic skills through personalised learning and group discussions, practices and/or presentations in flexible but guided and monitored physical or virtual classroom activities. Students also deepen their understanding of professional practices, theories and concepts and clarify subject content.Preparation for these activities requires students to complete exercises and reading/listening/viewing activities prior to coming to class and for them to make effective use of subject materials (including those posted online), community and peer resources available to them to support their learning.Students will customise their learning experience by engaging with Chinese media and their language, culture and practices from intercultural perspectives, and tailoring their learning experience to maximise understanding and appreciation of Chinese history, culture and society.Students are encouraged to explore subject materials and concepts through independent research, creative outputs and/or close textual analysis.
have_subject.description: This subject equips you with advanced Chinese linguistic skills, preparing you for careers in Chinese-language media and critical reflection on media practices. You'll master crafting nuanced texts, employing specialised vocabulary and rhetoric, and analysing contemporary China through diverse media sources. Learning combines online and in-person methods, including interactive practice and group work, with a focus on personalised and collaborative learning. Prior to classes, you'll engage with preparatory exercises and materials to enhance in-class discussions and presentations. You'll also tailor your studies to your interests, exploring Chinese media, culture, and society through independent research and creative analysis, fostering a deep intercultural understanding.

have_subject.name: Developing Chinese Reading and Writing Skills 1
learning_outcomes: Comprehend and respond to basic texts in written modern standard Chinese.
Produce basic texts in written modern standard Chinese.
Demonstrate cultural awareness of Chinese-speaking peoples and societies.
have_subject.learning_and_teaching_activities: A communicative approach is used in class to maximise students' potential for effective communication in the target language. A series of activities are used in this process. These include interactive activities where students work in pairs, small and large groups and/or presentations, and which are promoted in class to enhance students' engagement through content-based learning. Preparation for class activities requires students to complete exercises and reading/listening/viewing activities prior to class. Prior engagement with subject resources and materials facilitates student learning and a sense of community in the classroom.Students are encouraged to customise their learning experience as per the Global Chinese approach to promote their engagement with Chinese peoples and societies. This entails keeping in mind their goals for the use of Chinese (for instance studying abroad or travel in a particular country or region) and tailoring their practice and learning to maximize exposure to local variants of the language, relevant cultural norms and authentic materials from the area.As part of an immersive approach to language education, students are encouraged to use the target language within the classroom environment. This immersive approach will build students’ confidence when using the language outside the classroom. The approximate time commitment expected from students is 12 hours per week, including class time.
have_subject.description: This subject is designed to provide students who have a good command of spoken Chinese, but have little prior knowledge of the written Chinese language with basic reading and writing skills. It corresponds to Level 1 of the Chinese Proficiency Test (HSK). You'll acquire your vocabulary by around 500 words and learn Chinese radicals, which are an important element of the Chinese writing system. Through active participation in collaborative activities, you'll develop intercultural competence, learning to navigate between cultures.Your learning will involve a communicative approach, engaging in interactive tasks and activities to enhance language proficiency. You'll be expected to prepare for classes through various exercises and utilise online and peer resources. The language learning approach will focus on linguistic and cultural practices across the Chinese world.You’ll be expected to commit to approximately 12 hours of weekly study, including class time, and immerse yourself in Chinese both inside and outside the classroom to build confidence and fluency.

items.details: 97114 Developing Chinese Reading and Writing Skills 1

have_subject.name: Language in Chinese Screen Studies
learning_outcomes: Develop awareness of register, rhetoric, genre, concept and specialist vocabulary through exposure to contemporary Chinese movies and a wide variety of academic and professional readings
Communicate professionally and academically in writing and speech in Chinese through competent use of specific linguistic skills and devices
Analyse cinematic representation of Chinese social, cultural and political themes;
Develop basic writing skills to prepare a movie synopsis, storyboard, screen play and film reviews
have_subject.learning_and_teaching_activities: The subject utilises flipped and hybrid teaching and learning strategies and both online and face-to-face delivery modes. Students use online and face-to-face class time for interactive practice and advancement of linguistic skills through personalised learning and group discussions, practices and/or presentations in flexible but guided and monitored physical or virtual classroom activities. Students also deepen their understanding of professional practices, theories and concepts and clarify subject content.Preparation for these activities requires students to complete exercises and reading/listening/viewing activities prior to coming to class and for them to make effective use of subject materials (including those posted online), community and peer resources available to them to support their learning.Students will customise their learning experience by engaging with Chinese film industry and its culture and practices from intercultural perspectives, and tailoring their learning experience to maximise understanding and appreciation of Chinese history, culture and society.Students are encouraged to explore subject materials and concepts through independent research, creative outputs and/or close textual analysis.
have_subject.description: This subject equips you with advanced Modern Standard Chinese skills, focusing on specialised vocabulary and linguistic nuances relevant to film analysis. You'll gain proficiency in evaluating Chinese cinema within its socio-economic milieu, mastering technical language in both Chinese and English to critique films and script your mini screenplays. Learning is interactive, blending online and in-person sessions for practice and discussion, while independent study and resource utilisation enhance your grasp of professional practices and theoretical concepts. You'll tailor your experience by exploring the Chinese film industry's cultural context, fostering a deeper appreciation of China's heritage and societal norms through independent research and creative engagement.

have_sub_structures.structure_details: (CBK90497) Select 24 credit points from the following subjects:

have_subject.name: Francophone Cultures of Consumption
learning_outcomes: Comprehend and respond to complex texts in oral and written French.
Produce texts in oral and written French that show conceptual depth and an advanced command of the French language.
Demonstrate intercultural awareness across two or more cultural contexts.
have_subject.learning_and_teaching_activities: This subject uses a communicative approach in class to maximise students' potential for effective communication in the target language. A series of activities such as interactive activities working in pairs, small and large groups and/or presentations, are promoted in class to enhance students' engagement through content-based learning. Preparation for class activities requires students to complete exercises and reading/listening/viewing activities prior to class to make effective use of resources and subject material (including resources online), community and peer resources available to them to support their learning.Students are encouraged to customise their learning experience as per the Global French approach to promote their engagement with francophone peoples and societies. This entails keeping in mind their goals for the use of French (for instance studying abroad or travel in a particular country or region) and tailoring their practice and learning to maximize exposure to local variants of the language, relevant cultural norms and authentic materials from the area.As part of an immersive approach to language education, students are encouraged to use the target language within the classroom environment and to build confidence to use the target language outside the classroom. The approximate time commitment expected from students is 12 hours per week, including class time.
have_subject.description: This subject is designed to provide students who are proficient in reading, listening, speaking and writing in French with opportunities to engage with francophone culture. It corresponds to Level B2/C1 (independent/proficient user) of the Common European Framework of Reference (CEFR) for Languages. You’ll examine and engage with the sociocultural contexts in which the language is used, specifically with regard to questions about consumption in the francophone world.Via written, oral and aural assessments that demonstrate continuing mastery of the French language, your learning will involve a communicative approach, engaging in interactive tasks and using a variety of resources to enhance language proficiency. You'll be expected to prepare for classes through various exercises and analyse a variety of authentic sources in a manner that considers their cultural provenance and role. You’ll explore subject materials and concepts through independent research, creative outputs and/or close textual analysis. The language learning approach will focus on linguistic and cultural practices across the francophone world.You’ll be expected to commit to approximately 12 hours of weekly study, including class time, and immerse yourself in French both inside and outside the classroom to build confidence and fluency.

items.details: 97408 Show and Tell: Francophone Cultures on Display
97410 Places and Spaces of the Francophone World

have_subject.name: Francophone Identities in Conflict
learning_outcomes: Comprehend and respond to complex texts in oral and written French.
Produce complex texts in oral and written French.
Demonstrate intercultural awareness across two or more cultural contexts.
have_subject.learning_and_teaching_activities: Class time is used for interactive practice and advancement of skills through work in pairs, small and large groups and/or presentations, as well as clarification of subject content. Students will spend a substantial portion of each lesson in dialogue with their peers and their teacher.Preparation for these activities requires students to complete exercises and reading/listening/viewing activities prior to coming to class and for them to make effective use of subject materials (including UTS Online), community and peer resources available to them to support their learning. As this is an 8 credit point subject, the approximate time commitment expected from students is 12 hours per week, including class time.Students will customise their learning experience as per the Global French approach to promote their engagement with francophone peoples and societies. This entails keeping in mind one's goals for the use of French (for instance studying abroad or travel in a particular country or region) and tailoring one's practice and learning to maximise exposure to local variants of the language, relevant cultural norms and authentic materials from the area.Students are strongly encouraged to prioritise the use of French when communicating with each other or with their teacher in class.Students are encouraged to explore subject materials and concepts through independent research, creative outputs and/or close textual analysis.
have_subject.description: This subject is designed to provide students who are proficient in reading, listening, speaking and writing in French with opportunities to engage with francophone culture. It corresponds to Level B2/C1 (independent/proficient user) of the Common European Framework of Reference (CEFR) for Languages. You’ll examine and engage with the sociocultural contexts in which the language is used, specifically with regard to questions of history and conflict in the francophone world.Via written, oral and aural assessments that demonstrate continuing mastery of the French language, your learning will involve a communicative approach, engaging in interactive tasks and using a variety of resources to enhance language proficiency. You'll be expected to prepare for classes through various exercises and analyse a variety of authentic sources in a manner that considers their cultural provenance and role. You’ll explore subject materials and concepts through independent research, creative outputs and/or close textual analysis. The language learning approach will focus on linguistic and cultural practices across the francophone world.You’ll be expected to commit to approximately 12 hours of weekly study, including class time, and immerse yourself in French both inside and outside the classroom to build confidence and fluency.

Preparation for class activities requires students to complete exercises and reading/listening/viewing activities prior to class to make effective use of resources and subject material (including resources online), community and peer resources available to them to support their learning.Students are encouraged to customise their learning experience as per the Global French approach to promote their engagement with francophone peoples and societies. This entails keeping in mind their goals for the use of French (for instance studying abroad or travel in a particular country or region) and tailoring their practice and learning to maximize exposure to local variants of the language, relevant cultural norms and authentic materials from the area.As part of an immersive approach to language education, students are encouraged to use the target language within the classroom environment and to build confidence to use the target language outside the classroom. The approximate time commitment expected from students is 12 hours per week, including class time.
