have_subject.name: Places and Spaces of the Francophone World
learning_outcomes: Comprehend and respond to complex texts in oral and written French.
Produce texts in oral and written French that show conceptual depth and an advanced command of the French language.
Demonstrate intercultural awareness across two or more cultural contexts.
have_subject.learning_and_teaching_activities: Class time is used for interactive practice and advancement of skills through work in pairs, small and large groups and/or presentations, as well as clarification of subject content. Students will spend a substantial portion of each lesson in dialogue with their peers and their teacher.Preparation for these activities requires students to complete exercises and reading/listening/viewing activities prior to coming to class and for them to make effective use of subject materials (including UTS Online), community and peer resources available to them to support their learning. As this is an 8 credit point subject, the approximate time commitment expected from students is 12 hours per week, including class time.Students will customise their learning experience as per the Global French approach to promote their engagement with francophone peoples and societies. This entails keeping in mind one's goals for the use of French (for instance studying abroad or travel in a particular country or region) and tailoring one's practice and learning to maximise exposure to local variants of the language, relevant cultural norms and authentic materials from the area.As part of an immersive approach to language education, students are expected to use only French when communicating with each other or with their teacher in class.Students are encouraged to explore subject materials and concepts through independent research, creative outputs and/or close textual analysis.
have_subject.description: This subject is designed to provide students who are proficient in reading, listening, speaking and writing in French with opportunities to engage with francophone culture. It corresponds to Level B2/C1 (independent/proficient user) of the Common European Framework of Reference (CEFR) for Languages. You’ll examine and engage with the sociocultural contexts in which the language is used, specifically with regard to different physical and non-physical places and spaces in the francophone world.Via written, oral and aural assessments that demonstrate continuing mastery of the French language, your learning will involve a communicative approach, engaging in interactive tasks and using a variety of resources to enhance language proficiency. You'll be expected to prepare for classes through various exercises and analyse a variety of authentic sources in a manner that considers their cultural provenance and role. You’ll explore subject materials and concepts through independent research, creative outputs and/or close textual analysis. The language learning approach will focus on linguistic and cultural practices across the francophone world.You’ll be expected to commit to approximately 12 hours of weekly study, including class time, and immerse yourself in French both inside and outside the classroom to build confidence and fluency.

have_sub_structures.structure_details: (CBK90495) Select 24 credit points from the following subjects:

have_subject.name: Japanese Films and Popular Culture
learning_outcomes: Comprehend and respond to intermediate–advanced level texts in oral and written language.
Produce intermediate-advanced level texts in oral and written language.
Demonstrate intercultural awareness across two or more cultural contexts.
have_subject.learning_and_teaching_activities: This subject proposes a blended, practice-based and research-inspired learning experience. Japanese pop culture and films are analysed through a communicative and reflective approach to stimulate progressive and continuous learning of the target language. A series of authetic materials (books, articles and videos) are used to promote the use of real-life scenarios and situations such as interviews and discussion.Students are encouraged to customise their learning experience as per the Global Japanese approach to promote their engagement with Japanese peoples and societies. This entails keeping in mind their goals for the use of Japanese (for instance studying abroad or travel in a particular country or region) and tailoring their practice and learning to maximize exposure to local variants of the language, relevant cultural norms and authentic materials from the area.In addition to the in-class work, students are expected to complete a series of online activities, which strengthen their independent learning skills and facilitate a customised learning experience. The approximate time commitment expected from students is 12 hours per week, including class time.
have_subject.description: This subject is designed to provide students who are proficient in reading, listening, speaking and writing in Japanese with opportunities to engage with Japanese-speaking culture. It corresponds to N2/N1 of the Japanese Language Proficiency Test (JLPT) or Level B2/C1 (independent/proficient user) of the Common European Framework of Reference (CEFR) for Languages. You’ll examine the sociocultural contexts in which the Japanese language is used, focusing on the influence of cinema and other forms of popular culture on society. Via written and oral assessments that demonstrate continuing mastery of the Japanese language, your learning will involve a communicative approach, engaging in interactive tasks and resources to enhance language proficiency. You'll be expected to prepare for classes through various exercises and analyse a variety of authentic sources in a manner that considers their cultural provenance and role. You’ll explore subject materials and concepts through independent research, creative outputs and/or close textual analysis. The language learning approach will focus on linguistic and cultural practices across the Japanese-speaking world. You’ll be expected to commit to approximately 12 hours of weekly study, including class time, and immerse yourself in Japanese both inside and outside the classroom to build confidence and fluency.

have_subject.learning_and_teaching_activities: Flipped learning: Class time is used for interactive practice and advancement of skills through work in pairs, small and large groups and/or presentations, as well as clarification of subject content. Preparation for these activities requires students to complete exercises and reading/listening/viewing activities prior to coming to class and for them to make effective use of resources supplied to support their learning. As this is an 8 credit point subject, the approximate time commitment expected from students is 12 hours per week, including class time.
have_subject.description: This subject is designed to provide students who are proficient in reading, listening, speaking and writing in Spanish with opportunities to engage with Hispanic culture. It corresponds to Level C1 (independent/proficient user) of the Common European Framework of Reference (CEFR) for Languages. You’ll examine and engage with the sociocultural contexts in which the language is used, specifically with regard diaspora of the Hispanic imagination. Via written and oral assessments that demonstrate continuing mastery of the Spanish language, your learning will involve a genre-based approach, engaging in interactive tasks and resources to enhance language proficiency. You'll be expected to prepare for classes through various exercises and analyse a variety of authentic sources in a manner that considers their cultural provenance and role. You’ll explore subject materials and concepts through independent research, creative outputs and/or close textual analysis. The language learning approach will focus on linguistic and cultural practices across the Hispanic world. You’ll be expected to commit to approximately 12 hours of weekly study, including class time, and immerse yourself in Spanish both inside and outside the classroom to build confidence and fluency.

Oral and written popular texts are analysed through a communicative and reflective approach, able to stimulate progressive and continuous learning, as well as reflection on the use of language in context. The analysis is supported by a series of supplementary authentic intermedial materials (songs, films and documentaries) and scaffolded through the use of real-life scenarios and situations, such as debates. The subject is structured so as to favour the interaction of in-class and independent learning environments as well as the construction of a nuanced and personalised learning experience.In addition the in-class work, students are asked to submit research-based written texts which strengthen their independent learning skills and facilitate a customised learning experience. The approximate time commitment expected from students is 12 hours per week, including class time.
have_subject.description: This subject is designed to provide students who are proficient in reading, listening, speaking and writing in Spanish with opportunities to engage with Hispanic culture. It corresponds to Level B2+ (independent/proficient user) of the Common European Framework of Reference (CEFR) for Languages. You’ll examine and engage with the sociocultural contexts in which the language is used, specifically with regard to human rights in Hispanic cultures. Via written and oral assessments that demonstrate continuing mastery of the Spanish language, your learning will involve a genre-based approach, engaging in interactive tasks and resources to enhance language proficiency. You'll be expected to prepare for classes through various exercises and analyse a variety of authentic sources in a manner that considers their cultural provenance and role. You’ll explore subject materials and concepts through independent research, creative outputs and/or close textual analysis. The language learning approach will focus on linguistic and cultural practices across the Hispanic world. You’ll be expected to commit to approximately 12 hours of weekly study, including class time, and immerse yourself in Spanish both inside and outside the classroom to build confidence and fluency.

have_subject.name: Innocence and Experience in Hispanophone Societies
have_subject.learning_and_teaching_activities: Flipped learning: Class time is used for interactive practice and advancement of skills through work in pairs, small and large groups and/or presentations, as well as clarification of subject content. Preparation for these activities requires students to complete exercises and reading/listening/viewing activities prior to coming to class and for them to make effective use of resources supplied to support their learning. As this is an 8 credit point subject, the approximate time commitment expected from students is 12 hours per week, including class time.

SMJ09036.name: Specialist Country Studies
SMJ09036.description: This sub-major offers the opportunity to obtain an introduction to the language and culture of one of Australia's major international economic partners and to learn about the country itself. It is possible to complete this sub-major in a minimum of two sessions. Students should plan to start their language and culture study in Autumn session, to be followed by the second language and culture subject and the contemporary society subject in Spring session.

contents.structure_details: Select 24 credit points from the following options:

have_sub_structures.structure_details: Complete the following subject:
Complete the following subject:

have_subject.name: Contemporary China
learning_outcomes: Reflect on and analyse contemporary China in its historical, political, cultural, social, and economic dimensions.
Employ approaches and frameworks to engage in historical, political, cultural, social and economic inquiry.
Apply knowledges of China ethically in their reflective and inquiry-based works.
Demonstrate critical problem-solving and research-led analytical skills in the context of China.
Communicate effectively in English the findings of independent inquiries and reflections in written form.
Maintain good academic practice, observing ethics and referencing standards.
have_subject.learning_and_teaching_activities: Contemporary China combines lectures, tutorials, collaborative in-class learning, self-study and individual research.Lectures provide students with an overview of key information, theoretical concepts, and historical context. Assigned readings and in-class activities encourage students to reflect on and engage with the class material, providing students with the training and skills required to complete the assessment tasks.Before each tutorial, students are expected to have completed all of the preparation activities assigned. Online lectures and guiding questions help students to focus on the most important aspects of these activities. Students must come to class prepared to discuss the material and raise questions.Tutorials consist of whole class and small group discussions that extend the preparatory learning. Tutorials focus on development of student understanding and provide formative feedback for assessment tasks. Tutorial activities focus on developing the analytical and literacy skills required for this subject and subsequent study.Students receive peer and academic feedback on an initial journal entry early in the session and will have the opportunity to workshop their topic and initial ideas for the literature review.
have_subject.description: This subject provides an overview of contemporary China and its history, culture, politics, society and economy. You will learn to identify and analyse current issues from multiple perspectives using theory and methods for intercultural research. The subject combines lectures with tutorials and independent work to develop your analytical and problem-solving abilities in China studies, with a focus on cultural diversity, social and economic change, and political transformation. You will learn to develop comparative and ethical approaches for critical thinking and verbal and written communication skills, reflecting the subject's multidisciplinary approach. Topics include cities, gender, home and work, identity, governance, media, the economy and China's international relationships.

have_sub_structures.structure_details: (CBK90487) Select 16 credit points from the following subjects:

have_subject.name: Chinese Business and Administrative Language
learning_outcomes: Select and deploy register, rhetoric, genre, concept and specialist vocabulary through exposure to business and administrative writings of a wide range of genres and purposes
Communicate professionally in writing and speech in Chinese through competent use of specific and advanced linguistic skills and devices
Develop awareness of when to deploy various Chinese and business cross-cultural communication practices
have_subject.learning_and_teaching_activities: The subject utilises flipped and hybrid teaching and learning strategies and both online and face-to-face delivery modes. Students use online and face-to-face class time for interactive practice and advancement of linguistic skills through personalised learning and group discussions, practices and/or presentations in flexible but guided and monitored physical or virtual classroom activities. Students also deepen their understanding of professional practices, theories and concepts and clarify subject content.Preparation for these activities requires students to complete exercises and reading/listening/viewing activities prior to coming to class and for them to make effective use of subject materials (including those posted online), community and peer resources available to them to support their learning.Students will customise their learning experience by engaging with Chinese business and professional language and practices from intercultural perspectives, and tailoring their practice and learning to maximise understanding and appreciation of Chinese business and administrative systems, customs and common practices.Students are encouraged to explore subject materials and concepts through independent research, creative outputs and/or close textual analysis.
have_subject.description: This subject equips you with specialised proficiency in Modern Standard Chinese, focusing on business and administrative communication. You'll master formal writing styles, expand your business vocabulary, and enhance speaking skills through practical assessments, preparing you for cross-cultural business environments. Learning combines online and in-person methods, including interactive practice and group work, to advance your linguistic abilities. You'll engage with Chinese business language and practices, customising your learning to understand and appreciate the nuances of Chinese professional settings. Independent research and creative analysis further deepen your knowledge, making you more culturally aware and employable internationally.

have_subject.name: Communicating About Chinese Media
learning_outcomes: Develop awareness of register, rhetoric, genre, concept and specialist vocabulary through exposure to a wide variety of media and academic readings
Communicate professionally and academically in writing and speech in Chinese through competent use of specific linguistic skills and devices
Reflect on and analyse critically the impact of social issues as seen from media sources on the Chinese society and its people.
have_subject.learning_and_teaching_activities: The subject utilises flipped and hybrid teaching and learning strategies and both online and face-to-face delivery modes. Students use online and face-to-face class time for interactive practice and advancement of linguistic skills through personalised learning and group discussions, practices and/or presentations in flexible but guided and monitored physical or virtual classroom activities. Students also deepen their understanding of professional practices, theories and concepts and clarify subject content.Preparation for these activities requires students to complete exercises and reading/listening/viewing activities prior to coming to class and for them to make effective use of subject materials (including those posted online), community and peer resources available to them to support their learning.Students will customise their learning experience by engaging with Chinese media and their language, culture and practices from intercultural perspectives, and tailoring their learning experience to maximise understanding and appreciation of Chinese history, culture and society.Students are encouraged to explore subject materials and concepts through independent research, creative outputs and/or close textual analysis.
have_subject.description: This subject equips you with advanced Chinese linguistic skills, preparing you for careers in Chinese-language media and critical reflection on media practices. You'll master crafting nuanced texts, employing specialised vocabulary and rhetoric, and analysing contemporary China through diverse media sources. Learning combines online and in-person methods, including interactive practice and group work, with a focus on personalised and collaborative learning. Prior to classes, you'll engage with preparatory exercises and materials to enhance in-class discussions and presentations. You'll also tailor your studies to your interests, exploring Chinese media, culture, and society through independent research and creative analysis, fostering a deep intercultural understanding.

learning_outcomes: Comprehend and respond to basic texts in written modern standard Chinese.
Produce basic texts in written modern standard Chinese.
Demonstrate cultural awareness of Chinese-speaking peoples and societies.
have_subject.learning_and_teaching_activities: A communicative approach is used in class to maximise students' potential for effective communication in the target language. A series of activities are used in this process. These include interactive activities where students work in pairs, small and large groups and/or presentations, and which are promoted in class to enhance students' engagement through content-based learning. Preparation for class activities requires students to complete exercises and reading/listening/viewing activities prior to class. Prior engagement with subject resources and materials facilitates student learning and a sense of community in the classroom.Students are encouraged to customise their learning experience as per the Global Chinese approach to promote their engagement with Chinese peoples and societies. This entails keeping in mind their goals for the use of Chinese (for instance studying abroad or travel in a particular country or region) and tailoring their practice and learning to maximize exposure to local variants of the language, relevant cultural norms and authentic materials from the area.As part of an immersive approach to language education, students are encouraged to use the target language within the classroom environment. This immersive approach will build students’ confidence when using the language outside the classroom. The approximate time commitment expected from students is 12 hours per week, including class time.
have_subject.description: This subject is designed to provide students who have a good command of spoken Chinese, but have little prior knowledge of the written Chinese language with basic reading and writing skills. It corresponds to Level 1 of the Chinese Proficiency Test (HSK). You'll acquire your vocabulary by around 500 words and learn Chinese radicals, which are an important element of the Chinese writing system. Through active participation in collaborative activities, you'll develop intercultural competence, learning to navigate between cultures.Your learning will involve a communicative approach, engaging in interactive tasks and activities to enhance language proficiency. You'll be expected to prepare for classes through various exercises and utilise online and peer resources. The language learning approach will focus on linguistic and cultural practices across the Chinese world.You’ll be expected to commit to approximately 12 hours of weekly study, including class time, and immerse yourself in Chinese both inside and outside the classroom to build confidence and fluency.

learning_outcomes: Develop awareness of register, rhetoric, genre, concept and specialist vocabulary through exposure to contemporary Chinese movies and a wide variety of academic and professional readings
Communicate professionally and academically in writing and speech in Chinese through competent use of specific linguistic skills and devices
Analyse cinematic representation of Chinese social, cultural and political themes;
Develop basic writing skills to prepare a movie synopsis, storyboard, screen play and film reviews
have_subject.learning_and_teaching_activities: The subject utilises flipped and hybrid teaching and learning strategies and both online and face-to-face delivery modes. Students use online and face-to-face class time for interactive practice and advancement of linguistic skills through personalised learning and group discussions, practices and/or presentations in flexible but guided and monitored physical or virtual classroom activities. have_subject.description: This subject equips you with advanced Modern Standard Chinese skills, focusing on specialised vocabulary and linguistic nuances relevant to film analysis. You'll gain proficiency in evaluating Chinese cinema within its socio-economic milieu, mastering technical language in both Chinese and English to critique films and script your mini screenplays. Learning is interactive, blending online and in-person sessions for practice and discussion, while independent study and resource utilisation enhance your grasp of professional practices and theoretical concepts. You'll tailor your experience by exploring the Chinese film industry's cultural context, fostering a deeper appreciation of China's heritage and societal norms through independent research and creative engagement.

have_sub_structures.structure_details: Complete the following subject:
Complete the following subject:

have_subject.name: Contemporary France
learning_outcomes: Reflect on and analyse contemporary France in its historical, political, cultural, social, and economic dimensions.
Employ approaches and frameworks to engage in historical, political, cultural, social and economic inquiry.
Apply knowledges of France ethically in their reflective and inquiry-based works.
Demonstrate critical problem-solving and research-led analytical skills in the context of France.
Communicate effectively in English the findings of independent inquiries and reflections in written form.
Maintain good academic practice, observing ethics and referencing standards.
have_subject.learning_and_teaching_activities: Contemporary France combines lectures, tutorials, collaborative in-class learning, self-study and individual research.Lectures provide students with an overview of important theoretical concepts and historical context. Assigned readings and in-class activities are designed for students to engage with and reflect on these concepts and contexts. Tutorials also provide the training and skills required to complete the assessment tasks: visual analysis, critical reflection, annotated bibliographies, literature reviews and scoping a research topic.Contemporary France requires student engagement in and outside of class. Multimedia resources will be provided to students in tutorials and online, and these resources can be used to complete assessment tasks where appropriate. Students are encouraged to apply critical thinking skills to their weekly reflections and assessment tasks. Contemporary France also allows students a good degree of flexibility to choose their own topic for their literature reviews in consultation with their tutor based on their individual interests and disciplinary background.
have_subject.description: This subject blends lectures and independent and collaborative learning to provide a foundational understanding of contemporary French society, politics and culture. You'll develop critical thinking and intercultural research skills. In weeks 2 and 12 you'll follow an umbrella programme shared by all European contemporary society subjects, exploring identity, divisions and crises in Europe. Between weeks 3 and 11 you'll explore France's historical evolution and the concept of French identity, with special focus on France's 'revolutionary' spirit, the importance of its Republican values, and its relations with former French colonies. These elements are crucial for understanding France's place in the modern world and the social and political issues shaping contemporary French society.

have_sub_structures.structure_details: (CBK90490) Select 16 credit points from the following subjects:

have_subject.name: Francophone Cultures of Consumption
learning_outcomes: Comprehend and respond to complex texts in oral and written French.
Produce texts in oral and written French that show conceptual depth and an advanced command of the French language.
Demonstrate intercultural awareness across two or more cultural contexts.
have_subject.learning_and_teaching_activities: This subject uses a communicative approach in class to maximise students' potential for effective communication in the target language. A series of activities such as interactive activities working in pairs, small and large groups and/or presentations, are promoted in class to enhance students' engagement through content-based learning. Preparation for class activities requires students to complete exercises and reading/listening/viewing activities prior to class to make effective use of resources and subject material (including resources online), community and peer resources available to them to support their learning.Students are encouraged to customise their learning experience as per the Global French approach to promote their engagement with francophone peoples and societies. This entails keeping in mind their goals for the use of French (for instance studying abroad or travel in a particular country or region) and tailoring their practice and learning to maximize exposure to local variants of the language, relevant cultural norms and authentic materials from the area.As part of an immersive approach to language education, students are encouraged to use the target language within the classroom environment and to build confidence to use the target language outside the classroom. The approximate time commitment expected from students is 12 hours per week, including class time.
have_subject.description: This subject is designed to provide students who are proficient in reading, listening, speaking and writing in French with opportunities to engage with francophone culture. It corresponds to Level B2/C1 (independent/proficient user) of the Common European Framework of Reference (CEFR) for Languages. You’ll examine and engage with the sociocultural contexts in which the language is used, specifically with regard to questions about consumption in the francophone world.Via written, oral and aural assessments that demonstrate continuing mastery of the French language, your learning will involve a communicative approach, engaging in interactive tasks and using a variety of resources to enhance language proficiency. You'll be expected to prepare for classes through various exercises and analyse a variety of authentic sources in a manner that considers their cultural provenance and role. You’ll explore subject materials and concepts through independent research, creative outputs and/or close textual analysis. The language learning approach will focus on linguistic and cultural practices across the francophone world.You’ll be expected to commit to approximately 12 hours of weekly study, including class time, and immerse yourself in French both inside and outside the classroom to build confidence and fluency.

97410 Places and Spaces of the Francophone World

learning_outcomes: Comprehend and respond to complex texts in oral and written French.
Produce complex texts in oral and written French.

have_subject.learning_and_teaching_activities: Class time is used for interactive practice and advancement of skills through work in pairs, small and large groups and/or presentations, as well as clarification of subject content. Students will spend a substantial portion of each lesson in dialogue with their peers and their teacher.Preparation for these activities requires students to complete exercises and reading/listening/viewing activities prior to coming to class and for them to make effective use of subject materials (including UTS Online), community and peer resources available to them to support their learning. As this is an 8 credit point subject, the approximate time commitment expected from students is 12 hours per week, including class time.Students will customise their learning experience as per the Global French approach to promote their engagement with francophone peoples and societies. This entails keeping in mind one's goals for the use of French (for instance studying abroad or travel in a particular country or region) and tailoring one's practice and learning to maximise exposure to local variants of the language, relevant cultural norms and authentic materials from the area.Students are strongly encouraged to prioritise the use of French when communicating with each other or with their teacher in class.Students are encouraged to explore subject materials and concepts through independent research, creative outputs and/or close textual analysis.
have_subject.description: This subject is designed to provide students who are proficient in reading, listening, speaking and writing in French with opportunities to engage with francophone culture. It corresponds to Level B2/C1 (independent/proficient user) of the Common European Framework of Reference (CEFR) for Languages. You’ll examine and engage with the sociocultural contexts in which the language is used, specifically with regard to questions of history and conflict in the francophone world.Via written, oral and aural assessments that demonstrate continuing mastery of the French language, your learning will involve a communicative approach, engaging in interactive tasks and using a variety of resources to enhance language proficiency. You'll be expected to prepare for classes through various exercises and analyse a variety of authentic sources in a manner that considers their cultural provenance and role. You’ll explore subject materials and concepts through independent research, creative outputs and/or close textual analysis. The language learning approach will focus on linguistic and cultural practices across the francophone world.You’ll be expected to commit to approximately 12 hours of weekly study, including class time, and immerse yourself in French both inside and outside the classroom to build confidence and fluency.

have_subject.name: Places and Spaces of the Francophone World
learning_outcomes: Comprehend and respond to complex texts in oral and written French.
Produce texts in oral and written French that show conceptual depth and an advanced command of the French language.
Demonstrate intercultural awareness across two or more cultural contexts.
have_subject.learning_and_teaching_activities: Class time is used for interactive practice and advancement of skills through work in pairs, small and large groups and/or presentations, as well as clarification of subject content. Students will spend a substantial portion of each lesson in dialogue with their peers and their teacher.Preparation for these activities requires students to complete exercises and reading/listening/viewing activities prior to coming to class and for them to make effective use of subject materials (including UTS Online), community and peer resources available to them to support their learning. As this is an 8 credit point subject, the approximate time commitment expected from students is 12 hours per week, including class time.Students will customise their learning experience as per the Global French approach to promote their engagement with francophone peoples and societies. This entails keeping in mind one's goals for the use of French (for instance studying abroad or travel in a particular country or region) and tailoring one's practice and learning to maximise exposure to local variants of the language, relevant cultural norms and authentic materials from the area.As part of an immersive approach to language education, students are expected to use only French when communicating with each other or with their teacher in class.Students are encouraged to explore subject materials and concepts through independent research, creative outputs and/or close textual analysis.
have_subject.description: This subject is designed to provide students who are proficient in reading, listening, speaking and writing in French with opportunities to engage with francophone culture. It corresponds to Level B2/C1 (independent/proficient user) of the Common European Framework of Reference (CEFR) for Languages. You’ll examine and engage with the sociocultural contexts in which the language is used, specifically with regard to different physical and non-physical places and spaces in the francophone world.Via written, oral and aural assessments that demonstrate continuing mastery of the French language, your learning will involve a communicative approach, engaging in interactive tasks and using a variety of resources to enhance language proficiency. You'll be expected to prepare for classes through various exercises and analyse a variety of authentic sources in a manner that considers their cultural provenance and role. You’ll explore subject materials and concepts through independent research, creative outputs and/or close textual analysis. The language learning approach will focus on linguistic and cultural practices across the francophone world.You’ll be expected to commit to approximately 12 hours of weekly study, including class time, and immerse yourself in French both inside and outside the classroom to build confidence and fluency.

have_sub_structures.structure_details: Complete the following subject:
Complete the following subject:

have_subject.name: Contemporary Germany
learning_outcomes: Reflect on and analyse contemporary Germany in its historical, political, cultural, social, and economic dimensions.
Employ approaches and frameworks to engage in historical, political, cultural, social and economic inquiry.
Apply knowledges of Germany ethically in their reflective and inquiry-based works.
Demonstrate critical problem-solving and research-led analytical skills in the context of Germany.
Communicate effectively in English the findings of independent inquiries and reflections in written form.
Maintain good academic practice, observing ethics and referencing standards.
have_subject.learning_and_teaching_activities: Contemporary Germany combines lectures, tutorials, collaborative in-class learning, reflection and individual research.Lectures provide students with an overview of key theoretical concepts and historical context. Assigned readings and in-class activities are designed for students to engage with and reflect on these concepts and contexts. Tutorials also provide the training and skills required to complete the assessment tasks: visual analysis, critical reflection, annotated bibliographies, essays and scoping a research topic.Contemporary Germany requires student engagement in and outside of class. Multimedia resources are provided to students in tutorials and online, and these resources can be used to complete assessment tasks where appropriate. Students are encouraged to apply critical thinking skills to their weekly reflections and assessment tasks. Contemporary Germany also allows students a good degree of flexibility to choose their own topic for their essays in consultation with their tutor based on their individual interests and disciplinary background.Formative feedback on students' reflective writing will be provided in the week 3 tutorial. Extensive feedback will be provided on students’ Essay Proposal, giving students the opportunity to refine their Essay topic based on tutor feedback.
have_subject.description: This subject blends lectures, independent and collaborative learning to provide a foundational understanding of contemporary German history, politics, society, and culture. You'll develop critical thinking and intercultural research skills, essential for advanced In-country Studies. In weeks 2 and 12 you'll follow an umbrella program shared by all contemporary European society subjects, exploring its identity, divisions, and crises. Between weeks 3 and 11, you'll focus on Germany's post-World War II era, examining its divided past, reunification, and current challenges, including identity, social movements, economic growth, immigration, and international relations, alongside language issues and popular culture. These elements are crucial for understanding Germany's place in the modern world. German popular culture is dealt with throughout.

have_sub_structures.structure_details: (CBK90492) Select 16 credit points from the following subjects:

have_subject.name: German Film
learning_outcomes: Comprehend and respond to complex texts in oral and written German.
Produce complex texts in oral and written German.
Demonstrate intercultural awareness across two or more cultural contexts.
have_subject.learning_and_teaching_activities: This subject uses a communicative approach in class to maximise students' potential for effective communication in the target language. A series of activities such as interactive activities working in pairs, small and large groups and/or presentations, are promoted in class to enhance students' engagement through content-based learning. Preparation for class activities requires students to complete exercises and reading/listening/viewing activities prior to class to make effective use of resources and subject material (including resources online), community and peer resources available to them to support their learning.Students are encouraged to customise their learning experience as per the Global German approach to promote their engagement with German-speaking peoples and societies. This entails keeping in mind their goals for the use of German (for instance studying abroad or travel in a particular country or region) and tailoring their practice and learning to maximize exposure to local variants of the language, relevant cultural norms and authentic materials from the area.As part of an immersive approach to language education, students are encouraged to use the target language within the classroom environment and to build confidence to use the target language outside the classroom. The approximate time commitment expected from students is 12 hours per week, including class time.
have_subject.description: This subject equips you with the skills to analyse and discuss German film, enhancing your written and oral German proficiency. You'll explore the evolution of German cinema, from its silent beginnings to modern transnational trends, and learn to articulate its cultural significance. Through active participation, you'll refine your German language abilities, aiming for spontaneous, effective communication on diverse topics. This subject is designed to provide students who have an intermediate to proficient knowledge of the German language with more advanced reading, listening, speaking and writing skills. It corresponds to Level B1 / B2 / C1 (independent -to proficient user) of the Common European Framework of Reference (CEFR) for Languages. Through active participation in collaborative activities, you'll develop intercultural competence, learning to navigate between cultures. Your learning will involve a communicative approach, engaging in interactive tasks and activities to enhance language proficiency. You'll be expected to prepare for classes through various exercises and utilise online and peer resources. The language learning approach will focus on linguistic and cultural practices across the germanophone world. You’ll be expected to commit to approximately 12 hours of weekly study, including class time, and immerse yourself in German both inside and outside the classroom to build confidence and fluency.

have_subject.name: German Today
learning_outcomes: Comprehend and respond to complex texts in oral and written German.
Produce complex texts in oral and written German.
Demonstrate intercultural awareness across two or more cultural contexts.
have_subject.learning_and_teaching_activities: This subject uses a communicative approach in class to maximise students' potential for effective communication in the target language. A series of activities such as interactive activities working in pairs, small and large groups and/or presentations, are promoted in class to enhance students' engagement through content-based learning. Preparation for class activities requires students to complete exercises and reading/listening/viewing activities prior to class to make effective use of resources and subject material (including resources online), community and peer resources available to them to support their learning.Students are encouraged to customise their learning experience as per the Global German approach to promote their engagement with German-speaking peoples and societies. This entails keeping in mind their goals for the use of German (for instance studying abroad or travel in a particular country or region) and tailoring their practice and learning to maximize exposure to local variants of the language, relevant cultural norms and authentic materials from the area.As part of an immersive approach to language education, students are encouraged to use the target language within the classroom environment and to build confidence to use the target language outside the classroom. have_subject.description: This subject equips you with an understanding of modern German-speaking cultures and societies, enhancing your ability to discuss historical and cultural topics within global contexts. It is designed to provide students who have an intermediate to proficient knowledge of the German language with more advanced reading, listening, speaking and writing skills. It corresponds to Levels B1 / B2 / C1 (independent user) of the Common European Framework of Reference (CEFR) for Languages. Through active participation in collaborative activities, you'll develop intercultural competence, learning to navigate between cultures. The language learning approach will focus on linguistic and cultural practices across the germanophone world. You’ll be expected to commit to approximately 12 hours of weekly study, including class time, and immerse yourself in German both inside and outside the classroom to build confidence and fluency.

have_sub_structures.structure_details: Complete the following subject:
Complete the following subject:

have_subject.name: Contemporary Italy
learning_outcomes: Reflect on and analyse contemporary Italy in its historical, political, cultural, social, and economic dimensions.
Employ approaches and frameworks to engage in historical, political, cultural, social and economic inquiry.
Apply knowledges of Italy ethically in their reflective and inquiry-based works.
Demonstrate critical problem-solving and research-led analytical skills in the context of Italy.
Communicate effectively in English the findings of independent inquiries and reflections in written form.
Maintain good academic practice, observing ethics and referencing standards.
have_subject.learning_and_teaching_activities: Contemporary Italy combines lectures, tutorials, collaborative learning, reflection and individual research. Lectures provide students with an overview of key theoretical concepts and historical context. Assigned readings and in-class activities are designed for students to engage with and reflect on these concepts and contexts. Tutorials also provide the training and skills required to complete the assessment tasks: visual analysis, critical reflection, essays and scoping a research topic. Contemporary Italy requires student engagement in and outside of sessions. Multimedia resources are provided to students in tutorials and online, and these resources can be used to complete assessment tasks where appropriate. Students are encouraged to apply critical thinking skills to their weekly reflections and assessment tasks. Contemporary Italy also allows students a good degree of flexibility to choose their own topic for their essays in consultation with their tutor based on their individual interests and disciplinary background. Formative feedback on students' reflective writing will be provided in the Week 3 tutorial.
have_subject.description: This subject blends lectures, independent and collaborative learning to provide a foundational understanding of contemporary Italy's history and culture. Through this subject, you'll develop critical thinking and intercultural research skills. In weeks 2 and 12 you'll follow an umbrella program shared by all contemporary European society subjects, exploring its identity, divisions, and crises. Between weeks 3 and 11, you'll focus on Italy, its evolution from Unification and the Fascist era to post-war economic growth and recent sociopolitical changes, with a special emphasis on the roles of migration and multilingualism in shaping Italian culture and identity.

have_subject.name: Italian Language and the Arts
learning_outcomes: Comprehend, reflect upon and analyse complex texts in spoken and written Italian.
Produce complex texts in spoken and written Italian.
Demonstrate intercultural awareness across two or more cultural contexts.
have_subject.learning_and_teaching_activities: Italian Language and the Arts proposes an innovative blended, practice-based and research-inspired learning experience. Art works and cultural artifacts are analysed through a communicative and reflective approach, able to stimulate progressive and continuous learning, as well as reflection on the use of language in context. The analysis is supported by series of supplementary authentic intermedial materials (podcasts, images, videos) and scaffolded through the use of real-life scenarios and situations (debates, role-plays, interviews). Italian Language and the Arts is structured so to favour the interaction within a virtual learning environment as well as the construction of a nuanced and personalised learning experience. In addition to the sessions, students are asked to complete a series of on-line activities, which strengthen their independent learning skills and facilitate a customised learning experience. The approximate time commitment expected from students is 12 hours per week, including sessions.
have_subject.description: This subject equips you with a deep understanding of visual and artistic cultures in Italy, enhancing your Italian language skills. It corresponds to Level B1+ of the Common European Framework of Reference for Languages (CEFR). You'll learn to analyse and discuss artworks, connecting them to their socio-cultural contexts. Materials are explored through a communicative and reflective approach, aimed at stimulating progressive and continuous learning, as well as reflecting on the use of language in context. Your learning journey involves active participation in collaborative exercises. Expect to dedicate around 12 hours weekly, including independent online activities, to develop a tailored and comprehensive learning experience.

have_sub_structures.structure_details: Complete the following subject:
Complete the following subject:

have_subject.name: Contemporary Japan
learning_outcomes: Reflect on and analyse contemporary Japan in its historical, political, cultural, social, and economic dimensions.
Employ approaches and frameworks to engage in historical, political, cultural, social and economic inquiry.
Apply knowledges of Japan ethically in their reflective and inquiry-based works.
Demonstrate critical problem-solving and research-led analytical skills in the context of Japan.
Communicate effectively in English the findings of independent inquiries and reflections in written form.
Maintain good academic practice, observing ethics and referencing standards.
have_subject.learning_and_teaching_activities: Contemporary Japan combines lectures, tutorials, collaborative in-class learning, self-study and individual research.Lectures provide students with an overview of key theoretical concepts and historical context. Assigned readings and in-class activities encourage students to engage with and reflect on these concepts and contexts, providing students with the training and skills required to complete the assessment tasks.Before each lecture and tutorial, students are expected to have completed all of the preparation activities assigned. Guiding questions help students to focus on the most important aspects of these activities. Tutorials and lectures include preliminary work and formative feedback on assessment tasks.Tutorials consist of whole class and small group discussions that extend the preparatory learning. Students must come to class prepared to discuss the material and raise questions. Tutorial activities focus on developing the analytical and information literacy skills required both for this subject and subsequent study.Students receive peer and academic feedback on an initial journal entry early in the session and will have the opportunity to workshop their topic and initial ideas for the literature review.
have_subject.description: This subject provides an overview of contemporary Japan, examining its history, politics, culture, society, and economy. You'll acquire critical skills to pinpoint and analyse current issues, using theoretical and methodological frameworks for intercultural research. The subject combines lectures with independent work to enhance your problem-solving and analytical abilities in Japan studies, with a focus on cultural diversity, social change, and ethical practices. You'll develop critical thinking and communication skills, reflecting the subject's multidisciplinary nature. Early topics cover foundational concepts of Japan Studies, while subsequent weeks delve into Japanese identity, politics, work life, gender and Japan's international relationships.

have_subject.name: Japanese Films and Popular Culture
learning_outcomes: Comprehend and respond to intermediate–advanced level texts in oral and written language.
Produce intermediate-advanced level texts in oral and written language.
Demonstrate intercultural awareness across two or more cultural contexts.
have_subject.learning_and_teaching_activities: This subject proposes a blended, practice-based and research-inspired learning experience. Japanese pop culture and films are analysed through a communicative and reflective approach to stimulate progressive and continuous learning of the target language. A series of authetic materials (books, articles and videos) are used to promote the use of real-life scenarios and situations such as interviews and discussion.Students are encouraged to customise their learning experience as per the Global Japanese approach to promote their engagement with Japanese peoples and societies. This entails keeping in mind their goals for the use of Japanese (for instance studying abroad or travel in a particular country or region) and tailoring their practice and learning to maximize exposure to local variants of the language, relevant cultural norms and authentic materials from the area.In addition to the in-class work, students are expected to complete a series of online activities, which strengthen their independent learning skills and facilitate a customised learning experience. The approximate time commitment expected from students is 12 hours per week, including class time.
have_subject.description: This subject is designed to provide students who are proficient in reading, listening, speaking and writing in Japanese with opportunities to engage with Japanese-speaking culture. It corresponds to N2/N1 of the Japanese Language Proficiency Test (JLPT) or Level B2/C1 (independent/proficient user) of the Common European Framework of Reference (CEFR) for Languages. You’ll examine the sociocultural contexts in which the Japanese language is used, focusing on the influence of cinema and other forms of popular culture on society. Via written and oral assessments that demonstrate continuing mastery of the Japanese language, your learning will involve a communicative approach, engaging in interactive tasks and resources to enhance language proficiency. You'll be expected to prepare for classes through various exercises and analyse a variety of authentic sources in a manner that considers their cultural provenance and role. You’ll explore subject materials and concepts through independent research, creative outputs and/or close textual analysis. The language learning approach will focus on linguistic and cultural practices across the Japanese-speaking world. You’ll be expected to commit to approximately 12 hours of weekly study, including class time, and immerse yourself in Japanese both inside and outside the classroom to build confidence and fluency.

have_sub_structures.structure_details: Complete the following subject:
Complete the following subject:

have_subject.name: Contemporary Spain
learning_outcomes: Reflect on and analyse contemporary Spain in its historical, political, cultural, social, and economic dimensions.
Employ approaches and frameworks to engage in historical, political, cultural, social and economic inquiry.
Apply knowledges of Spain ethically in their reflective and inquiry-based works.
Demonstrate critical problem-solving and research-led analytical skills in the context of Spain.
Communicate effectively in English the findings of independent inquiries and reflections in written form.
Maintain good academic practice, observing ethics and referencing standards.
have_subject.learning_and_teaching_activities: Contemporary Spain combines lectures, tutorials, collaborative in-class learning, reflection and individual research.Lectures provide students with an overview of key theoretical concepts and historical context. Assigned readings and in-class activities are designed for students to engage with and reflect on these concepts and contexts. Tutorials also provide the training and skills required to complete the assessment tasks: visual analysis, critical reflection, annotated bibliographies, literature reviews and scoping a research topic.Contemporary Spain requires student engagement in and outside of class. Multimedia resources are provided to students in tutorials and online, and these resources can be used to complete assessment tasks where appropriate. Students are encouraged to apply critical thinking skills to their weekly reflections and assessment tasks. Contemporary Spain also allows students a good degree of flexibility to choose their own topic for their literature reviews in consultation with their tutor based on their individual interests and disciplinary background.Formative feedback on students' reflective writing will be provided in the week 3 tutorial. Extensive feedback will be provided on students’ Literature Review Proposal, giving students the opportunity to refine their Literature Review topic based on tutor feedback.
have_subject.description: This subject introduces you to contemporary Spain through its historical, political, cultural, social, and economic dimensions from a local, national, continental, and global context. You will be provided with the critical skills to identify, reflect, and analyse major current socio-cultural challenges that shape contemporary Spain through the learning of theoretical and methodological approaches for engaging you in intercultural research. In weeks 2 and 12, you will follow an umbrella program shared by all contemporary European society subjects that introduces general issues relating to contemporary Europe: definitions, geographical space, addressing its identity, unification, internal divisions, colonialism, and the impact of migration, among others. In the other weeks, the subject focuses on divergent and historical ways of conceptualising Spain. You will learn about important moments in Spain’s history, such as the unification, the Second Republic, the Civil War, the Franco dictatorship, the restoration of democracy, and the sociopolitical developments of recent decades. You will delve into the diversity of national and cultural identities (regionalisms), plurilingualism, gender, film, music, art, migration, economics, and the evolving socio-cultural aspects of contemporary Spain. This subject will also provide you with an understanding of some of the current challenges that the country faces and how a Spanish citizen thinks and feels in the twenty-first century. The collaborative and reflective learning activities will allow you to develop your intercultural capabilities via critical thinking on Spain's diverse and dynamic culture(s), as well as an international perspective. In the assessments, you will apply your critical and reflection skills to identify and analyse contemporary Spain, through theoretical and methodological approaches.

A series of activities such as interactive activities working in pairs, small and large groups and/or presentations, are promoted in class to enhance students' engagement through content-based learning. Preparation for class activities requires students to complete exercises and reading/listening/viewing activities prior to class to make effective use of resources and subject material (including resources online), community and peer resources available to them to support their learning.Students are encouraged to customise their learning experience as per the Global Spanish approach to promote their engagement with Hispanic-phone peoples and societies. Your learning will involve a genre-based approach, engaging in interactive tasks and activities to enhance language proficiency. You'll be expected to prepare for classes through various exercises and utilise online and peer resources. The language learning approach will focus on linguistic and cultural practices across the Hispanic world.You’ll be expected to commit to approximately 12 hours of weekly study, including class time, and immerse yourself in Spanish both inside and outside the classroom to build confidence and fluency.

have_subject.name: Diaspora of the Hispanic Imagination
have_subject.learning_and_teaching_activities: Flipped learning: Class time is used for interactive practice and advancement of skills through work in pairs, small and large groups and/or presentations, as well as clarification of subject content. Preparation for these activities requires students to complete exercises and reading/listening/viewing activities prior to coming to class and for them to make effective use of resources supplied to support their learning. As this is an 8 credit point subject, the approximate time commitment expected from students is 12 hours per week, including class time.
have_subject.description: This subject is designed to provide students who are proficient in reading, listening, speaking and writing in Spanish with opportunities to engage with Hispanic culture. It corresponds to Level C1 (independent/proficient user) of the Common European Framework of Reference (CEFR) for Languages. You’ll examine and engage with the sociocultural contexts in which the language is used, specifically with regard diaspora of the Hispanic imagination. Via written and oral assessments that demonstrate continuing mastery of the Spanish language, your learning will involve a genre-based approach, engaging in interactive tasks and resources to enhance language proficiency. You'll be expected to prepare for classes through various exercises and analyse a variety of authentic sources in a manner that considers their cultural provenance and role. You’ll explore subject materials and concepts through independent research, creative outputs and/or close textual analysis. The language learning approach will focus on linguistic and cultural practices across the Hispanic world. You’ll be expected to commit to approximately 12 hours of weekly study, including class time, and immerse yourself in Spanish both inside and outside the classroom to build confidence and fluency.

have_subject.name: Human Rights in Hispanophone Societies
learning_outcomes: Comprehend, reflect upon and analyse complex texts in spoken (oral) and written (language).
Produce complex texts in spoken (oral) and written (language).
Demonstrate critical cultural awareness of Spanish speaking people and societies.
have_subject.learning_and_teaching_activities: Human Rights in Hispanophone Societies blends practice-based and research-inspired learning experience. Oral and written popular texts are analysed through a communicative and reflective approach, able to stimulate progressive and continuous learning, as well as reflection on the use of language in context. The analysis is supported by a series of supplementary authentic intermedial materials (songs, films and documentaries) and scaffolded through the use of real-life scenarios and situations, such as debates. The subject is structured so as to favour the interaction of in-class and independent learning environments as well as the construction of a nuanced and personalised learning experience.In addition the in-class work, students are asked to submit research-based written texts which strengthen their independent learning skills and facilitate a customised learning experience. The approximate time commitment expected from students is 12 hours per week, including class time.

42172.name: Introduction to Artificial Intelligence
learning_outcomes: Describe key ideas and applications of common AI techniques to people in the same discipline. (D.1)
Apply common AI algorithms to solve simple real-world problems. (D.1)
Design and justify AI solutions for medium to large problems. (C.1)
Collaborate effectively on a project team. (E.1)
42172.learning_and_teaching_activities: This subject will be delivered through a series of lectures and laboratory/ tutorials. In each week, students will attend a lecture class (1.5 hours) and a lab or a tutorial class (1.5 hours). Lecture classes will unpack the weekly topics. Students are expected to have adequate off class preparation so that they can make best use of lecture classes to build sound AI knowledge base; The lab classes include demonstration of the weekly AI techniques and exercise of using the AI techniques. The lab classes provide opportunities for students to make sense of the conceptual knowledge and gain hands-on experience of applying these AI techniques. The tutorial classes include explanation of concepts with examples and exercises for students to gain better understanding of the techniques for knowledge presentation, and knowledge-based reasoning and inference.
42172.description: This subject provides a grounding in Artificial Intelligence (AI), covering core areas such as learning, natural language processing, computer vision, search, knowledge representation, and reasoning. Through lectures/workshops and labs, you'll gain conceptual understanding of basic AI techniques and practical experience of using AI techniques and tools. You will be assessed in a knowledge test, a set of simple AI applications, and a group project designing an AI solution for a real-world problem, developing sound judgement and competence in this cutting-edge field.

requisite.rule: Requisite rule: ((1 OR 1a OR 1b OR 1c OR 1d OR 1e))

32555.name: Fundamentals of Software Development
learning_outcomes: Analyse and document system requirements based on user needs and context. (B.1)
Use a range of modelling and programming techniques and tools to propose software system for users. (C.1)
Design a software system to meet the specified user requirements. (C.1)
Create software programs to address the specified user requirements. (D.1)
Apply appropriate testing techniques to ensure the quality of the software system. (D.1)
32555.learning_and_teaching_activities: Students will learn foundation level software development practices through online lectures and hands-on workshops (tutorials and labs). Students will prepare for the workshops by listening to online lectures and studying related material to effectively participate in the workshop activities. In workshops, the software modelling and development methods, tools, and techniques will be demonstrated by the tutors before being applied by students to different scenarios. Feedback is regularly provided throughout the teaching sessions in several different formats: discussions of the workshop questions, feedback on the software models and programs developed in the laboratory and as a part of the formal assessment tasks. As the subject progresses, students gain sufficient foundational level competence to complete software development tasks within a small range of acceptable solutions.
32555.description: This subject provides a foundation in modern software development, covering the theoretical and practical aspects of software modelling and development methods, tools, and techniques. You will learn how to analyse, design, develop, and evaluate software programs that implement realistic but manageably small-scale software requirements. Teaching is delivered through pre-recorded lectures and demos, hands-on workshops, and mini-lectures in these workshops. You are encouraged to practice peer learning and collaboration within and outside the classroom; however, you must complete the individual tasks and your part of the group tasks independently. By completing this subject, you will understand the importance of foundational software development practices and be able to apply programming skills to build software that meets the defined criteria.

32130.name: Fundamentals of Data Analytics
learning_outcomes: Identify skills and attributes required for the understanding and application of data analytics in Industry. (D.1)
Describe the methods involved in data analytics, their scope and limitations. (D.1)
Design a data analytics project in a business environment. (C.1)
Apply data analytics methods for descriptive and predictive analytics tasks in a business environment. (D.1)
32130.learning_and_teaching_activities: Subject presentation includes combined lecture and workshop sessions and practical data analytics tasks for the assignments. Students will need to undertake preparation using material on Canvas to make effective use of their workshop time. Lectures will present the theoretical aspects of data analytics, including guest lectures about case studies of real-world business applications of data mining techniques. The workshop sessions focus on hands-on experience in data analytics and data analytics tools, and understanding and interpretation of the results. Practical assignments can be performed anywhere.Prepreparation will help students to participate in the in-class individual and group exercises. Regular zero mark quizzes throughout the semester will allow students to gauge their progress
32130.description: This subject equips you with both theoretical and hands-on experience of data analytics, the process of extracting meaningful insights from large data sets. You'll learn statistical methods and data visualisation techniques to explore and process vast volumes of data, and unsupervised as well as supervised techniques to discover patterns and insights for knowledge discovery. The subject synthesises cutting-edge research and practice, preparing you to lead data analytics projects that transform data into commercially valuable information.

32513.name: Advanced Data Analytics Algorithms
learning_outcomes: Describe the scope, limitations and application of several advanced machine learning methods. (D.1)
Use or program a machine learning method. (C.1)
Design an approach to machine learning problems in specialised domains. (C.1)
Demonstrate an understanding of the issues underlying machine learning to successfully outline an approach to solving a machine learning problem. (D.1)
32513.learning_and_teaching_activities: The subject is delivered by online learning materials (organised videos of short lectures and algorithm implementation tutorials) and interactive workshops, as well as industry-based guest lectures. The subject features in-depth study of the theory and algorithm of data analytics, as well as detailed hands-on implementation tutorials of classical algorithms. Guest lectures also highlight UTS-specific and industry-based research that give students the opportunity to engage deeply with experts and ask questions that address advanced methods in data analytics. Students will engage with pre-reading material that will be used as basis for discussion and activities in class. Each week an in-class test is made available for students to check their knowledge and gauge their strengths and areas needing further practice. In-class tests with immediate feedback will help students to reflect on their learning. In this subject, students have the opportunity to prepare a firm foundation for further study in data science and artificial intelligence by engaging deeply with the project.
32513.description: This subject advances your understanding of data analytics through a machine learning lens, exploring how intelligent agents learn and adapt. Applied across various sectors, machine learning is central to discovering new knowledge. You'll delve into knowledge discovery algorithms, building on prior data analytics knowledge to comprehend both elementary and complex algorithms. The subject blends hands-on practice with foundational theories, enabling you to master key techniques through implementation and analysis. Additionally, you'll explore machine learning's practical applications, particularly in artificial intelligence, equipping you with the skills to apply this knowledge in real-world scenarios.

requisite.rule: Requisite rule: (((1 OR 1a)) OR ((2 OR 2a) AND (3 OR 3a)) OR (4 AND (5 OR 5a)) OR (6))

32146.name: Data Visualisation and Visual Analytics
learning_outcomes: Recognise, compare and apply a range of static data visualisation techniques. (D.1)
Apply interactive data visualisation techniques. (D.1)
Design and create data visualisations for particular social contexts that are appropriate for specific users. (C.1)
Design and create data visualisations that facilitate the discovery and presentation of data-driven stories. (C.1)
32146.learning_and_teaching_activities: This subject is comprised of an introductory lecture (week 1) and workshops (weeks 2 to 12). Each week of workshops is self-paced through the learning management system. You are expected to complete a range of different learning activities throughout the week. Activities provide opportunities to learn, apply and discuss the knowledge gained in a practical manner. Feedback is provided from both peers and teaching staff throughout the activities. You are encouraged to actively provide feedback and interact with staff and students. The workshops will allow you to interact with staff and students, ask questions and receive clarification and feedback.
32146.description: This subject focuses on the vital technologies of data visualisation and visual interaction that underpin visual analytics and decision-making. You'll engage with recent data visualisation literature and practice using advanced software. The subject outlines the visual data analytics process and methodology, including the critical role of human interaction with interactive visual interfaces. Upon completion, you'll be adept at designing and evaluating sophisticated visualisation interfaces, positioning you for roles as a data visualisation designer and visual data analyst.

requisite.rule: Requisite rule: ((1 AND (2 OR 2a OR 2b)) OR (3) OR ((4 OR 4a)))

42049.name: Architecting on Amazon Web Services
learning_outcomes: Identity and express where and how AWS services can be used to solve a business problem. (B.1)
Comparative analysis and justification of the selected AWS services for solving business problem. (C.1, D.1)
42049.learning_and_teaching_activities: Students will attend lectures and labs every week. The exact duration of the lectures and labs (combined) varies from one week to another. However, it would be between 4 to 6 hours per week. Students will be provided materials prior to the lecture that they are expected to read and come to the lecture. Some aspects of the lectures will be flipped wherein students will be expected to work in groups for solving problem.Students apply the knowledge delivered in lectures by designing solutions to two AWS-provided case studies using the AWS stack. Students work in groups to solve these case studies. Apart from the technical content they also learn about group work and collaboration. Furthermore, the labs each week would contribute to their learning process as well.
42049.description: As enterprises globally transition to the Cloud, AWS expertise is increasingly sought-after. The Faculty of Engineering and Information Technology is an AWS Academy member, offering a subject on engineering and deploying enterprise-grade applications on AWS. Through this subject, you'll learn to build and deploy applications in the Cloud, equipping you with the skills to become an AWS Certified Solutions Architect (Associate) upon completion. The curriculum aligns with the authorised AWS Academy program.

requisite.rule: Requisite rule: ((1 AND (2 OR 2a OR 2b OR 2c OR 2d OR 2e)) OR (3))

32567.name: Business Intelligence for Decision Support
learning_outcomes: Apply decision support models, methods and systems in related business intelligence systems. (C.1)
Analyse decision models, decision methods, and decision support systems within a business intelligence systems. (D.1)
Communicate technical knowledge through reports and presentations. (E.1)
Collaborate as a team to meet business intelligence project goals. (E.1)
32567.learning_and_teaching_activities: Three-hour sessions per week of integrated presentations.The subject will consist of approximately 18 hours of lectures and 21 hours of seminar/workshops supplemented by guided online/offline discussion. These sessions may be offered on a weekly basis throughout the session or in block mode.
32567.description: This subject delves into business intelligence (BI), a comprehensive term encompassing architectures, tools, databases, analytics, applications, and methodologies aimed at facilitating interactive data access and empowering managers to make informed decisions. It introduces decision support systems (DSS), which are computer-based systems combining models, methods, and data to tackle semi-structured or unstructured decisions, demanding user involvement and a user-friendly interface. As high-level business decisions often fall into these categories and are data-intensive, DSS are increasingly integrated with BI techniques for enhanced problem-solving. You'll learn about various aspects of BI and DSS, including group and intelligent support systems, web-based systems, decision optimisation, and personalised recommender systems. Additionally, the subject teaches the design, implementation, and integration of BI techniques to bolster business decision-making processes.

requisite.rule: Requisite rule: ((1 OR 1a OR 1b OR 1c OR 1d))
